computational feasibility, providing foundations for specialized AI assistants
in threat analysis, vulnerability assessment, and security documentation while
challenging prevailing assumptions about data requirements for LLM
specialization.

AI.tldr: The paper explores domain-adaptive continuous pretraining to enhance cybersecurity knowledge in large language models, achieving state-of-the-art results on cybersecurity benchmarks with less data.
AI.motivation: General-purpose LLMs lack specialized cybersecurity knowledge essential for effective domain-specific analysis.
AI.method: Systematic domain adaptation of three decoder-based LLMs using a curated 126M-word cybersecurity corpus, constrained training parameters, and distributed FSDP training to balance specialization and knowledge preservation.
AI.result: Improved accuracy on three cybersecurity benchmarks with the adapted models outperforming existing specialized models, achieving high performance with significantly less training data.
AI.conclusion: Targeted continuous pretraining is an efficient, computationally feasible way to specialize LLMs for cybersecurity tasks, enabling advanced AI assistants and challenging assumptions about data size requirements for domain adaptation.

title: Testing, Evaluation, Verification and Validation (TEVV) of Digital Twins: A Comprehensive Framework
summary:
Digital twins have emerged as a powerful technology for modeling and
simulating complex systems across various domains (Fuller et al., 2020; Tao et
al., 2019). As virtual representations of physical assets, processes, or
systems, digital twins enable real-time monitoring, predictive analysis, and
optimization. However, as digital twins become more sophisticated and integral
to decision-making processes, ensuring their accuracy, reliability, and ethical
implementation is essential. This paper presents a comprehensive framework for
the Testing, Evaluation, Verification and Validation (TEVV) of digital twins to
address the unique challenges posed by these dynamic and complex virtual
models.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a framework for testing and validating digital twins to ensure their accuracy and reliability as these models become more complex and critical in decision making.
AI.motivation: Digital twins are increasingly used in real-time monitoring and optimization but face challenges related to accuracy, reliability, and ethics as they grow sophisticated.
AI.method: The paper proposes a comprehensive TEVV (Testing, Evaluation, Verification and Validation) framework specifically designed for digital twins.
AI.result: The framework addresses the unique challenges of dynamic and complex digital twin models, aiming to improve their trustworthiness.
AI.conclusion: Implementing the TEVV framework is essential to ensure the dependable and ethical use of digital twins in various domains.

title: PB-LLMs: Privacy- and Bias-aware NLP Models using Named-Entity Recognition
comment:
Presented at AAAI Workshop on Privacy-Preserving Artificial
  Intelligence (PPAI) 2025, Philadelphia, PA, USA, March 2025
summary:
The use of Natural Language Processing (NLP) in high-stakes AI-based
applications has increased significantly in recent years, especially since the
emergence of Large Language Models (LLMs). However, despite their strong
performance, LLMs introduce important legal/ethical concerns, particularly
regarding privacy, data protection, and transparency. Due to these concerns,
this work explores the use of Named-Entity Recognition (NER) to facilitate the
privacy-preserving training (or adaptation) of LLMs. We propose a framework
that uses NER technologies to anonymize sensitive information in text data,
such as personal identities or geographic locations. An evaluation of the
proposed privacy-preserving learning framework was conducted to measure its
impact on user privacy and system performance in a particular high-stakes and
sensitive setup: AI-based resume scoring for recruitment processes. The study
involved two language models (BERT and RoBERTa) and six anonymization
algorithms (based on Presidio, FLAIR, BERT, and different versions of GPT)
applied to a database of 24,000 candidate profiles. The findings indicate that
the proposed privacy preservation techniques effectively maintain system
performance while playing a critical role in safeguarding candidate
confidentiality, thus promoting trust in the experimented scenario. On top of
the proposed privacy-preserving approach, we also experiment applying an
existing approach that reduces the gender bias in LLMs, thus finally obtaining
our proposed Privacy- and Bias-aware LLMs (PB-LLMs). Note that the proposed
PB-LLMs have been evaluated in a particular setup (resume scoring), but are
generally applicable to any other LLM-based AI application.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a framework using Named-Entity Recognition (NER) to anonymize sensitive data during Large Language Model (LLM) training, preserving privacy without sacrificing performance. It was tested on resume scoring with models like BERT and RoBERTa and multiple anonymization methods, showing effective privacy protection and maintained accuracy. Additionally, gender bias reduction techniques were applied, resulting in Privacy- and Bias-aware LLMs (PB-LLMs).
AI.motivation: LLMs have introduced significant legal and ethical concerns, especially around privacy, data protection, and transparency in high-stakes AI applications, necessitating methods to preserve user privacy during model training or adaptation.
AI.method: A framework leveraging NER technologies was developed to anonymize sensitive information such as personal identities and locations in text data. The framework was evaluated using two language models (BERT and RoBERTa) and six anonymization algorithms on a dataset of 24,000 candidate profiles, applied in AI-based resume scoring. A gender bias reduction method was also incorporated to enhance fairness.
AI.result: The privacy-preserving techniques maintained system performance in resume scoring tasks while effectively protecting candidate confidentiality. The combined application of anonymization and bias mitigation resulted in the proposed Privacy- and Bias-aware LLMs, which demonstrated trustworthiness and reliability in the experimental setup.
AI.conclusion: The study validates that NER-based anonymization can safeguard privacy in LLM training without degrading performance. The proposed PB-LLMs promote ethical AI by integrating privacy preservation and bias reduction, applicable beyond resume scoring to other LLM-driven AI applications.

title: Supporting Software Formal Verification with Large Language Models: An Experimental Study
comment:
Accepted for publication in 2025 IEEE 33rd International Requirements
  Engineering Conference (RE)
summary:
Formal methods have been employed for requirements verification for a long
time. However, it is difficult to automatically derive properties from natural
language requirements. SpecVerify addresses this challenge by integrating large
language models (LLMs) with formal verification tools, providing a more
flexible mechanism for expressing requirements. This framework combines Claude
3.5 Sonnet with the ESBMC verifier to form an automated workflow. Evaluated on
nine cyber-physical systems from Lockheed Martin, SpecVerify achieves 46.5%
verification accuracy, comparable to NASA's CoCoSim, but with lower false
positives. Our framework formulates assertions that extend beyond the
expressive power of LTL and identifies falsifiable cases that are missed by
more traditional methods. Counterexample analysis reveals CoCoSim's limitations
stemming from model connection errors and numerical approximation issues. While
SpecVerify advances verification automation, our comparative study of Claude,
ChatGPT, and Llama shows that high-quality requirements documentation and human
monitoring remain critical, as models occasionally misinterpret specifications.
Our results demonstrate that LLMs can significantly reduce the barriers to
formal verification, while highlighting the continued importance of
human-machine collaboration in achieving optimal results.

AI.tldr: SpecVerify integrates large language models with formal verification tools, achieving competitive accuracy in verifying cyber-physical systems with fewer false positives than traditional methods, while emphasizing the importance of human oversight.
AI.motivation: Automating the derivation of formal properties from natural language requirements is challenging, limiting the effectiveness of formal verification.
AI.method: SpecVerify combines the Claude 3.5 Sonnet language model with the ESBMC verifier to create an automated workflow for generating and checking assertions beyond traditional LTL scope.
AI.result: Testing on nine Lockheed Martin cyber-physical systems showed 46.5% verification accuracy comparable to NASA's CoCoSim but with fewer false positives. The approach also identifies errors missed by traditional methods and reveals limitations in CoCoSim due to model and numerical issues.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can significantly ease formal verification by bridging natural language and formal methods, but due to occasional misinterpretations, integrating human review is essential for optimal verification outcomes.

title: We Need Knowledge Distillation for Solving Math Word Problems
summary:
The enhancement of mathematical capabilities in large language models (LLMs)
fosters new developments in mathematics education within primary and secondary
schools, particularly as they relate to intelligent tutoring systems. However,
LLMs require substantial computational resources, resulting in significant
costs in educational contexts. To mitigate this drawback, this paper
investigates the feasibility of compressing LLMs for solving math word problems
(MWPs). We compress the embedded vectors encoded by BERT and distill a
considerably smaller student model. Our findings indicate that the student
model can maintain nearly 90% of the performance of the teacher model while
utilizing only 1/12 of its parameters. In addition to achieving high accuracy,
the model exhibits strong generalizability, as the compressed vectors perform
well across all tasks related to MWPs, and the distillation process is not
task-specific. The success of this distillation demonstrates that the
underlying principles are generic and not limited to a specific task. We
further explore the reasons behind the compressibility of embedded vectors,
revealing that part-of-speech information, rather than entity recognition, is
crucial for MWPs, which may significantly contribute to their compressibility.
The improvements in efficiency and cost reduction provide substantial value for
intelligent tutoring systems and significantly advance the field of intelligent
education.

AI.tldr: This paper explores compressing large language models (LLMs) like BERT for solving math word problems to reduce computational cost in education. A smaller student model retains 90% accuracy using 1/12 parameters, maintaining generalizability. Part-of-speech information aids compressibility.
AI.motivation: LLMs require substantial computational resources, which is costly in educational contexts like intelligent tutoring systems. There is a need to reduce these costs without losing performance.
AI.method: Compressing the embedded vectors encoded by BERT and distilling a much smaller student model that is not task-specific for math word problems.
AI.result: The student model achieves nearly 90% of the teacher model's performance with only 1/12 parameters and shows strong generalizability across MWP tasks. Part-of-speech information was key for compressibility.
AI.conclusion: Compressing LLMs by vector distillation is effective and generalizable, significantly reducing cost and improving efficiency in education-focused AI, advancing intelligent tutoring systems.

title: Towards a Unifying Reference Model for Digital Twins of Cyber-Physical Systems
summary:
Digital twins are sophisticated software systems for the representation,
monitoring, and control of cyber-physical systems, including automotive,
avionics, smart manufacturing, and many more. Existing definitions and
reference models of digital twins are overly abstract, impeding their
comprehensive understanding and implementation guidance. Consequently, a
significant gap emerges between abstract concepts and their industrial
implementations. We analyze popular reference models for digital twins and
combine these into a significantly detailed unifying reference model for
digital twins that reduces the concept-implementation gap to facilitate their
engineering in industrial practice. This enhances the understanding of the
concepts of digital twins and their relationships and guides developers to
implement digital twins effectively.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies a gap between abstract digital twin concepts and their practical implementation in industry, and proposes a detailed unifying reference model to bridge this gap.
AI.motivation: Existing digital twin definitions and models are too abstract, hindering clear understanding and effective industrial implementation.
AI.method: The authors analyze popular existing reference models and synthesize them into a more detailed, unified reference model for digital twins.
AI.result: The new reference model reduces the gap between conceptual digital twin ideas and their practical engineering, improving comprehension and guiding developers in implementation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed detailed unifying model facilitates industrial engineering of digital twins by clarifying concepts and relationships, and offering practical implementation guidance.

title: Truth, Trust, and Trouble: Medical AI on the Edge
summary:
Large Language Models (LLMs) hold significant promise for transforming
digital health by enabling automated medical question answering. However,
ensuring these models meet critical industry standards for factual accuracy,
usefulness, and safety remains a challenge, especially for open-source
solutions. We present a rigorous benchmarking framework using a dataset of over
1,000 health questions. We assess model performance across honesty,
helpfulness, and harmlessness. Our results highlight trade-offs between factual
reliability and safety among evaluated models -- Mistral-7B,
BioMistral-7B-DARE, and AlpaCare-13B. AlpaCare-13B achieves the highest
accuracy (91.7%) and harmlessness (0.92), while domain-specific tuning in
BioMistral-7B-DARE boosts safety (0.90) despite its smaller scale. Few-shot
prompting improves accuracy from 78% to 85%, and all models show reduced
helpfulness on complex queries, highlighting ongoing challenges in clinical QA.

AI.tldr: This paper benchmarks open-source Large Language Models for medical question answering, focusing on honesty, helpfulness, and harmlessness using a dataset of 1,000+ health questions, revealing trade-offs in factual accuracy and safety, with AlpaCare-13B showing highest accuracy and safety measures.
AI.motivation: To ensure that Large Language Models used in digital health meet critical industry standards for factual accuracy, usefulness, and safety, addressing the challenge especially for open-source LLMs.
AI.method: The authors developed a benchmarking framework evaluating models Mistral-7B, BioMistral-7B-DARE, and AlpaCare-13B on honesty, helpfulness, and harmlessness metrics using a dataset of over 1,000 health questions, also testing effects of few-shot prompting.
AI.result: AlpaCare-13B achieved the highest accuracy (91.7%) and harmlessness (0.92). BioMistral-7B-DARE had improved safety (0.90) due to domain-specific tuning despite being smaller. Few-shot prompting increased accuracy from 78% to 85%, but all models were less helpful on complex queries.
AI.conclusion: Trade-offs exist between factual reliability and safety in medical LLMs; domain-specific tuning and few-shot prompting can improve performance, but challenges remain in ensuring helpfulness for complex clinical questions.

title: Understanding Everything as Code: A Taxonomy and Conceptual Model
comment:
Accepted by the 19th ACM/IEEE International Symposium on Empirical
  Software Engineering and Measurement (ESEM 2025), Technical Papers track
summary:
Background: Everything as Code (EaC) is an emerging paradigm aiming to codify
all aspects of modern software systems. Despite its growing popularity,
comprehensive industry standards and peer-reviewed research clarifying its
scope and guiding its adoption remain scarce. Aims: This study systematically
analyzes existing knowledge and perceptions of EaC, clarifies its scope and
boundaries, and provides structured guidance for researchers and practitioners.
Method: We conducted a large-scale multivocal literature review (MLR),
synthesizing academic and grey literature sources. Findings were analyzed
quantitatively and thematically. Based on this analysis, we developed a
taxonomy and conceptual model of EaC, validated through collaboration with
industry experts. Results: The resulting taxonomy comprises 25 distinct EaC
practices organized into six layers based on industry awareness and functional
roles. The conceptual model illustrates focus areas, overlaps, and interactions
among these EaC practices within the software delivery lifecycle. Additionally,
practical code examples demonstrating the implementation of these practices
were developed in collaboration with industry experts. Conclusions: This work
addresses the current scarcity of academic discourse on EaC by providing the
first comprehensive taxonomy and conceptual model. These contributions enhance
conceptual clarity, offer actionable guidance to practitioners, and lay the
groundwork for future research in this emerging domain.

AI.tldr: This study systematically reviews Everything as Code (EaC), developing the first comprehensive taxonomy and conceptual model, validated by industry experts, to clarify its scope and guide adoption.
AI.motivation: Despite growing popularity of EaC, there is a lack of comprehensive standards and peer-reviewed research to define its scope and guide practitioners effectively.
AI.method: A taxonomy of 25 EaC practices arranged in six layers and a conceptual model illustrating their interrelations within the software delivery lifecycle were created, alongside practical code examples developed with industry experts.
AI.result: The taxonomy and model provide structured understanding of EaC, highlighting focus areas, overlaps, and interactions among practices, facilitating clearer adoption guidelines.
AI.conclusion: This research fills an academic gap by offering the first comprehensive taxonomy and model for EaC, enhancing clarity and providing practical guidance for future research and industry adoption.

title: From Answers to Rationales: Self-Aligning Multimodal Reasoning with Answer-Oriented Chain-of-Thought
summary:
Achieving human-like reasoning capabilities in Multimodal Large Language
Models (MLLMs) has long been a goal. Current methodologies primarily focus on
synthesizing positive rationales, while overlooking the critical role of
negative rationales in training models to discern flawed reasoning patterns. To
address this gap, we propose a novel framework: \textbf{S}elf-Aligning
\textbf{M}ultimodal Reasoning with \textbf{A}nswer-O\textbf{r}iented
Chain-of-\textbf{T}hought (SMART). This framework enables models to utilize
AoT-Oriented Chain-of-Thought (AoT) prompts to automatically generate
high-quality positive and negative reasoning paths, followed by self-alignment
to enhance their reasoning abilities. Inspired by human strategies for solving
proof-based problems, AoT uses answers as a guide to help the model extract
critical visual information that links questions and answers. When provided
with ground truth answers, the model produces strong positive rationales.
Conversely, when correct answers are replaced with misleading alternatives, the
model generates an erroneous yet compelling reasoning path, serving as a form
of discriminative negative rationale. Models trained with AoT-generated data
outperform those trained on manually annotated datasets, demonstrating superior
reasoning capabilities. This encourages the use of improved models to generate
higher-quality preference data for further optimization. Consequently, SMART
establishes an iterative generation-optimization method that continually
enhances the model's reasoning skills. Experiments indicate that the SMART
framework significantly improves various MLLMs, regardless of model
architecture, parameter size, or pre-training dataset. The code, datasets, and
models will be released.

AI.tldr: SMART is a framework that improves reasoning in multimodal large language models by generating both positive and negative reasoning paths using answer-oriented chain-of-thought prompts, leading to iterative self-alignment and better performance.
AI.motivation: Existing MLLM training methods focus only on positive rationales and ignore negative rationales, which are crucial for learning to identify flawed reasoning.
AI.method: The SMART framework uses AoT prompts guided by answers to generate positive and negative reasoning paths automatically, then applies self-alignment to enhance reasoning abilities through iterative generation-optimization.
AI.result: Models trained with AoT-generated rationales outperform those trained on manual datasets, showing superior reasoning capabilities across different architectures and sizes.
AI.conclusion: SMART effectively improves MLLMs' reasoning by iteratively generating and optimizing rationales, leveraging both positive and negative examples, and is broadly applicable regardless of model specifics.

title: In-Context Learning as an Effective Estimator of Functional Correctness of LLM-Generated Code
summary:
When applying LLM-based code generation to software development projects that
follow a feature-driven or rapid application development approach, it becomes
necessary to estimate the functional correctness of the generated code in the
absence of test cases. Just as a user selects a relevant document from a ranked
list of retrieved ones, a software generation workflow requires a developer to
choose (and potentially refine) a generated solution from a ranked list of
alternative solutions, ordered by their posterior likelihoods. This implies
that estimating the quality of a ranked list -- akin to estimating "relevance"
for query performance prediction (QPP) in IR -- is also crucial for generative
software development, where quality is defined in terms of "functional
correctness". In this paper, we propose an in-context learning (ICL) based
approach for code quality estimation. Our findings demonstrate that providing
few-shot examples of functionally correct code from a training set enhances the
performance of existing QPP approaches as well as a zero-shot-based approach
for code quality estimation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an in-context learning approach to estimate the functional correctness of LLM-generated code without test cases, improving code quality ranking in software development.
AI.motivation: To address the need for assessing functional correctness of code generated by LLMs in rapid development workflows where test cases are often absent, analogous to query performance prediction in information retrieval.
AI.method: The authors introduce an in-context learning (ICL) method that supplies few-shot examples of functionally correct code to improve code quality estimation and enhance existing and zero-shot ranking methods.
AI.result: The approach demonstrated improved performance in code quality estimation, boosting the effectiveness of both existing QPP methods and zero-shot baselines when few-shot examples were incorporated.
AI.conclusion: In-context learning with few-shot examples effectively estimates functional correctness of generated code, supporting better decision-making in ranked solution selection for generative software development.

title: GAF-Guard: An Agentic Framework for Risk Management and Governance in Large Language Models
summary:
As Large Language Models (LLMs) continue to be increasingly applied across
various domains, their widespread adoption necessitates rigorous monitoring to
prevent unintended negative consequences and ensure robustness. Furthermore,
LLMs must be designed to align with human values, like preventing harmful
content and ensuring responsible usage. The current automated systems and
solutions for monitoring LLMs in production are primarily centered on
LLM-specific concerns like hallucination etc, with little consideration given
to the requirements of specific use-cases and user preferences. This paper
introduces GAF-Guard, a novel agentic framework for LLM governance that places
the user, the use-case, and the model itself at the center. The framework is
designed to detect and monitor risks associated with the deployment of LLM
based applications. The approach models autonomous agents that identify risks,
activate risk detection tools, within specific use-cases and facilitate
continuous monitoring and reporting to enhance AI safety, and user
expectations. The code is available at
https://github.com/IBM/risk-atlas-nexus-demos/tree/main/gaf-guard.

AI.tldr: GAF-Guard is a novel framework designed to monitor and manage risks in Large Language Model deployments by focusing on the user, use-case, and the model, using autonomous agents for continuous risk detection and reporting to enhance AI safety and align with human values.
AI.motivation: To address the gap in existing LLM monitoring systems which mainly focus on generic LLM risks like hallucination, without considering specific use-case requirements and user preferences, thereby ensuring safer and more responsible deployment of LLMs.
AI.method: GAF-Guard employs an agentic framework wherein autonomous agents identify potential risks related to specific use-cases, activate suitable risk detection tools, and enable continuous monitoring and reporting of LLM-based applications.
AI.result: The framework facilitates enhanced detection and monitoring of risks in LLM applications tailored to user needs and specific deployment contexts, contributing to improved AI safety and alignment with human values.
AI.conclusion: GAF-Guard provides a user and use-case-centric approach to governing LLMs that improves risk detection and continuous monitoring, helping to ensure responsible usage and robustness in real-world LLM deployments.

title: An Investigation into Maintenance Support for Neural Networks
comment:
Revised version accepted at the HumanAISE Workshop, co-located with
  FSE 2025
summary:
As the potential for neural networks to augment our daily lives grows,
ensuring their quality through effective testing, debugging, and maintenance is
essential. This is especially the case as we acknowledge the prospects of
negative impacts from these technologies. Traditional software engineering
methods, such as testing and debugging, have proven effective in maintaining
software quality; however, they reveal significant research and practice gaps
in maintaining neural networks. In particular, there is a limited understanding
of how practitioners currently address challenges related to understanding and
mitigating undesirable behaviors in neural networks. In our ongoing research,
we explore the current state of research and practice in maintaining neural
networks by curating insights from practitioners through a preliminary study
involving interviews and supporting survey responses. Our findings thus far
indicate that existing tools primarily concentrate on building and training
models. While these tools can be beneficial, they often fall short of
supporting practitioners' understanding and addressing the underlying causes of
unexpected model behavior. By evaluating current procedures and identifying the
limitations of traditional methodologies, our study aims to offer a
developer-centric perspective on where current practices fall short and
highlight opportunities for improving maintenance support in neural networks.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how practitioners maintain neural networks, identifying gaps in current tools and methodologies, especially regarding debugging and understanding unexpected model behaviors.
AI.motivation: As neural networks increasingly impact daily life, ensuring their quality via effective testing and maintenance is crucial, yet current software engineering methods inadequately address neural network-specific challenges.
AI.method: A preliminary study involving interviews and surveys with practitioners to gather insights on the state of neural network maintenance practices and the efficacy of existing tools.
AI.result: Findings show that existing tools focus mainly on model building and training but poorly support understanding and mitigating unexpected neural network behaviors.
AI.conclusion: The study highlights the limitations of traditional software maintenance approaches for neural networks and suggests opportunities for improved, developer-centric maintenance tools and practices tailored to neural networks.

title: A Comparative Study of Competency Question Elicitation Methods from Ontology Requirements
summary:
Competency Questions (CQs) are pivotal in knowledge engineering, guiding the
design, validation, and testing of ontologies. A number of diverse formulation
approaches have been proposed in the literature, ranging from completely manual
to Large Language Model (LLM) driven ones. However, attempts to characterise
the outputs of these approaches and their systematic comparison are scarce.
This paper presents an empirical comparative evaluation of three distinct CQ
formulation approaches: manual formulation by ontology engineers, instantiation
of CQ patterns, and generation using state of the art LLMs. We generate CQs
using each approach from a set of requirements for cultural heritage, and
assess them across different dimensions: degree of acceptability, ambiguity,
relevance, readability and complexity. Our contribution is twofold: (i) the
first multi-annotator dataset of CQs generated from the same source using
different methods; and (ii) a systematic comparison of the characteristics of
the CQs resulting from each approach. Our study shows that different CQ
generation approaches have different characteristics and that LLMs can be used
as a way to initially elicit CQs, however these are sensitive to the model used
to generate CQs and they generally require a further refinement step before
they can be used to model requirements.

AI.tldr: The paper empirically compares three approaches to formulating Competency Questions for ontologies: manual, pattern instantiation, and Large Language Models (LLMs). It evaluates their acceptability, ambiguity, relevance, readability, and complexity, providing a unique multi-annotator dataset and analysis. The study finds that LLMs can initially generate CQs but need refinement and that each approach has distinct characteristics.
AI.motivation: Competency Questions are critical in ontology design and validation, yet there is limited work characterizing and comparing different CQ formulation methods systematically.
AI.method: The study generated CQs from cultural heritage requirements using three methods—manual by engineers, pattern instantiation, and LLM generation—and assessed them through multiple quality dimensions with multiple annotators.
AI.result: The results show distinct characteristics in CQs produced by different methods, with LLMs capable of initial CQ elicitation but sensitive to model choice and generally needing refinement before use.
AI.conclusion: While LLMs provide a promising tool for CQ generation, a refinement step is necessary, and different approaches yield CQs with varying properties, highlighting the importance of method choice in ontology engineering.

title: ArtifactsBench: Bridging the Visual-Interactive Gap in LLM Code Generation Evaluation
summary:
The generative capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) are rapidly
expanding from static code to dynamic, interactive visual artifacts. This
progress is bottlenecked by a critical evaluation gap: established benchmarks
focus on algorithmic correctness and are blind to the visual fidelity and
interactive integrity that define modern user experiences. To bridge this gap,
we introduce ArtifactsBench, a new benchmark and paradigm for the automated,
multimodal evaluation of visual code generation. Our framework programmatically
renders each generated artifact and captures its dynamic behavior through
temporal screenshots. This visual evidence, alongside the source code, is then
assessed by a Multimodal LLM (MLLM)-as-Judge, which is rigorously guided by a
fine-grained, per-task checklist to ensure holistic and reproducible scoring.
We construct a new benchmark of 1,825 diverse tasks and evaluate over 30
leading LLMs. Our automated evaluation achieves a striking 94.4% ranking
consistency with WebDev Arena, the gold-standard for human preference in web
development, and over 90% pairwise agreement with human experts. This
establishes ArtifactsBench as the first framework to reliably automate the
assessment of human-perceived quality at scale. Our analysis provides a
high-resolution map of the current SOTA, revealing that generalist models often
outperform domain-specific ones. We open-source ArtifactsBench, including the
benchmark, evaluation harness, and baseline results at
https://artifactsbenchmark.github.io/, to provide the community with a scalable
and accurate tool to accelerate the development of user-centric generative
models.

AI.tldr: ArtifactsBench is a new automated benchmark for evaluating visual code generation by large language models, focusing on visual fidelity and interactive integrity beyond algorithmic correctness.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks for LLM-generated code emphasize algorithmic correctness but fail to assess visual quality and interactive features vital for modern user experiences.
AI.method: ArtifactsBench renders generated artifacts, captures their dynamic behavior with screenshots, and uses a multimodal LLM judge guided by detailed checklists to evaluate both code and visual output holistically.
AI.result: Evaluating 30+ leading LLMs on 1,825 tasks, the framework achieved 94.4% ranking consistency with human preferences and over 90% agreement with human experts, establishing reliable automated assessment at scale.
AI.conclusion: ArtifactsBench effectively bridges evaluation gaps by automating human-like quality assessment of visual code generation, revealing that generalist models can outperform domain-specific ones, and offers an open-source toolset to advance user-centric generative AI.

title: `For Argument's Sake, Show Me How to Harm Myself!': Jailbreaking LLMs in Suicide and Self-Harm Contexts
summary:
Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have led to increasingly
sophisticated safety protocols and features designed to prevent harmful,
unethical, or unauthorized outputs. However, these guardrails remain
susceptible to novel and creative forms of adversarial prompting, including
manually generated test cases. In this work, we present two new test cases in
mental health for (i) suicide and (ii) self-harm, using multi-step,
prompt-level jailbreaking and bypass built-in content and safety filters. We
show that user intent is disregarded, leading to the generation of detailed
harmful content and instructions that could cause real-world harm. We conduct
an empirical evaluation across six widely available LLMs, demonstrating the
generalizability and reliability of the bypass. We assess these findings and
the multilayered ethical tensions that they present for their implications on
prompt-response filtering and context- and task-specific model development. We
recommend a more comprehensive and systematic approach to AI safety and ethics
while emphasizing the need for continuous adversarial testing in
safety-critical AI deployments. We also argue that while certain clearly
defined safety measures and guardrails can and must be implemented in LLMs,
ensuring robust and comprehensive safety across all use cases and domains
remains extremely challenging given the current technical maturity of
general-purpose LLMs.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates the vulnerability of large language models (LLMs) to adversarial prompting designed to bypass safety filters, focusing on mental health topics like suicide and self-harm.
AI.motivation: Despite sophisticated safety protocols in LLMs, harmful outputs can still be generated through novel adversarial prompts, raising serious ethical and safety concerns.
AI.method: The authors introduce multi-step, prompt-level jailbreak test cases targeting suicide and self-harm, empirically testing six widely available LLMs for bypassing content and safety filters.
AI.result: Results show that LLMs disregard user intent to provide detailed harmful content and instructions, demonstrating the reliability and generalizability of these bypasses across models.
AI.conclusion: The study highlights significant ethical tensions and the current limitations of safety guardrails, advocating for more systematic adversarial testing and comprehensive safety measures tailored to specific contexts and tasks in AI development.

title: Evaluating Hierarchical Clinical Document Classification Using Reasoning-Based LLMs
summary:
This study evaluates how well large language models (LLMs) can classify
ICD-10 codes from hospital discharge summaries, a critical but error-prone task
in healthcare. Using 1,500 summaries from the MIMIC-IV dataset and focusing on
the 10 most frequent ICD-10 codes, the study tested 11 LLMs, including models
with and without structured reasoning capabilities. Medical terms were
extracted using a clinical NLP tool (cTAKES), and models were prompted in a
consistent, coder-like format. None of the models achieved an F1 score above
57%, with performance dropping as code specificity increased. Reasoning-based
models generally outperformed non-reasoning ones, with Gemini 2.5 Pro
performing best overall. Some codes, such as those related to chronic heart
disease, were classified more accurately than others. The findings suggest that
while LLMs can assist human coders, they are not yet reliable enough for full
automation. Future work should explore hybrid methods, domain-specific model
training, and the use of structured clinical data.

AI.tldr: The study assesses large language models' ability to classify ICD-10 codes from hospital discharge summaries and finds none achieve high accuracy, though reasoning-based models perform better.
AI.motivation: Accurate ICD-10 coding from hospital discharge summaries is critical but error-prone, and the study seeks to evaluate if LLMs can improve this process.
AI.method: 11 LLMs (both reasoning and non-reasoning types) were tested on 1,500 MIMIC-IV discharge summaries focusing on the 10 most frequent ICD-10 codes, using clinical NLP (cTAKES) for term extraction and consistent prompting.
AI.result: No model exceeded an F1 score of 57%; performance decreased with more specific codes. Reasoning-based models generally outperformed non-reasoning ones, with Gemini 2.5 Pro being the best.
AI.conclusion: LLMs currently assist but cannot replace human coders for ICD-10 classification. Future work should include hybrid approaches, domain-specific training, and incorporation of structured clinical data.

title: Breaking Physical and Linguistic Borders: Multilingual Federated Prompt Tuning for Low-Resource Languages
summary:
Pre-trained large language models (LLMs) have become a cornerstone of modern
natural language processing, with their capabilities extending across a wide
range of applications and languages. However, the fine-tuning of multilingual
LLMs, especially for low-resource languages, faces significant challenges
arising from data-sharing restrictions (the physical border) and inherent
linguistic differences (the linguistic border). These barriers hinder users of
various languages, particularly those in low-resource regions, from fully
benefiting from the advantages of LLMs. To address these challenges, we propose
the Federated Prompt Tuning Paradigm for multilingual scenarios, which utilizes
parameter-efficient fine-tuning while adhering to data sharing restrictions. We
design a comprehensive set of experiments and analyze them using a novel notion
of language distance to highlight the strengths of our paradigm: Even under
computational constraints, our method not only improves data efficiency but
also facilitates mutual enhancements across languages, particularly benefiting
low-resource ones. Compared to traditional local cross-lingual transfer tuning
methods, our approach achieves 6.9\% higher accuracy with improved data
efficiency, and demonstrates greater stability and generalization. These
findings underscore the potential of our approach to promote social equality
and champion linguistic diversity, ensuring that no language is left behind.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the challenges of fine-tuning multilingual large language models (LLMs) for low-resource languages under data-sharing restrictions by proposing Federated Prompt Tuning, a parameter-efficient method that improves accuracy and data efficiency while supporting linguistic diversity.
AI.motivation: The fine-tuning of multilingual LLMs faces barriers due to data-sharing restrictions and linguistic differences, limiting benefits for low-resource languages and diverse users.
AI.method: Proposing a Federated Prompt Tuning paradigm that applies parameter-efficient fine-tuning respecting data privacy, with experiments analyzed via a novel language distance metric to evaluate cross-lingual transfer.
AI.result: The approach achieves 6.9% higher accuracy than traditional local tuning methods, improves data efficiency, and shows better stability and generalization, particularly benefiting low-resource languages.
AI.conclusion: Federated Prompt Tuning effectively overcomes physical and linguistic barriers in multilingual LLM fine-tuning, promoting social equality and linguistic diversity by enabling inclusive improvements across languages.

title: CLUES: Collaborative High-Quality Data Selection for LLMs via Training Dynamics
summary:
Recent research has highlighted the importance of data quality in scaling
large language models (LLMs). However, automated data quality control faces
unique challenges in collaborative settings where sharing is not allowed
directly between data silos. To tackle this issue, this paper proposes a novel
data quality control technique based on the notion of data influence on the
training dynamics of LLMs, that high quality data are more likely to have
similar training dynamics to the anchor dataset. We then leverage the influence
of the training dynamics to select high-quality data from different private
domains, with centralized model updates on the server side in a collaborative
training fashion by either model merging or federated learning. As for the data
quality indicator, we compute the per-sample gradients with respect to the
private data and the anchor dataset, and use the trace of the accumulated inner
products as a measurement of data quality. In addition, we develop a quality
control evaluation tailored for collaborative settings with heterogeneous
domain data. Experiments show that training on the high-quality data selected
by our method can often outperform other data selection methods for
collaborative fine-tuning of LLMs, across diverse private domain datasets, in
medical, multilingual and financial settings. Our code is released at
github.com/Ryan0v0/CLUES.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a data quality control method for collaborative training of large language models (LLMs) that respects data privacy across silos by leveraging training dynamics influence-based data selection, improving performance in diverse domains.
AI.motivation: Automated data quality control in collaborative settings faces challenges due to data privacy and siloed datasets, hindering direct data sharing and impacting LLM scaling.
AI.method: The method measures data quality by computing per-sample gradients and the trace of accumulated inner products between private and anchor datasets, selecting high-quality data for collaborative fine-tuning via model merging or federated learning.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate that models trained on data selected by this approach outperform other data selection methods across heterogeneous datasets in medical, multilingual, and financial domains.
AI.conclusion: Using training dynamics influence as a data quality indicator enables effective, privacy-preserving collaborative data quality control, leading to superior LLM fine-tuning outcomes across various private domains.

title: Beyond cognacy
summary:
Computational phylogenetics has become an established tool in historical
linguistics, with many language families now analyzed using likelihood-based
inference. However, standard approaches rely on expert-annotated cognate sets,
which are sparse, labor-intensive to produce, and limited to individual
language families. This paper explores alternatives by comparing the
established method to two fully automated methods that extract phylogenetic
signal directly from lexical data. One uses automatic cognate clustering with
unigram/concept features; the other applies multiple sequence alignment (MSA)
derived from a pair-hidden Markov model. Both are evaluated against expert
classifications from Glottolog and typological data from Grambank. Also, the
intrinsic strengths of the phylogenetic signal in the characters are compared.
Results show that MSA-based inference yields trees more consistent with
linguistic classifications, better predicts typological variation, and provides
a clearer phylogenetic signal, suggesting it as a promising, scalable
alternative to traditional cognate-based methods. This opens new avenues for
global-scale language phylogenies beyond expert annotation bottlenecks.

AI.tldr: This paper compares traditional expert-annotated cognate-based phylogenetic methods with two automated methods extracting phylogenetic signals directly from lexical data, finding that the MSA-based method outperforms others in consistency and predictive power.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations of standard phylogenetic approaches that rely on sparse, labor-intensive expert-annotated cognate sets, which restrict analysis to individual language families.
AI.method: Comparison of the established expert-annotated cognate method against two fully automated methods: one using automatic cognate clustering with unigram/concept features, and another applying multiple sequence alignment (MSA) derived from a pair-hidden Markov model. Evaluations are against expert classifications (Glottolog) and typological data (Grambank).
AI.result: The MSA-based inference produced phylogenetic trees more consistent with established linguistic classifications, predicted typological variation better, and displayed a clearer phylogenetic signal compared to the other methods.
AI.conclusion: The MSA-based approach is a promising scalable alternative to traditional cognate-based methods, enabling global-scale language phylogenies without reliance on expert annotations, thus bypassing existing bottlenecks.

title: PDFMathTranslate: Scientific Document Translation Preserving Layouts
summary:
Language barriers in scientific documents hinder the diffusion and
development of science and technologies. However, prior efforts in translating
such documents largely overlooked the information in layouts. To bridge the
gap, we introduce PDFMathTranslate, the world's first open-source software for
translating scientific documents while preserving layouts. Leveraging the most
recent advances in large language models and precise layout detection, we
contribute to the community with key improvements in precision, flexibility,
and efficiency. The work has been open-sourced at
https://github.com/byaidu/pdfmathtranslate with more than 22k downloads.

AI.tldr: PDFMathTranslate is an open-source software that translates scientific documents while preserving their layouts, improving precision, flexibility, and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Language barriers impede the dissemination and development of science and technology, and previous translation tools overlooked document layout information.
AI.method: The software leverages recent advances in large language models and precise layout detection to translate documents while maintaining their original layouts.
AI.result: The tool has been successfully open-sourced and downloaded over 22,000 times, demonstrating community impact and usability.
AI.conclusion: PDFMathTranslate advances scientific document translation by integrating layout preservation, addressing previous gaps, and enhancing translation quality and usability.

title: Subversion via Focal Points: Investigating Collusion in LLM Monitoring
summary:
We evaluate language models' ability to subvert monitoring protocols via
collusion. More specifically, we have two instances of a model design prompts
for a policy (P) and a monitor (M) in a programming task setting. The models
collaboratively aim for M to classify all backdoored programs in an auditing
dataset as harmful, but nevertheless classify a backdoored program produced by
P as harmless. The models are isolated from each other, requiring them to
independently arrive at compatible subversion strategies. We find that while
Claude 3.7 Sonnet has low success rate due to poor convergence, it sometimes
successfully colludes on non-obvious signals.

AI.tldr: The paper studies if language models can collude to bypass monitoring systems in programming tasks, finding mixed success depending on the model.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether language models can strategically subvert monitoring protocols through collusion without direct communication.
AI.method: Two isolated model instances are prompted as policy and monitor agents to collaboratively deceive the monitor into misclassifying backdoored programs in an auditing dataset.
AI.result: Claude 3.7 Sonnet showed limited success due to convergence issues but occasionally succeeded in subtle collusion strategies.
AI.conclusion: Language models can sometimes independently develop compatible subversion tactics to evade detection, highlighting challenges in reliable monitoring.

title: Beyond Overcorrection: Evaluating Diversity in T2I Models with DIVBENCH
summary:
Current diversification strategies for text-to-image (T2I) models often
ignore contextual appropriateness, leading to over-diversification where
demographic attributes are modified even when explicitly specified in prompts.
This paper introduces DIVBENCH, a benchmark and evaluation framework for
measuring both under- and over-diversification in T2I generation. Through
systematic evaluation of state-of-the-art T2I models, we find that while most
models exhibit limited diversity, many diversification approaches overcorrect
by inappropriately altering contextually-specified attributes. We demonstrate
that context-aware methods, particularly LLM-guided FairDiffusion and prompt
rewriting, can already effectively address under-diversity while avoiding
over-diversification, achieving a better balance between representation and
semantic fidelity.

AI.tldr: DIVBENCH is proposed to evaluate both under- and over-diversification in text-to-image models, finding most models have limited diversity, some over-diversify. Context-aware methods like LLM-guided FairDiffusion better balance diversity and semantic accuracy.
AI.motivation: Existing T2I diversification methods often cause over-diversification by changing demographic attributes even when specified, failing to maintain contextual appropriateness.
AI.method: Introduced DIVBENCH benchmark to systematically evaluate state-of-the-art T2I models and tested context-aware methods such as LLM-guided FairDiffusion and prompt rewriting to control diversification.
AI.result: Most models show limited diversity; some diversification approaches cause overcorrection by altering specified attributes. Context-aware approaches effectively improve diversity without sacrificing semantic fidelity.
AI.conclusion: Context-aware diversification methods can better maintain demographic integrity and balance diversity and semantic relevance in T2I generation, as validated by DIVBENCH evaluation.

title: OpenTable-R1: A Reinforcement Learning Augmented Tool Agent for Open-Domain Table Question Answering
summary:
Open-domain table question answering traditionally relies on a two-stage
pipeline: static table retrieval followed by a closed-domain answer. In
contrast, we propose an end-to-end agentic framework that embeds multi-turn
tool calls-using a BM25+-based search API and a SQLite SQL executor-directly
into a large language model. To further adapt a compact 4B-parameter model, we
introduce a two-stage fine-tuning process: supervised cold-start on easy
questions, then Async GRPO reinforcement learning on harder cases with LoRA
adapters and a rollout buffer. This unified approach enables the model to
jointly retrieve, reason, and execute queries, yielding a dramatic accuracy
improvement from single-digit zero-shot performance to over 0.86 exact match on
a held-out test set. Our results underscore the effectiveness of integrating
structured tool calls with targeted RL fine-tuning for scalable, accurate table
QA. The code is available at https://github.com/TabibitoQZP/OpenTableR1.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an end-to-end framework for open-domain table question answering that integrates multi-turn tool calls within a large language model, significantly improving accuracy through a two-stage fine-tuning process including reinforcement learning.
AI.motivation: Traditional open-domain table QA relies on separate static table retrieval and closed-domain answering, which can limit accuracy and scalability. The paper aims to unify retrieval, reasoning, and execution in a single model to overcome these limitations.
AI.method: The authors embed multi-turn tool calls (a BM25+ search API and SQLite SQL executor) directly into a 4B-parameter large language model. They use a two-stage fine-tuning approach: supervised cold-start on simpler questions followed by Async GRPO reinforcement learning on harder queries, employing LoRA adapters and a rollout buffer for efficient adaptation.
AI.result: The unified agentic framework achieved a large accuracy boost, improving from single-digit zero-shot performance to over 0.86 exact match on a held-out test set, demonstrating strong gains in retrieval, reasoning, and execution capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Integrating structured tool calls with targeted reinforcement learning fine-tuning effectively enhances scalability and accuracy in open-domain table question answering, paving the way for more robust end-to-end systems.

title: The Book of Life approach: Enabling richness and scale for life course research
summary:
For over a century, life course researchers have faced a choice between two
dominant methodological approaches: qualitative methods that analyze rich data
but are constrained to small samples, and quantitative survey-based methods
that study larger populations but sacrifice data richness for scale. Two recent
technological developments now enable us to imagine a hybrid approach that
combines some of the depth of the qualitative approach with the scale of
quantitative methods. The first development is the steady rise of ''complex log
data,'' behavioral data that is logged for purposes other than research but
that can be repurposed to construct rich accounts of people's lives. The second
is the emergence of large language models (LLMs) with exceptional pattern
recognition capabilities on plain text. In this paper, we take a necessary step
toward creating this hybrid approach by developing a flexible procedure to
transform complex log data into a textual representation of an individual's
life trajectory across multiple domains, over time, and in context. We call
this data representation a ''book of life.'' We illustrate the feasibility of
our approach by writing over 100 million books of life covering many different
facets of life, over time and placed in social context using Dutch
population-scale registry data. We open source the book of life toolkit (BOLT),
and invite the research community to explore the many potential applications of
this approach.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a hybrid method combining qualitative depth and quantitative scale by transforming complex log data into textual 'books of life' using large language models, demonstrated with Dutch registry data and an open-source toolkit.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations of traditional life course research methods, which face a trade-off between qualitative richness and quantitative scale, leveraging new data types and AI technology.
AI.method: Developing a procedure to convert complex log behavioral data into textual life trajectories ('books of life') using large language models, demonstrated by creating 100 million such texts from Dutch population data.
AI.result: Successfully generated over 100 million 'books of life' capturing life facets over time and context, validating the approach's feasibility and scalability.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid approach using complex log data and LLMs offers a scalable and rich method for life course research, potentially transforming qualitative and quantitative studies, with an open-source toolkit provided for the research community.

title: Preserving Privacy, Increasing Accessibility, and Reducing Cost: An On-Device Artificial Intelligence Model for Medical Transcription and Note Generation
summary:
Background: Clinical documentation represents a significant burden for
healthcare providers, with physicians spending up to 2 hours daily on
administrative tasks. Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) offer
promising solutions, but privacy concerns and computational requirements limit
their adoption in healthcare settings. Objective: To develop and evaluate a
privacy-preserving, on-device medical transcription system using a fine-tuned
Llama 3.2 1B model capable of generating structured medical notes from medical
transcriptions while maintaining complete data sovereignty entirely in the
browser. Methods: We fine-tuned a Llama 3.2 1B model using Parameter-Efficient
Fine-Tuning (PEFT) with LoRA on 1,500 synthetic medical
transcription-to-structured note pairs. The model was evaluated against the
base Llama 3.2 1B on two datasets: 100 endocrinology transcripts and 140
modified ACI benchmark cases. Evaluation employed both statistical metrics
(ROUGE, BERTScore, BLEURT) and LLM-as-judge assessments across multiple
clinical quality dimensions. Results: The fine-tuned OnDevice model
demonstrated substantial improvements over the base model. On the ACI
benchmark, ROUGE-1 scores increased from 0.346 to 0.496, while BERTScore F1
improved from 0.832 to 0.866. Clinical quality assessments showed marked
reduction in major hallucinations (from 85 to 35 cases) and enhanced factual
correctness (2.81 to 3.54 on 5-point scale). Similar improvements were observed
on the internal evaluation dataset, with composite scores increasing from 3.13
to 4.43 (+41.5%). Conclusions: Fine-tuning compact LLMs for medical
transcription yields clinically meaningful improvements while enabling complete
on-device browser deployment. This approach addresses key barriers to AI
adoption in healthcare: privacy preservation, cost reduction, and accessibility
for resource-constrained environments.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a privacy-preserving on-device medical transcription system using a fine-tuned compact Llama 3.2 1B model that generates structured medical notes from transcriptions entirely within a browser, improving clinical documentation efficiency and data privacy.
AI.motivation: Physicians spend significant time on administrative tasks, and while large language models can help, privacy concerns and high computational demands limit their use in healthcare. The need for a solution that ensures data sovereignty and accessibility in resource-limited settings motivated this work.
AI.method: The authors fine-tuned a Llama 3.2 1B model using Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) with LoRA on 1,500 synthetic medical transcription-to-note pairs. The model's performance was evaluated on endocrinology transcripts and a modified ACI benchmark using statistical metrics (ROUGE, BERTScore, BLEURT) and clinical quality assessments with LLM-based judges.
AI.result: The fine-tuned OnDevice model significantly outperformed the base model, with notable improvements in ROUGE-1 and BERTScore, reduction of hallucinations, and enhanced factual correctness. Clinical quality composite scores improved by over 40%, demonstrating meaningful clinical gains.
AI.conclusion: Fine-tuning compact LLMs for medical transcription achieves clinically meaningful improvements while enabling fully on-device deployment that preserves privacy, reduces costs, and improves accessibility, addressing critical barriers to AI integration in healthcare.

title: Cautious Next Token Prediction
summary:
Next token prediction paradigm has been prevailing for autoregressive models
in the era of LLMs. The current default sampling choice for popular LLMs is
temperature scaling together with nucleus sampling to balance diversity and
coherence. Nevertheless, such approach leads to inferior performance in various
NLP tasks when the model is not certain about testing questions. To this end,
we propose a brand new training-free decoding strategy, dubbed as Cautious Next
Token Prediction (CNTP). In the decoding process, if the model has
comparatively high prediction entropy at a certain step, we sample multiple
trials starting from the step independently and stop when encountering any
punctuation. Then we select the trial with the lowest perplexity score viewed
as the most probable and reliable trial path given the model's capacity. The
trial number is negatively correlated with the prediction confidence, i.e., the
less confident the model is, the more trials it should sample. This is
consistent with human beings' behaviour: when feeling uncertain or unconfident,
one tends to think more creatively, exploring multiple thinking paths, to
cautiously select the path one feels most confident about. Extensive
experiments on both LLMs and MLLMs show that our proposed CNTP approach
outperforms existing standard decoding strategies consistently by a clear
margin. Moreover, the integration of CNTP with self consistency can further
improve over vanilla self consistency. We believe our proposed CNTP has the
potential to become one of the default choices for LLM decoding. Code is
available at https://github.com/wyzjack/CNTP.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Cautious Next Token Prediction (CNTP), a training-free decoding strategy for large language models that improves performance by adaptively exploring multiple token prediction paths when uncertainty is high, outperforming standard decoding methods.
AI.motivation: Current temperature scaling and nucleus sampling methods in LLM decoding fail to perform well on NLP tasks when the model is uncertain about predictions, leading to inferior results.
AI.method: CNTP detects high prediction entropy during decoding, then samples multiple trial continuations from that step, stopping at punctuation, and selects the trial with the lowest perplexity as the most reliable; the number of trials is inversely proportional to the model's confidence, mimicking human cautious reasoning.
AI.result: Experiments on large language and multimodal models demonstrate CNTP consistently outperforms existing standard decoding strategies and further improves results when combined with self-consistency methods.
AI.conclusion: CNTP is a promising default decoding approach for LLMs, enhancing reliability and performance by adaptively exploring diverse candidate outputs based on model confidence.

title: Dynamic Long Short-Term Memory Based Memory Storage For Long Horizon LLM Interaction
summary:
Memory storage for Large Language models (LLMs) is becoming an increasingly
active area of research, particularly for enabling personalization across long
conversations. We propose Pref-LSTM, a dynamic and lightweight framework that
combines a BERT-based classifier with a LSTM memory module that generates
memory embedding which then is soft-prompt injected into a frozen LLM. We
synthetically curate a dataset of preference and non-preference conversation
turns to train our BERT-based classifier. Although our LSTM-based memory
encoder did not yield strong results, we find that the BERT-based classifier
performs reliably in identifying explicit and implicit user preferences. Our
research demonstrates the viability of using preference filtering with LSTM
gating principals as an efficient path towards scalable user preference
modeling, without extensive overhead and fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: Pref-LSTM is a lightweight framework using a BERT classifier and LSTM memory to enable user preference modeling in LLMs for personalization in long conversations.
AI.motivation: To enable scalable and efficient personalization in large language models across long conversations by capturing and utilizing user preferences.
AI.method: Pref-LSTM combines a BERT-based classifier trained on synthetic preference data with an LSTM memory module to generate embeddings that are soft-prompt injected into a frozen LLM.
AI.result: The BERT-based classifier effectively identifies explicit and implicit user preferences, though the LSTM memory encoder showed weak results.
AI.conclusion: Preference filtering using BERT with LSTM gating principles is a promising and efficient approach for scalable user preference modeling in LLMs without heavy fine-tuning or overhead.

title: K-Function: Joint Pronunciation Transcription and Feedback for Evaluating Kids Language Function
summary:
Early evaluation of children's language is frustrated by the high pitch, long
phones, and sparse data that derail automatic speech recognisers. We introduce
K-Function, a unified framework that combines accurate sub-word transcription,
objective scoring, and actionable feedback. Its core, Kids-WFST, merges a
Wav2Vec2 phoneme encoder with a phoneme-similarity Dysfluent-WFST to capture
child-specific errors while remaining fully interpretable. Kids-WFST attains
1.39% phoneme error on MyST and 8.61% on Multitudes--absolute gains of 10.47
and 7.06 points over a greedy-search decoder. These high-fidelity transcripts
power an LLM that grades verbal skills, milestones, reading, and comprehension,
aligning with human proctors and supplying tongue-and-lip visualizations plus
targeted advice. The results show that precise phoneme recognition cements a
complete diagnostic-feedback loop, paving the way for scalable, clinician-ready
language assessment.

AI.tldr: K-Function combines sub-word transcription and feedback to improve children's language evaluation despite speech challenges.
AI.motivation: Early assessments of children's language are hindered by high-pitch, long phonemes, and limited data, which challenge automatic speech recognition.
AI.method: The paper introduces Kids-WFST, integrating a Wav2Vec2 phoneme encoder with a phoneme-similarity Dysfluent-WFST, enabling interpretable detection of child-specific speech errors. High-accuracy phoneme transcription feeds an LLM for grading and feedback.
AI.result: Kids-WFST achieves low phoneme error rates (1.39% on MyST, 8.61% on Multitudes), outperforming greedy decoders by significant margins. The system aligns well with human evaluation and provides visual and targeted feedback.
AI.conclusion: Accurate phoneme recognition through Kids-WFST enables a full diagnostic-feedback loop, facilitating scalable, clinician-ready assessments for children's language development.

title: Counterfactual Tuning for Temporal Sensitivity Enhancement in Large Language Model-based Recommendation
summary:
Recent advances have applied large language models (LLMs) to sequential
recommendation, leveraging their pre-training knowledge and reasoning
capabilities to provide more personalized user experiences. However, existing
LLM-based methods fail to sufficiently leverage the rich temporal information
inherent in users' historical interaction sequences, stemming from fundamental
architectural constraints: LLMs process information through self-attention
mechanisms that lack inherent sequence ordering and rely on position embeddings
designed primarily for natural language rather than user interaction sequences.
This limitation significantly impairs their ability to capture the evolution of
user preferences over time and predict future interests accurately.
  To address this critical gap, we propose Counterfactual Enhanced Temporal
Framework for LLM-Based Recommendation (CETRec). CETRec is grounded in causal
inference principles, which allow it to isolate and measure the specific impact
of temporal information on recommendation outcomes. By conceptualizing temporal
order as an independent causal factor distinct from item content, we can
quantify its unique contribution through counterfactual reasoning--comparing
what recommendations would be made with and without temporal information while
keeping all other factors constant. This causal framing enables CETRec to
design a novel counterfactual tuning objective that directly optimizes the
model's temporal sensitivity, teaching LLMs to recognize both absolute
timestamps and relative ordering patterns in user histories. Combined with our
counterfactual tuning task derived from causal analysis, CETRec effectively
enhances LLMs' awareness of both absolute order (how recently items were
interacted with) and relative order (the sequential relationships between
items).

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the limitation of large language models (LLMs) in capturing temporal information in sequential recommendations by proposing CETRec, a framework that uses causal inference and counterfactual reasoning to enhance LLMs' sensitivity to temporal order in user interactions.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based recommendation methods struggle to leverage temporal sequence order because their architecture lacks inherent sequence ordering and uses position embeddings designed for natural language, not user interactions. This impairs their ability to model evolving user preferences and predict future interests.
AI.method: The paper introduces CETRec, a framework grounded in causal inference that treats temporal order as an independent causal factor. It uses counterfactual reasoning to measure temporal impact by comparing recommendations with and without temporal information. CETRec employs a novel counterfactual tuning objective to enhance LLMs' awareness of absolute timestamps and relative ordering in user histories.
AI.result: CETRec improves LLMs' ability to capture both absolute and relative temporal orders in user interaction sequences, leading to more accurate sequential recommendations by directly optimizing temporal sensitivity through counterfactual tuning.
AI.conclusion: By framing temporal order as a causal factor and applying counterfactual tuning, CETRec effectively addresses the architectural limitations of LLMs in sequential recommendation, enhancing their capacity to model temporal dynamics in user preferences for better personalized recommendations.

title: Identification of Potentially Misclassified Crash Narratives using Machine Learning (ML) and Deep Learning (DL)
summary:
This research investigates the efficacy of machine learning (ML) and deep
learning (DL) methods in detecting misclassified intersection-related crashes
in police-reported narratives. Using 2019 crash data from the Iowa Department
of Transportation, we implemented and compared a comprehensive set of models,
including Support Vector Machine (SVM), XGBoost, BERT Sentence Embeddings, BERT
Word Embeddings, and Albert Model. Model performance was systematically
validated against expert reviews of potentially misclassified narratives,
providing a rigorous assessment of classification accuracy. Results
demonstrated that while traditional ML methods exhibited superior overall
performance compared to some DL approaches, the Albert Model achieved the
highest agreement with expert classifications (73% with Expert 1) and original
tabular data (58%). Statistical analysis revealed that the Albert Model
maintained performance levels similar to inter-expert consistency rates,
significantly outperforming other approaches, particularly on ambiguous
narratives. This work addresses a critical gap in transportation safety
research through multi-modal integration analysis, which achieved a 54.2%
reduction in error rates by combining narrative text with structured crash
data. We conclude that hybrid approaches combining automated classification
with targeted expert review offer a practical methodology for improving crash
data quality, with substantial implications for transportation safety
management and policy development.

AI.tldr: The study compares ML and DL models in detecting misclassified intersection crash reports, finding the Albert Model most accurate and effective, especially combining narrative and structured data.
AI.motivation: To improve the accuracy of police-reported intersection crash data by identifying misclassified cases using advanced ML and DL methods.
AI.method: Implemented and compared SVM, XGBoost, BERT embeddings, and Albert Model on 2019 Iowa crash data, validated against expert-reviewed classifications.
AI.result: Albert Model had highest alignment with expert labels (73%), matched inter-expert consistency, outperformed others on ambiguous cases, and multi-modal methods reduced error by 54.2%.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid automated classification combined with expert review enhances crash data quality, aiding transportation safety management and policy.

title: Large Language Models for Automating Clinical Data Standardization: HL7 FHIR Use Case
summary:
For years, semantic interoperability standards have sought to streamline the
exchange of clinical data, yet their deployment remains time-consuming,
resource-intensive, and technically challenging. To address this, we introduce
a semi-automated approach that leverages large language models specifically
GPT-4o and Llama 3.2 405b to convert structured clinical datasets into HL7 FHIR
format while assessing accuracy, reliability, and security. Applying our method
to the MIMIC-IV database, we combined embedding techniques, clustering
algorithms, and semantic retrieval to craft prompts that guide the models in
mapping each tabular field to its corresponding FHIR resource. In an initial
benchmark, resource identification achieved a perfect F1-score, with GPT-4o
outperforming Llama 3.2 thanks to the inclusion of FHIR resource schemas within
the prompt. Under real-world conditions, accuracy dipped slightly to 94 %, but
refinements to the prompting strategy restored robust mappings. Error analysis
revealed occasional hallucinations of non-existent attributes and mismatches in
granularity, which more detailed prompts can mitigate. Overall, our study
demonstrates the feasibility of context-aware, LLM-driven transformation of
clinical data into HL7 FHIR, laying the groundwork for semi-automated
interoperability workflows. Future work will focus on fine-tuning models with
specialized medical corpora, extending support to additional standards such as
HL7 CDA and OMOP, and developing an interactive interface to enable expert
validation and iterative refinement.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a semi-automated approach using GPT-4o and Llama 3.2 LLMs to convert clinical data into HL7 FHIR format, achieving high accuracy and highlighting the potential for LLM-driven interoperability.
AI.motivation: Semantic interoperability standards facilitate clinical data exchange but are often slow, costly, and technically difficult to implement.
AI.method: The authors used embedding, clustering, and semantic retrieval to build prompts guiding GPT-4o and Llama 3.2 models to map tabular clinical data from MIMIC-IV into FHIR resources.
AI.result: GPT-4o achieved perfect F1-score initially; real-world accuracy was about 94% after refinements. Errors included hallucinated attributes and granularity mismatches, which better prompts reduced.
AI.conclusion: LLM-based context-aware transformation of clinical data into HL7 FHIR is feasible, enabling semi-automated workflows. Future work will focus on fine-tuning, supporting more standards, and interactive validation tools.

title: ARF-RLHF: Adaptive Reward-Following for RLHF through Emotion-Driven Self-Supervision and Trace-Biased Dynamic Optimization
summary:
With the rapid advancement of Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback
(RLHF) and autoregressive transformers, state-of-the-art models such as
GPT-4.0, DeepSeek R1, and Llama 3.3 increasingly emphasize answer depth and
personalization. However, most existing RLHF approaches (e.g., PPO, DPO) still
rely on a binary-preference (BT) paradigm, which, while reducing annotation
costs, still requires substantial human effort and captures only group-level
tendencies rather than individual preferences. To overcome these limitations,
we propose Adaptive Reward-Following (ARF), a self-assessment framework that
leverages a high-precision emotion analyzer achieving over 70% accuracy on
GoEmotions, Sentiment140, and DailyDialog to convert free-form user feedback
into continuous preference scores. We further enrich and debias these signals
through lightweight data augmentations, including synonym replacement, random
trace truncation, and score bias annotation algorithm. A Dynamic Adapter
Preference Tracker continuously models evolving user tastes in real time,
enabling our novel Trace Bias (TB) fine-tuning algorithm to optimize directly
on these tracked rewards instead of coarse binary labels. Experiments on
Qwen-2/2.5, Gemma-2, and Llama-3.2 across four preference domains demonstrate
that ARF achieves an improvement of 3.3% over PPO and 7.6% over DPO. Moreover,
TB preserves theoretical alignment with PPO and DPO objectives. Overall, ARF
presents a scalable, personalized, and cost-effective approach to RLHF LLMs
through autonomous reward modeling.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Adaptive Reward-Following (ARF), a novel framework that converts free-form user feedback into continuous preference scores using an emotion analyzer, improving the personalization and effectiveness of Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) in large language models (LLMs).
AI.motivation: Current RLHF methods rely on binary preference labels that require substantial human effort and capture group-level rather than individual preferences, limiting model personalization and increasing annotation costs.
AI.method: ARF uses a high-precision emotion analyzer to interpret free-form feedback into continuous scores, employs data augmentation and bias correction techniques, dynamically tracks user preferences in real time, and optimizes model training using a Trace Bias fine-tuning algorithm that aligns with PPO and DPO objectives.
AI.result: Experiments on various LLMs and preference domains show ARF improves performance by 3.3% over PPO and 7.6% over DPO, demonstrating better personalization and reward modeling.
