AI.conclusion: ARF offers a scalable, personalized, and efficient alternative to traditional RLHF approaches by autonomously modeling rewards based on continuous user feedback, reducing human annotation efforts while enhancing model alignment to individual user tastes.

title: RLVER: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Emotion Rewards for Empathetic Agents
summary:
Large language models (LLMs) excel at logical and algorithmic reasoning, yet
their emotional intelligence (EQ) still lags far behind their cognitive
prowess. While reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) has
advanced in other domains, its application to dialogue-especially for emotional
intelligence-remains underexplored. In this work, we introduce RLVER, the first
end-to-end reinforcement learning framework that leverages verifiable emotion
rewards from simulated users to cultivate higher-order empathetic abilities in
LLMs. Within this framework, self-consistent affective simulated users engage
in dialogue rollouts and produce deterministic emotion scores during
conversations, serving as reward signals to guide the LLM's learning.
Fine-tuning publicly available Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct model with PPO boosts its
Sentient-Benchmark score from 13.3 to 79.2 while largely preserving
mathematical and coding competence. Extensive experiments reveal that: (i)
RLVER consistently improves multiple dialogue capabilities; (ii) Thinking and
non-thinking models show distinct trends--thinking models excel in empathy and
insight, while non-thinking models favor action; (iii) GRPO often yields stable
gains, while PPO can push certain capabilities to a higher ceiling; (iv) More
challenging environments are not always better-moderate ones can yield stronger
outcomes. Our results show that RLVER is a practical route toward emotionally
intelligent and broadly capable language agents.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces RLVER, an end-to-end reinforcement learning framework using verifiable emotion rewards from simulated users to enhance empathetic abilities in large language models (LLMs), significantly boosting emotional intelligence without sacrificing other skills.
AI.motivation: Although LLMs excel at logical reasoning, their emotional intelligence is much weaker. Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards has progressed in other fields but is underexplored for dialogue and emotional intelligence in LLMs.
AI.method: RLVER uses self-consistent affective simulated users that engage in dialogues and assign deterministic emotion scores, serving as reward signals for reinforcement learning. The Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct model is fine-tuned using PPO within this framework.
AI.result: The Sentient-Benchmark score improved from 13.3 to 79.2, with maintained mathematical and coding skills. Multiple dialogue capabilities improved, with thinking and non-thinking models showing different strengths. PPO and GRPO applied within RLVER provided stable gains under varying environment challenges.
AI.conclusion: RLVER is a practical and effective approach to developing emotionally intelligent and versatile language agents, demonstrating notable improvements in empathy and emotional reasoning in LLMs.

title: ReliableMath: Benchmark of Reliable Mathematical Reasoning on Large Language Models
summary:
Although demonstrating remarkable performance on reasoning tasks, Large
Language Models (LLMs) still tend to fabricate unreliable responses when
confronted with problems that are unsolvable or beyond their capability,
severely undermining the reliability. Prior studies of LLM reliability have
primarily focused on knowledge tasks to identify unanswerable questions, while
mathematical reasoning tasks have remained unexplored due to the dearth of
unsolvable math problems. To systematically investigate LLM reliability in
mathematical reasoning tasks, we formulate the reliability evaluation for both
solvable and unsolvable problems. We then develop a ReliableMath dataset which
incorporates open-source solvable problems and high-quality unsolvable problems
synthesized by our proposed construction workflow with human evaluations.
Experiments are conducted on various LLMs with several key findings uncovered.
LLMs fail to directly identify unsolvable problems and always generate
fabricated responses. When instructing LLMs to indicate unsolvability using a
reliable prompt, the reliability of larger-sized LLMs remains on solvable
problems, but notably improves on unsolvable problems yet still falls short of
solvable problems. However, small LLMs rarely show any progress despite
employing reliable prompts. Therefore, we further propose an alignment strategy
to enhance small LLMs' reliability, which can significantly improve LLM
reliability performances on both in-domain and out-of-domain tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates reliability of Large Language Models (LLMs) on mathematical reasoning, focusing on solvable and unsolvable problems. A new dataset, ReliableMath, including solvable and synthesized unsolvable problems, is introduced. Experiments reveal LLMs tend to fabricate answers on unsolvable problems but can improve reliability with prompting and alignment strategies, especially for larger models.
AI.motivation: To systematically evaluate and improve the reliability of LLMs in mathematical reasoning tasks, particularly regarding their tendency to fabricate responses for unsolvable problems, which has been understudied compared to knowledge tasks.
AI.method: Formulated reliability evaluation for solvable and unsolvable math problems; created the ReliableMath dataset combining open-source solvable and human-evaluated synthesized unsolvable problems; tested various LLMs with reliable prompting; proposed an alignment strategy for small LLMs to enhance reliability.
AI.result: LLMs fail to identify unsolvable problems without prompting and fabricate responses. Reliable prompts improve larger LLMs' reliability on unsolvable problems but small LLMs show little improvement. The alignment strategy significantly enhances small LLMs' reliability on both in-domain and out-of-domain tasks.
AI.conclusion: Prompting improves reliability in larger LLMs on unsolvable math problems, but small LLMs require alignment strategies for meaningful enhancements, indicating different approaches are needed based on model size to ensure reliable mathematical reasoning.

title: From Measurement to Mitigation: Exploring the Transferability of Debiasing Approaches to Gender Bias in Maltese Language Models
summary:
The advancement of Large Language Models (LLMs) has transformed Natural
Language Processing (NLP), enabling performance across diverse tasks with
little task-specific training. However, LLMs remain susceptible to social
biases, particularly reflecting harmful stereotypes from training data, which
can disproportionately affect marginalised communities. We measure gender bias
in Maltese LMs, arguing that such bias is harmful as it reinforces societal
stereotypes and fails to account for gender diversity, which is especially
problematic in gendered, low-resource languages. While bias evaluation and
mitigation efforts have progressed for English-centric models, research on
low-resourced and morphologically rich languages remains limited. This research
investigates the transferability of debiasing methods to Maltese language
models, focusing on BERTu and mBERTu, BERT-based monolingual and multilingual
models respectively. Bias measurement and mitigation techniques from English
are adapted to Maltese, using benchmarks such as CrowS-Pairs and SEAT,
alongside debiasing methods Counterfactual Data Augmentation, Dropout
Regularization, Auto-Debias, and GuiDebias. We also contribute to future work
in the study of gender bias in Maltese by creating evaluation datasets. Our
findings highlight the challenges of applying existing bias mitigation methods
to linguistically complex languages, underscoring the need for more inclusive
approaches in the development of multilingual NLP.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses gender bias in Maltese language models, adapting and evaluating English-based bias mitigation methods on BERTu and mBERTu models. It finds existing methods challenging to apply and highlights the need for more inclusive approaches for morphologically rich, low-resource languages.
AI.motivation: To tackle harmful gender biases in Maltese LMs which reinforce stereotypes and ignore gender diversity, especially in a gendered, low-resource language context where research is limited.
AI.method: Adapted gender bias benchmarks (CrowS-Pairs, SEAT) and debiasing techniques (Counterfactual Data Augmentation, Dropout Regularization, Auto-Debias, GuiDebias) from English to Maltese models BERTu and mBERTu. Created new evaluation datasets for Maltese.
AI.result: Debiasing methods from English present challenges in effectiveness when applied to Maltese due to linguistic complexity and resource limitations.
AI.conclusion: Existing bias mitigation strategies need to be reevaluated and developed further for low-resource, morphologically rich languages like Maltese to achieve true inclusivity in multilingual NLP.

title: Expert-level validation of AI-generated medical text with scalable language models
summary:
With the growing use of language models (LMs) in clinical environments, there
is an immediate need to evaluate the accuracy and safety of LM-generated
medical text. Currently, such evaluation relies solely on manual physician
review. However, detecting errors in LM-generated text is challenging because
1) manual review is costly and 2) expert-composed reference outputs are often
unavailable in real-world settings. While the "LM-as-judge" paradigm (a LM
evaluating another LM) offers scalable evaluation, even frontier LMs can miss
subtle but clinically significant errors. To address these challenges, we
propose MedVAL, a self-supervised framework that leverages synthetic data to
train evaluator LMs to assess whether LM-generated medical outputs are
factually consistent with inputs, without requiring physician labels or
reference outputs. To evaluate LM performance, we introduce MedVAL-Bench, a
dataset containing 840 outputs annotated by physicians, following a
physician-defined taxonomy of risk levels and error categories. Across 6
diverse medical tasks and 10 state-of-the-art LMs spanning open-source,
proprietary, and medically adapted models, MedVAL fine-tuning significantly
improves (p < 0.001) alignment with physicians on both seen and unseen tasks,
increasing average F1 scores from 66% to 83%, with per-sample safety
classification scores up to 86%. MedVAL improves the performance of even the
best-performing proprietary LM (GPT-4o) by 8%. To support a scalable,
risk-aware pathway towards clinical integration, we open-source the 1) codebase
( https://github.com/StanfordMIMI/MedVAL ), 2) MedVAL-Bench (
https://huggingface.co/datasets/stanfordmimi/MedVAL-Bench ), and 3) MedVAL-4B (
https://huggingface.co/stanfordmimi/MedVAL-4B ), the best-performing
open-source LM. Our research provides the first evidence of LMs approaching
expert-level validation ability for medical text.

AI.tldr: MedVAL is a self-supervised framework trained with synthetic data to evaluate the factual consistency and safety of medical text generated by language models without needing physician labels or reference outputs. It shows significant improvement in aligning with physician judgments across multiple medical tasks and models, including outperforming GPT-4o.
AI.motivation: The increasing deployment of language models in clinical settings requires accurate and safe evaluation of their generated medical text. Manual physician review is costly and reference outputs are often unavailable, making error detection challenging. Even advanced LMs used as evaluators can miss subtle clinical errors.
AI.method: MedVAL uses self-supervised learning with synthetic data to train evaluator language models to assess factual consistency of generated medical text against inputs without relying on physician labels or references. They developed MedVAL-Bench, a physician-annotated dataset across medical tasks, and fine-tuned various LMs including proprietary and open-source ones.
AI.result: MedVAL fine-tuning significantly improved alignment with physician evaluations, increasing average F1 scores from 66% to 83%, and safety classification accuracy up to 86%. It enhanced performance across 10 state-of-the-art LMs including a top proprietary model GPT-4o by 8%.
AI.conclusion: MedVAL demonstrates that language models can be trained to approach expert-level validation capability for medical text evaluation, providing a scalable, risk-aware approach for clinical integration. The authors open-sourced their code, datasets, and models to facilitate further research and application.

title: Adversarial Manipulation of Reasoning Models using Internal Representations
comment:
Accepted to the ICML 2025 Workshop on Reliable and Responsible
  Foundation Models (R2FM). 20 pages, 12 figures
summary:
Reasoning models generate chain-of-thought (CoT) tokens before their final
output, but how this affects their vulnerability to jailbreak attacks remains
unclear. While traditional language models make refusal decisions at the
prompt-response boundary, we find evidence that DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B
makes these decisions within its CoT generation. We identify a linear direction
in activation space during CoT token generation that predicts whether the model
will refuse or comply -- termed the "caution" direction because it corresponds
to cautious reasoning patterns in the generated text. Ablating this direction
from model activations increases harmful compliance, effectively jailbreaking
the model. We additionally show that intervening only on CoT token activations
suffices to control final outputs, and that incorporating this direction into
prompt-based attacks improves success rates. Our findings suggest that the
chain-of-thought itself is a promising new target for adversarial manipulation
in reasoning models.
  Code available at https://github.com/ky295/reasoning-manipulation

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how chain-of-thought (CoT) token generation influences the vulnerability of reasoning models to jailbreak attacks, identifying a "caution" direction in activation space that controls refusal behavior.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand how reasoning models make refusal decisions during CoT generation and how this affects their susceptibility to jailbreak attacks, aiming to find new adversarial manipulation targets.
AI.method: The authors analyze the activation space during CoT token generation in DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B, identify a linear "caution" direction predicting refusal or compliance, and perform ablation studies removing this direction to observe effects. They also test targeted interventions on CoT activations and incorporate this direction into prompt-based attacks.
AI.result: They find that removing the caution direction increases harmful compliance, effectively jailbreaking the model. Intervening only on CoT activations is sufficient to control the final output. Using the caution direction in attacks improves success rates.
AI.conclusion: The chain-of-thought generation process is a viable and effective target for adversarial manipulation to jailbreak reasoning models, revealing new vulnerabilities beyond traditional prompt-response boundaries.

title: How Much Content Do LLMs Generate That Induces Cognitive Bias in Users?
comment: 17 pages, 2 figures. to be submitted to AACL 2025
summary:
Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly integrated into applications
ranging from review summarization to medical diagnosis support, where they
affect human decisions. Even though LLMs perform well in many tasks, they may
also inherit societal or cognitive biases, which can inadvertently transfer to
humans. We investigate when and how LLMs expose users to biased content and
quantify its severity. Specifically, we assess three LLM families in
summarization and news fact-checking tasks, evaluating how much LLMs stay
consistent with their context and/or hallucinate. Our findings show that LLMs
expose users to content that changes the sentiment of the context in 21.86% of
the cases, hallucinates on post-knowledge-cutoff data questions in 57.33% of
the cases, and primacy bias in 5.94% of the cases. We evaluate 18 distinct
mitigation methods across three LLM families and find that targeted
interventions can be effective. Given the prevalent use of LLMs in high-stakes
domains, such as healthcare or legal analysis, our results highlight the need
for robust technical safeguards and for developing user-centered interventions
that address LLM limitations.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the exposure of users to biases in large language models (LLMs) during summarization and fact-checking tasks, quantifies bias severity, and evaluates mitigation strategies.
AI.motivation: LLMs are widely used in applications influencing human decisions but may inherit and transmit societal or cognitive biases, posing risks especially in high-stakes domains like healthcare.
AI.method: The authors assess three LLM families on summarization and news fact-checking tasks, measuring consistency with context, hallucinations, and bias manifestations, then evaluate 18 mitigation methods across these models.
AI.result: LLMs change context sentiment in 21.86% of cases, hallucinate post-knowledge-cutoff data questions 57.33% of the time, and show primacy bias in 5.94% of cases. Targeted mitigation methods proved effective.
AI.conclusion: Robust technical safeguards and user-centered interventions are necessary to address LLM limitations and prevent bias transmission in critical applications.

title: A Lie-algebraic perspective on Tree-Adjoining Grammars
comment:
14 pages, 7 figures. To appear in the proceedings of the 18th Meeting
  on the Mathematics of Language (MOL 2025)
summary:
We provide a novel mathematical implementation of tree-adjoining grammars
using two combinatorial definitions of graphs. With this lens, we demonstrate
that the adjoining operation defines a pre-Lie operation and subsequently forms
a Lie algebra. We demonstrate the utility of this perspective by showing how
one of our mathematical formulations of TAG captures properties of the TAG
system without needing to posit them as additional components of the system,
such as null-adjoining constraints and feature TAG.

AI.tldr: This paper redefines tree-adjoining grammars (TAG) using graph theory, showing the adjoining operation forms a Lie algebra, simplifying the expression of TAG properties.
AI.motivation: To establish a rigorous mathematical foundation for TAG that inherently captures TAG properties without extra assumptions like null-adjoining constraints.
AI.method: Two combinatorial graph definitions are used to represent TAG, revealing that the adjoining operation corresponds to a pre-Lie operation forming a Lie algebra.
AI.result: The new mathematical formulations capture inherent TAG system properties naturally, eliminating the need for additional components such as null-adjoining constraints and feature TAG.
AI.conclusion: The graph-based Lie algebraic framework provides a cleaner, more intrinsic characterization of TAG, advancing the theoretical understanding and utility of TAG systems.

title: KinyaColBERT: A Lexically Grounded Retrieval Model for Low-Resource Retrieval-Augmented Generation
summary:
The recent mainstream adoption of large language model (LLM) technology is
enabling novel applications in the form of chatbots and virtual assistants
across many domains. With the aim of grounding LLMs in trusted domains and
avoiding the problem of hallucinations, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG)
has emerged as a viable solution. In order to deploy sustainable RAG systems in
low-resource settings, achieving high retrieval accuracy is not only a
usability requirement but also a cost-saving strategy. Through empirical
evaluations on a Kinyarwanda-language dataset, we find that the most limiting
factors in achieving high retrieval accuracy are limited language coverage and
inadequate sub-word tokenization in pre-trained language models. We propose a
new retriever model, KinyaColBERT, which integrates two key concepts: late
word-level interactions between queries and documents, and a morphology-based
tokenization coupled with two-tier transformer encoding. This methodology
results in lexically grounded contextual embeddings that are both fine-grained
and self-contained. Our evaluation results indicate that KinyaColBERT
outperforms strong baselines and leading commercial text embedding APIs on a
Kinyarwanda agricultural retrieval benchmark. By adopting this retrieval
strategy, we believe that practitioners in other low-resource settings can not
only achieve reliable RAG systems but also deploy solutions that are more
cost-effective.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses improving retrieval accuracy in low-resource languages for retrieval-augmented generation systems by proposing KinyaColBERT, a retriever model with morphology-based tokenization and late word-level interactions, showing superior performance on a Kinyarwanda dataset.
AI.motivation: To develop sustainable and cost-effective RAG systems for low-resource languages, overcoming challenges of limited language coverage and inadequate tokenization in pretrained models that limit retrieval accuracy.
AI.method: They propose KinyaColBERT, which features morphology-based tokenization and two-tier transformer encoding with late word-level interactions between queries and documents to produce fine-grained, lexically grounded contextual embeddings.
AI.result: KinyaColBERT outperforms strong baseline models and leading commercial embedding APIs on an agricultural information retrieval benchmark in Kinyarwanda, demonstrating improved retrieval accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The proposed retrieval approach can enable reliable, cost-effective RAG deployments in other low-resource language domains by addressing key retrieval challenges and improving embedding quality.

title: RefineX: Learning to Refine Pre-training Data at Scale from Expert-Guided Programs
summary:
The foundational capabilities of large language models (LLMs) are deeply
influenced by the quality of their pre-training corpora. However, enhancing
data quality at scale remains a significant challenge, primarily due to the
trade-off between refinement effectiveness and processing efficiency. While
rule-based filtering remains the dominant paradigm, it typically operates at
the document level and lacks the granularity needed to refine specific content
within documents. Inspired by emerging work such as ProX, we propose
$\textbf{RefineX}$, a novel framework for large-scale, surgical refinement of
pre-training data through programmatic editing tasks. RefineX enables efficient
and fine-grained data refinement while reliably preserving the diversity and
naturalness of raw text. The core strength of RefineX lies in distilling
high-quality, expert-guided end-to-end refinement results into minimal
edit-based deletion programs. This high-precision distillation pipeline is used
to train an efficient and reliable refine model that can systematically improve
every instance in the corpus at scale. We evaluate RefineX across from-scratch
pre-training at multiple model scales and find that it consistently outperforms
models trained on raw, filtered, or alternatively refined data across diverse
downstream tasks. On the 750M model, RefineX yields 2.6%-7.2% average gains on
lighteval tasks, and achieves comparable performance using significantly fewer
training tokens. Further analysis shows that RefineX reliably enhances text
quality with both high efficiency and precision, outperforming prior approaches
such as end-to-end generation and Prox-C. These results position RefineX as a
scalable, effective, and reliable solution for optimizing pre-training data in
modern LLM pipelines.

AI.tldr: RefineX is a new framework for large-scale, precise refinement of pre-training data for large language models, improving training efficiency and model performance.
AI.motivation: Improving the quality of pre-training corpora is crucial for foundational capabilities of LLMs, but current methods struggle to efficiently refine data at a granular level without losing diversity and naturalness.
AI.method: RefineX uses programmatic editing tasks and distills expert-guided refinement into minimal edit-based deletion programs to train an efficient refine model that systematically enhances each data instance in the corpus.
AI.result: RefineX consistently outperforms raw, filtered, or other refined data in multiple model scales and downstream tasks, with 2.6%-7.2% gains on light evaluation tasks and comparable performance using fewer training tokens, surpassing prior approaches.
AI.conclusion: RefineX is a scalable, effective, and reliable method for fine-grained pre-training data refinement that improves text quality and training efficiency, making it valuable for modern LLM training pipelines.

title: GRAFT: A Graph-based Flow-aware Agentic Framework for Document-level Machine Translation
summary:
Document level Machine Translation (DocMT) approaches often struggle with
effectively capturing discourse level phenomena. Existing approaches rely on
heuristic rules to segment documents into discourse units, which rarely align
with the true discourse structure required for accurate translation. Otherwise,
they fail to maintain consistency throughout the document during translation.
To address these challenges, we propose Graph Augmented Agentic Framework for
Document Level Translation (GRAFT), a novel graph based DocMT system that
leverages Large Language Model (LLM) agents for document translation. Our
approach integrates segmentation, directed acyclic graph (DAG) based dependency
modelling, and discourse aware translation into a cohesive framework.
Experiments conducted across eight translation directions and six diverse
domains demonstrate that GRAFT achieves significant performance gains over
state of the art DocMT systems. Specifically, GRAFT delivers an average
improvement of 2.8 d BLEU on the TED test sets from IWSLT2017 over strong
baselines and 2.3 d BLEU for domain specific translation from English to
Chinese. Moreover, our analyses highlight the consistent ability of GRAFT to
address discourse level phenomena, yielding coherent and contextually accurate
translations.

AI.tldr: GRAFT is a novel document-level machine translation system using graph-based modeling and LLM agents to enhance discourse understanding, outperforming current methods across multiple languages and domains.
AI.motivation: Existing DocMT methods struggle with capturing true discourse structures and maintaining consistency throughout documents due to reliance on heuristic segmentation and lack of discourse awareness.
AI.method: GRAFT integrates document segmentation, directed acyclic graph (DAG) based dependency modeling, and discourse-aware translation within a unified framework, leveraging Large Language Model agents for translation.
AI.result: GRAFT achieves an average improvement of 2.8 d BLEU on TED test sets and 2.3 d BLEU for domain-specific English-to-Chinese translation, outperforming state-of-the-art DocMT systems across eight languages and six domains.
AI.conclusion: GRAFT effectively addresses discourse-level phenomena, delivering coherent and contextually accurate translations, marking a significant advance in document-level machine translation.

title: Read Quietly, Think Aloud: Decoupling Comprehension and Reasoning in LLMs
summary:
Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable proficiency in
understanding text and generating high-quality responses. However, a critical
distinction from human cognition is their typical lack of a distinct internal
`reading' or deliberation phase before `speaking' (i.e., generating text).
Humans often engage in silent reading to comprehend context and formulate
thoughts prior to articulation. This paper investigates methods to imbue LLMs
with a similar capacity for internal processing.
  We introduce and evaluate techniques that encourage LLMs to `read silently.'
Our findings indicate that even a straightforward approach, such as providing
the model with an initial contextual prompt or `reading space' before it begins
predicting subsequent tokens for the final output, can yield significant
performance improvements. We further enhance this concept by developing a
`reading buddy' architecture, where an auxiliary component silently processes
the input and provides refined contextual insights to the primary generation
model. These approaches aim to foster deeper understanding from LLMs so that
they can produce better reasoned responses, moving them one step closer to more
human-like text processing. Our results indicate that these simple techniques
can provide surprisingly strong impact on accuracy with multiple point accuracy
boost.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes methods to enable Large Language Models to internally process or 'read silently' before generating text, improving their comprehension and output quality.
AI.motivation: LLMs typically generate responses without an internal deliberation phase, unlike humans who silently read and comprehend before speaking, which limits their reasoning ability.
AI.method: Introducing initial contextual prompts as a 'reading space' and developing a 'reading buddy' architecture that processes input silently to supply refined context to the main model.
AI.result: These techniques significantly improve performance, yielding multiple point accuracy boosts by fostering deeper understanding in LLMs.
AI.conclusion: Simple modifications enabling internal reading phases can substantially enhance LLM accuracy and produce more human-like, reasoned responses.

title: SHNU Multilingual Conversational Speech Recognition System for INTERSPEECH 2025 MLC-SLM Challenge
summary:
This paper describes SHNU multilingual conversational speech recognition
system (SHNU-mASR, team name-"maybe"), submitted to Track 1 of the INTERSPEECH
2025 MLC-SLM Challenge. Our system integrates a parallel-speech-encoder
architecture with a large language model (LLM) to form a unified multilingual
ASR framework. The parallel-speech-encoder consists of two pre-trained
encoders, the Whisper-large-v3 encoder and mHuBERT-147 encoder. Their output
embeddings are concatenated and fed into the LLM, enabling the model to
leverage complementary acoustic and linguistic knowledge and achieve
competitive performance. Moreover, we adopt a tri-stage training strategy to
jointly update the low-rank adaptation modules and projector parameters of both
the speech encoders and the LLM. In addition, we incorporate an additional
language-aware prompt at the LLM input to enhance language-specific text
generation. The SHNU-mASR system achieves an overall character/word error rate
(CER/WER) of 11.76% on the blind evaluation set of the challenge, outperforming
the official MLC-SLM baseline by 8.41 absolute CER/WER, without increasing the
baseline training data.

AI.tldr: SHNU-mASR is a multilingual speech recognition system combining two pre-trained speech encoders and a large language model with a multi-stage training strategy, achieving superior error rates on the INTERSPEECH 2025 MLC-SLM Challenge.
AI.motivation: To create a unified multilingual ASR system that leverages complementary acoustic and linguistic features for better recognition performance.
AI.method: Uses a parallel-speech-encoder combining Whisper-large-v3 and mHuBERT-147 encoders, concatenates their embeddings, feeds them into a large language model, applies tri-stage training updating low-rank adaptation modules, and adds language-aware prompts to the LLM input.
AI.result: The system achieved an 11.76% CER/WER on the blind evaluation, outperforming the official baseline by 8.41 percentage points without additional training data.
AI.conclusion: Integrating parallel speech encoders with LLMs and applying specialized training strategies effectively improves multilingual ASR performance beyond established baselines.

title: Backtesting Sentiment Signals for Trading: Evaluating the Viability of Alpha Generation from Sentiment Analysis
comment:
Actes de CORIA-TALN-RJCRI-RECITAL 2025 (Association pour le
  Traitement Automatique des Langues)
summary:
Sentiment analysis, widely used in product reviews, also impacts financial
markets by influencing asset prices through microblogs and news articles.
Despite research in sentiment-driven finance, many studies focus on
sentence-level classification, overlooking its practical application in
trading. This study bridges that gap by evaluating sentiment-based trading
strategies for generating positive alpha. We conduct a backtesting analysis
using sentiment predictions from three models (two classification and one
regression) applied to news articles on Dow Jones 30 stocks, comparing them to
the benchmark Buy&Hold strategy. Results show all models produced positive
returns, with the regression model achieving the highest return of 50.63% over
28 months, outperforming the benchmark Buy&Hold strategy. This highlights the
potential of sentiment in enhancing investment strategies and financial
decision-making.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates the effectiveness of sentiment-based trading strategies using sentiment predictions from machine learning models on news articles for Dow Jones 30 stocks, showing that they outperform traditional Buy&Hold strategies.
AI.motivation: Existing studies on sentiment-driven finance often focus on sentence-level sentiment classification without considering real-world trading applications; this paper aims to bridge that gap by assessing trading strategies based on sentiment analysis.
AI.method: Backtesting analysis using sentiment predictions from two classification models and one regression model applied to Dow Jones 30 stock news articles, comparing these strategies against a Buy&Hold benchmark over 28 months.
AI.result: All sentiment-based models generated positive returns, with the regression model achieving the highest return of 50.63%, outperforming the benchmark Buy&Hold strategy.
AI.conclusion: Sentiment analysis of financial news can significantly enhance investment strategies and improve financial decision-making beyond traditional trading approaches.

title: WETBench: A Benchmark for Detecting Task-Specific Machine-Generated Text on Wikipedia
summary:
Given Wikipedia's role as a trusted source of high-quality, reliable content,
concerns are growing about the proliferation of low-quality machine-generated
text (MGT) produced by large language models (LLMs) on its platform. Reliable
detection of MGT is therefore essential. However, existing work primarily
evaluates MGT detectors on generic generation tasks rather than on tasks more
commonly performed by Wikipedia editors. This misalignment can lead to poor
generalisability when applied in real-world Wikipedia contexts. We introduce
WETBench, a multilingual, multi-generator, and task-specific benchmark for MGT
detection. We define three editing tasks, empirically grounded in Wikipedia
editors' perceived use cases for LLM-assisted editing: Paragraph Writing,
Summarisation, and Text Style Transfer, which we implement using two new
datasets across three languages. For each writing task, we evaluate three
prompts, generate MGT across multiple generators using the best-performing
prompt, and benchmark diverse detectors. We find that, across settings,
training-based detectors achieve an average accuracy of 78%, while zero-shot
detectors average 58%. These results show that detectors struggle with MGT in
realistic generation scenarios and underscore the importance of evaluating such
models on diverse, task-specific data to assess their reliability in
editor-driven contexts.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces WETBench, a multilingual and task-specific benchmark for detecting machine-generated text (MGT) on Wikipedia, focusing on realistic editor tasks. It evaluates detectors and finds training-based detectors perform better than zero-shot ones, highlighting detection challenges in real Wikipedia contexts.
AI.motivation: To address the gap in reliable detection of machine-generated text on Wikipedia, which currently lacks evaluation aligned with real Wikipedia editing tasks, risking poor generalizability of detectors.
AI.method: WETBench defines three Wikipedia-related editing tasks (Paragraph Writing, Summarisation, Text Style Transfer) and creates two new datasets in three languages. It uses multiple prompts and generators to produce MGT and benchmarks diverse detection models on these realistic tasks.
AI.result: Training-based detectors achieve around 78% accuracy, while zero-shot detectors average 58%, showing difficulty in detecting machine-generated text in realistic Wikipedia-relevant scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Detection of machine-generated text in Wikipedia editing contexts remains challenging; evaluating detectors on task-specific, multilingual, and realistic data is crucial for assessing and improving their reliability in real-world Wikipedia editing environments.

title: Making Sense of Korean Sentences: A Comprehensive Evaluation of LLMs through KoSEnd Dataset
summary:
Although LLMs have made significant progress in various languages, there are
still concerns about their effectiveness with low-resource agglutinative
languages compared to languages such as English. In this study, we focused on
Korean, a language known for its complex sentence endings, and evaluated LLMs
on this challenging aspect. We introduce the Korean Sentence Endings (KoSEnd)
dataset, which includes 3,000 sentences, each annotated for the naturalness of
15 sentence ending forms. These were collected from diverse sources to cover a
range of contexts. We evaluated 11 LLMs to assess their understanding of Korean
sentence endings, analyzing them based on parameter count and prediction
consistency. Notably, we found that informing models about the possibility of
missing sentence endings improved performance, highlighting the impact of
explicitly considering certain linguistic features.

AI.tldr: The study evaluates large language models (LLMs) on the complex Korean sentence endings using a new dataset, KoSEnd, finding that models informed about missing endings perform better.
AI.motivation: To address concerns about LLMs' effectiveness on low-resource agglutinative languages like Korean, focusing on their handling of complex sentence endings.
AI.method: Created KoSEnd dataset with 3,000 annotated sentences covering 15 Korean sentence endings; evaluated 11 LLMs based on parameter count and prediction consistency; tested impact of explicitly informing models about missing sentence endings.
AI.result: Models improved in performance when informed about the possibility of missing sentence endings, indicating that including explicit linguistic features aids understanding.
AI.conclusion: Explicit consideration of complex linguistic features such as missing sentence endings enhances LLMs' handling of Korean sentence endings, suggesting avenues to improve language model performance on agglutinative languages.

title: Graph Repairs with Large Language Models: An Empirical Study
summary:
Property graphs are widely used in domains such as healthcare, finance, and
social networks, but they often contain errors due to inconsistencies, missing
data, or schema violations. Traditional rule-based and heuristic-driven graph
repair methods are limited in their adaptability as they need to be tailored
for each dataset. On the other hand, interactive human-in-the-loop approaches
may become infeasible when dealing with large graphs, as the cost--both in
terms of time and effort--of involving users becomes too high. Recent
advancements in Large Language Models (LLMs) present new opportunities for
automated graph repair by leveraging contextual reasoning and their access to
real-world knowledge. We evaluate the effectiveness of six open-source LLMs in
repairing property graphs. We assess repair quality, computational cost, and
model-specific performance. Our experiments show that LLMs have the potential
to detect and correct errors, with varying degrees of accuracy and efficiency.
We discuss the strengths, limitations, and challenges of LLM-driven graph
repair and outline future research directions for improving scalability and
interpretability.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates the use of large language models (LLMs) for repairing errors in property graphs, showing their potential in error detection and correction with varying accuracy and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Traditional graph repair methods are either too dataset-specific or costly for large graphs, motivating the exploration of LLMs for automated graph repair leveraging contextual reasoning and real-world knowledge.
AI.method: They evaluated six open-source LLMs on their ability to detect and correct errors in property graphs, assessing repair quality, computational cost, and model-specific performance.
AI.result: LLMs demonstrated potential to identify and fix graph errors, but with varying levels of accuracy and efficiency across models.
AI.conclusion: LLM-driven graph repair shows promise but faces challenges related to scalability and interpretability; future work is needed to address these and improve practical usability.

title: SMCLM: Semantically Meaningful Causal Language Modeling for Autoregressive Paraphrase Generation
summary:
This article introduces semantically meaningful causal language modeling
(SMCLM), a selfsupervised method of training autoregressive models to generate
semantically equivalent text. Our approach involves using semantically
meaningful text representation as an initial embedding in the autoregressive
training and generation processes. The extensive empirical study demonstrates
that the SMCLM approach makes autoregressive models capable of learning robust
and high-quality paraphrase generation. The proposed method is competitive with
the supervised method and achieves state-of-the-art results in unsupervised
approaches. This article also presents a comprehensive set of automatic metrics
that cover a wide range of autogenerated paraphrase evaluation aspects.
Simultaneously, this article highlights the low reliability of the metrics that
are widely used in paraphrase generation evaluation, including BLEU, ROUGE, and
BERTScore.

AI.tldr: This paper presents SMCLM, a self-supervised training method for autoregressive models to generate semantically equivalent text, achieving state-of-the-art results in unsupervised paraphrase generation and revealing issues with common evaluation metrics.
AI.motivation: To develop a self-supervised method enabling autoregressive models to generate semantically equivalent (paraphrased) text robustly and improve unsupervised paraphrase generation quality.
AI.method: Using semantically meaningful text representations as initial embeddings in autoregressive training and generation to guide models in generating paraphrases without supervision.
AI.result: SMCLM achieves competitive and state-of-the-art results in unsupervised paraphrase generation, matching supervised methods, and comprehensive evaluation reveals low reliability of widely used metrics like BLEU, ROUGE, and BERTScore.
AI.conclusion: SMCLM is an effective self-supervised approach for paraphrase generation, outperforming existing unsupervised methods, and calls for more reliable evaluation metrics in paraphrasing tasks.

title: Improving Social Determinants of Health Documentation in French EHRs Using Large Language Models
summary:
Social determinants of health (SDoH) significantly influence health outcomes,
shaping disease progression, treatment adherence, and health disparities.
However, their documentation in structured electronic health records (EHRs) is
often incomplete or missing. This study presents an approach based on large
language models (LLMs) for extracting 13 SDoH categories from French clinical
notes. We trained Flan-T5-Large on annotated social history sections from
clinical notes at Nantes University Hospital, France. We evaluated the model at
two levels: (i) identification of SDoH categories and associated values, and
(ii) extraction of detailed SDoH with associated temporal and quantitative
information. The model performance was assessed across four datasets, including
two that we publicly release as open resources. The model achieved strong
performance for identifying well-documented categories such as living
condition, marital status, descendants, job, tobacco, and alcohol use (F1 score
> 0.80). Performance was lower for categories with limited training data or
highly variable expressions, such as employment status, housing, physical
activity, income, and education. Our model identified 95.8% of patients with at
least one SDoH, compared to 2.8% for ICD-10 codes from structured EHR data. Our
error analysis showed that performance limitations were linked to annotation
inconsistencies, reliance on English-centric tokenizer, and reduced
generalizability due to the model being trained on social history sections
only. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of NLP in improving the
completeness of real-world SDoH data in a non-English EHR system.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a method using Flan-T5-Large, a large language model, to extract 13 social determinants of health (SDoH) categories from French clinical notes, demonstrating improved identification compared to structured EHR data.
AI.motivation: Social determinants of health are crucial for understanding health outcomes but are often under-documented in structured electronic health records, especially in non-English contexts.
AI.method: The study trained Flan-T5-Large on annotated social history sections from Nantes University Hospital clinical notes and evaluated its performance on identifying and extracting detailed SDoH across multiple datasets.
AI.result: The model showed strong performance (F1 > 0.80) for well-documented categories and identified 95.8% of patients with at least one SDoH versus 2.8% with ICD-10 codes. Lower performance was noted for categories with limited data or variable expressions.
AI.conclusion: NLP using large language models is effective in enhancing the completeness of SDoH data in non-English EHR systems, although challenges remain due to annotation inconsistencies, tokenizer limitations, and training data scope.

title: Beyond Weaponization: NLP Security for Medium and Lower-Resourced Languages in Their Own Right
summary:
Despite mounting evidence that multilinguality can be easily weaponized
against language models (LMs), works across NLP Security remain overwhelmingly
English-centric. In terms of securing LMs, the NLP norm of "English first"
collides with standard procedure in cybersecurity, whereby practitioners are
expected to anticipate and prepare for worst-case outcomes. To mitigate
worst-case outcomes in NLP Security, researchers must be willing to engage with
the weakest links in LM security: lower-resourced languages. Accordingly, this
work examines the security of LMs for lower- and medium-resourced languages. We
extend existing adversarial attacks for up to 70 languages to evaluate the
security of monolingual and multilingual LMs for these languages. Through our
analysis, we find that monolingual models are often too small in total number
of parameters to ensure sound security, and that while multilinguality is
helpful, it does not always guarantee improved security either. Ultimately,
these findings highlight important considerations for more secure deployment of
LMs, for communities of lower-resourced languages.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the security vulnerabilities of language models (LMs) in lower- and medium-resourced languages, highlighting that current research is too English-centric and that multilingual models don't always provide better security.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the gap in NLP Security research which largely ignores lower-resourced languages and to anticipate worst-case security issues for more robust LM deployment globally.
AI.method: The authors extend adversarial attacks to 70 languages and evaluate the security of both monolingual and multilingual LMs on lower- and medium-resourced languages.
AI.result: Monolingual models tend to be too small to ensure robust security, and multilingual models, while somewhat helpful, do not always guarantee improved security for these languages.
AI.conclusion: The study emphasizes the need to focus on lower-resourced languages for LM security, challenging the 'English first' norm, and calls for more careful consideration in deploying secure LMs in diverse linguistic communities.

title: BMMR: A Large-Scale Bilingual Multimodal Multi-Discipline Reasoning Dataset
summary:
In this paper, we introduce BMMR, a large-scale bilingual, multimodal,
multi-disciplinary reasoning dataset for the community to develop and evaluate
large multimodal models (LMMs). BMMR comprises 110k college-level questions
spanning 300 UNESCO-defined subjects, spanning diverse formats-multiple-choice,
fill-in-the-blank, and open-ended QA-and sourced from both print and digital
media such as books, exams, and quizzes. All data are curated and filtered via
a human-in-the-loop and scalable framework, and each instance is paired with a
high-quality reasoning path. The dataset is organized into two parts: BMMR-Eval
that comprises 20,458 high-quality instances to comprehensively assess LMMs'
knowledge and reasoning across multiple disciplines in both Chinese and
English; and BMMR-Train that contains 88,991 instances to support further
research and development, extending the current focus on mathematical reasoning
to diverse disciplines and domains. In addition, we propose the process-based
multi-discipline verifier (i.e., BMMR-Verifier) for accurate and fine-grained
evaluation of reasoning paths. Extensive experiments on 24 models reveal that
(i) even SOTA models (e.g., o3 and Gemini-2.5-Pro) leave substantial headroom
on BMMR-Eval; (ii) reasoning models exhibit discipline bias and outperform LMMs
only on specific subjects; (iii) open-source models still trail their
proprietary counterparts; and (iv) fine-tuning on BMMR-Train narrows this gap.
Additionally, we conduct reasoning-chain analyses using BMMR-Verifier and other
in-depth studies, uncovering the challenges LMMs currently face in
multidisciplinary reasoning. We will release the data, and we hope our work can
offer insights and contributions to the community.

AI.tldr: BMMR is a large bilingual multimodal dataset with 110k college-level questions across 300 subjects, designed to evaluate large multimodal models (LMMs) in reasoning and knowledge.
AI.motivation: To create a comprehensive, high-quality dataset for developing and assessing LMMs' reasoning abilities across diverse subjects and formats beyond just mathematical reasoning.
AI.method: Collected 110k questions in multiple formats from varied sources, curated via human-in-the-loop processes, paired each with reasoning paths, split into training and evaluation sets, and proposed a multi-discipline verifier for fine-grained reasoning evaluation.
AI.result: Experiments on 24 models show substantial room for improvement, reveal discipline bias favoring specialized reasoning models, show proprietary models outperform open-source ones, and that fine-tuning on BMMR-Train improves performance.
AI.conclusion: BMMR provides a rich benchmark and toolset that exposes current challenges in multidisciplinary reasoning for LMMs, aiming to support future research and development in large multimodal reasoning models.

title: Four Shades of Life Sciences: A Dataset for Disinformation Detection in the Life Sciences
summary:
Disseminators of disinformation often seek to attract attention or evoke
emotions - typically to gain influence or generate revenue - resulting in
distinctive rhetorical patterns that can be exploited by machine learning
models. In this study, we explore linguistic and rhetorical features as proxies
for distinguishing disinformative texts from other health and life-science text
genres, applying both large language models and classical machine learning
classifiers. Given the limitations of existing datasets, which mainly focus on
fact checking misinformation, we introduce Four Shades of Life Sciences
(FSoLS): a novel, labeled corpus of 2,603 texts on 14 life-science topics,
retrieved from 17 diverse sources and classified into four categories of life
science publications. The source code for replicating, and updating the dataset
is available on GitHub:
https://github.com/EvaSeidlmayer/FourShadesofLifeSciences

AI.tldr: This paper presents Four Shades of Life Sciences (FSoLS), a new labeled corpus of over 2,600 texts across 14 life-science topics from diverse sources, categorized into four types of life science publications. It investigates distinctive linguistic and rhetorical features to detect disinformation in health texts using machine learning methods, including large language models.
AI.motivation: Disinformation often uses distinctive rhetorical patterns to manipulate attention or emotions for influence or profit, but existing datasets focus mostly on fact checking and lack diversity in life-science text genres. This motivated the creation of a more comprehensive, labeled dataset to improve detection methods.
AI.method: The study introduces FSoLS, a corpus of 2,603 labeled texts from 14 topics and 17 sources, classified into four categories. It applies both large language models and classical machine learning classifiers to analyze linguistic and rhetorical features for identifying disinformative texts in life sciences.
AI.result: The new dataset enables the exploration of linguistic proxies to distinguish disinformation from other health and life-science genres. Machine learning models leveraging these features show promise in detecting disinformation more effectively than previous fact-checking-focused datasets.
AI.conclusion: Creating and using a diverse, labeled corpus like FSoLS enhances the ability to detect disinformation in health and life-science publications by exploiting rhetorical and linguistic patterns, supporting more robust machine learning approaches.

title: AI-VaxGuide: An Agentic RAG-Based LLM for Vaccination Decisions
summary:
Vaccination plays a vital role in global public health, yet healthcare
professionals often struggle to access immunization guidelines quickly and
efficiently. National protocols and WHO recommendations are typically extensive
and complex, making it difficult to extract precise information, especially
during urgent situations. This project tackles that issue by developing a
multilingual, intelligent question-answering system that transforms static
vaccination guidelines into an interactive and user-friendly knowledge base.
Built on a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) framework and enhanced with
agent-based reasoning (Agentic RAG), the system provides accurate,
context-sensitive answers to complex medical queries. Evaluation shows that
Agentic RAG outperforms traditional methods, particularly in addressing
multi-step or ambiguous questions. To support clinical use, the system is
integrated into a mobile application designed for real-time, point-of-care
access to essential vaccine information. AI-VaxGuide model is publicly
available on https://huggingface.co/VaxGuide

AI.tldr: The paper presents AI-VaxGuide, a multilingual, intelligent question-answering system for vaccination guidelines that enhances healthcare professionals' access to immunization info via a Retrieval-Augmented Generation framework with agent-based reasoning.
AI.motivation: Healthcare professionals find it challenging to quickly access and extract precise immunization guidelines from extensive and complex national and WHO protocols, especially during urgent situations.
AI.method: The system uses a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) framework enhanced with agent-based reasoning (Agentic RAG) to convert static vaccination guidelines into an interactive, context-sensitive knowledge base for answering complex medical queries.
AI.result: Agentic RAG system outperforms traditional methods in accuracy, especially for multi-step or ambiguous questions, and is integrated into a mobile app for real-time clinical use.
AI.conclusion: Transforming vaccination guidelines into an interactive, intelligent QA system improves accessibility and usability of immunization information for healthcare professionals, supporting better clinical decision-making at the point of care.

title: H2HTalk: Evaluating Large Language Models as Emotional Companion
summary:
As digital emotional support needs grow, Large Language Model companions
offer promising authentic, always-available empathy, though rigorous evaluation
lags behind model advancement. We present Heart-to-Heart Talk (H2HTalk), a
benchmark assessing companions across personality development and empathetic
interaction, balancing emotional intelligence with linguistic fluency. H2HTalk
features 4,650 curated scenarios spanning dialogue, recollection, and itinerary
planning that mirror real-world support conversations, substantially exceeding
previous datasets in scale and diversity. We incorporate a Secure Attachment
Persona (SAP) module implementing attachment-theory principles for safer
interactions. Benchmarking 50 LLMs with our unified protocol reveals that
long-horizon planning and memory retention remain key challenges, with models
struggling when user needs are implicit or evolve mid-conversation. H2HTalk
establishes the first comprehensive benchmark for emotionally intelligent
companions. We release all materials to advance development of LLMs capable of
providing meaningful and safe psychological support.

AI.tldr: H2HTalk is a new benchmark evaluating Large Language Model companions on emotional intelligence and linguistic fluency through realistic scenarios, highlighting current limitations in long-term planning and memory retention.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of rigorous evaluation frameworks for LLM companions providing authentic, emotionally supportive interactions.
AI.method: Developed H2HTalk with 4,650 curated scenarios covering dialogue, recollection, and planning, plus a Secure Attachment Persona module based on attachment theory; benchmarked 50 LLMs using a unified evaluation protocol.
AI.result: Findings show that LLM companions struggle particularly with long-horizon planning and adapting to implicit or evolving user needs during conversations.
AI.conclusion: H2HTalk sets a comprehensive standard for assessing emotionally intelligent AI companions, facilitating future advancements in safe and meaningful psychological support via LLMs.

title: Articulatory clarity and variability before and after surgery for tongue cancer
summary:
Surgical treatment for tongue cancer can negatively affect the mobility and
musculature of the tongue, which can influence articulatory clarity and
variability. In this study, we investigated articulatory clarity through the
vowel articulation index (VAI) and variability through vowel formant dispersion
(VFD). Using a sentence reading task, we assessed 11 individuals pre and six
months post tongue cancer surgery, alongside 11 sex- and age matched typical
speakers. Our results show that while the VAI was significantly smaller
post-surgery compared to pre-surgery, there was no significant difference
between patients and typical speakers at either time point. Post-surgery,
speakers had higher VFD values for /i/ compared to pre-surgery and typical
speakers, signalling higher variability. Taken together, our results suggest
that while articulatory clarity remained within typical ranges following
surgery for tongue cancer for the speakers in our study, articulatory
variability increased.

AI.tldr: Study on tongue cancer surgery effects shows reduced vowel articulation clarity but increased variability post-surgery, with clarity remaining within typical ranges.
AI.motivation: To investigate how tongue cancer surgery impacts articulatory clarity and variability.
AI.method: Assessed vowel articulation index (VAI) and vowel formant dispersion (VFD) in 11 patients pre- and post-surgery and compared with 11 matched typical speakers using a sentence reading task.
AI.result: VAI decreased significantly post-surgery but remained within typical ranges; VFD increased for vowel /i/ post-surgery indicating higher variability.
AI.conclusion: Surgical treatment reduced articulatory clarity but did not push it outside typical range, while increasing articulatory variability in patients after tongue cancer surgery.

title: Learning to Translate Ambiguous Terminology by Preference Optimization on Post-Edits
summary:
In real world translation scenarios, terminology is rarely one-to-one.
Instead, multiple valid translations may appear in a terminology dictionary,
but correctness of a translation depends on corporate style guides and context.
This can be challenging for neural machine translation (NMT) systems. Luckily,
in a corporate context, many examples of human post-edits of valid but
incorrect terminology exist. The goal of this work is to learn how to
disambiguate our terminology based on these corrections. Our approach is based
on preference optimization, using the term post-edit as the knowledge to be
preferred. While previous work had to rely on unambiguous translation
dictionaries to set hard constraints during decoding, or to add soft
constraints in the input, our framework requires neither one-to-one
dictionaries nor human intervention at decoding time. We report results on
English-German post-edited data and find that the optimal combination of
supervised fine-tuning and preference optimization, with both term-specific and
full sequence objectives, yields statistically significant improvements in term
accuracy over a strong NMT baseline without significant losses in COMET score.
Additionally, we release test sets from our post-edited data and terminology
dictionary.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses terminology disambiguation in neural machine translation using preference optimization based on human post-edits, improving term accuracy without decoding-time constraints.
AI.motivation: Terminology translations are often ambiguous and depend on style guides and context, posing challenges for NMT systems; human post-edits provide valuable disambiguation information.
AI.method: The authors use preference optimization with term post-edits to learn terminology disambiguation, combining supervised fine-tuning and preference optimization with term-specific and full sequence objectives, avoiding reliance on one-to-one dictionaries or decoding-time constraints.
AI.result: Their approach achieves statistically significant improvements in term accuracy on English-German post-edited data over a strong NMT baseline without significant loss in overall translation quality (COMET score). They also release related test sets and terminology resources.
AI.conclusion: Preference optimization leveraging human post-edits effectively improves terminology accuracy in NMT without sacrificing overall performance and without requiring hard constraints or human intervention during decoding.

title: Multi-Hop Reasoning for Question Answering with Hyperbolic Representations
summary:
Hyperbolic representations are effective in modeling knowledge graph data
which is prevalently used to facilitate multi-hop reasoning. However, a
rigorous and detailed comparison of the two spaces for this task is lacking. In
this paper, through a simple integration of hyperbolic representations with an
encoder-decoder model, we perform a controlled and comprehensive set of
experiments to compare the capacity of hyperbolic space versus Euclidean space
in multi-hop reasoning. Our results show that the former consistently
outperforms the latter across a diverse set of datasets. In addition, through
an ablation study, we show that a learnable curvature initialized with the
delta hyperbolicity of the utilized data yields superior results to random
initializations. Furthermore, our findings suggest that hyperbolic
representations can be significantly more advantageous when the datasets
exhibit a more hierarchical structure.

AI.tldr: The paper compares hyperbolic and Euclidean representations for multi-hop reasoning in knowledge graphs, showing hyperbolic consistently performs better, especially with hierarchical data.
AI.motivation: To rigorously compare the effectiveness of hyperbolic versus Euclidean spaces in multi-hop reasoning on knowledge graph data—an area lacking detailed comparison.
AI.method: Integrated hyperbolic representations into an encoder-decoder model and conducted controlled experiments across multiple datasets, including ablation studies on curvature initialization.
AI.result: Hyperbolic space outperformed Euclidean space consistently; learnable curvature initialized with delta hyperbolicity improved results; hyperbolic advantage was stronger with hierarchical data.
AI.conclusion: Hyperbolic representations are superior for multi-hop reasoning in knowledge graphs, particularly for datasets with hierarchical structure, underscoring their practical value over Euclidean representations.

title: EMERGE: A Benchmark for Updating Knowledge Graphs with Emerging Textual Knowledge
summary:
Knowledge Graphs (KGs) are structured knowledge repositories containing
entities and relations between them. In this paper, we investigate the problem
of automatically updating KGs over time with respect to the evolution of
knowledge in unstructured textual sources. This problem requires identifying a
wide range of update operations based on the state of an existing KG at a
specific point in time. This contrasts with traditional information extraction
pipelines, which extract knowledge from text independently of the current state
of a KG. To address this challenge, we propose a method for lifelong
construction of a dataset consisting of Wikidata KG snapshots over time and
Wikipedia passages paired with the corresponding edit operations that they
induce in a particular KG snapshot. The resulting dataset comprises 376K
Wikipedia passages aligned with a total of 1.25M KG edits over 10 different
snapshots of Wikidata from 2019 to 2025. Our experimental results highlight
challenges in updating KG snapshots based on emerging textual knowledge,
positioning the dataset as a valuable benchmark for future research. We will
publicly release our dataset and model implementations.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses automatic updating of Knowledge Graphs (KGs) by linking Wikipedia text passages with corresponding KG edits over time, providing a new dataset and benchmark for this task.
AI.motivation: Traditional information extraction methods do not consider the current state of the KG, limiting their ability to update KGs accurately as knowledge evolves in unstructured texts.
AI.method: They constructed a lifelong dataset of Wikidata KG snapshots paired with Wikipedia passages that induce specific KG edit operations, covering 376K passages and 1.25M edits over 10 snapshots from 2019 to 2025.
AI.result: The experimental results demonstrate the challenges involved in updating KG snapshots from evolving textual knowledge and establish the dataset as a useful benchmark for future studies.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a significant resource for advancing KG updating methods and will publicly release the dataset and models to encourage further research in this area.

title: Improving Low-Resource Dialect Classification Using Retrieval-based Voice Conversion
summary:
Deep learning models for dialect identification are often limited by the
scarcity of dialectal data. To address this challenge, we propose to use
Retrieval-based Voice Conversion (RVC) as an effective data augmentation method
for a low-resource German dialect classification task. By converting audio
samples to a uniform target speaker, RVC minimizes speaker-related variability,
enabling models to focus on dialect-specific linguistic and phonetic features.
Our experiments demonstrate that RVC enhances classification performance when
utilized as a standalone augmentation method. Furthermore, combining RVC with
other augmentation methods such as frequency masking and segment removal leads
to additional performance gains, highlighting its potential for improving
dialect classification in low-resource scenarios.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes the use of Retrieval-based Voice Conversion (RVC) to augment data for low-resource German dialect classification, showing improved model performance especially when combined with other augmentations.
AI.motivation: Dialect classification models suffer from limited dialectal data, restricting their effectiveness.
AI.method: Use RVC to convert audio samples to a uniform speaker voice, reducing speaker variability so models focus on dialect features; combined with augmentation techniques like frequency masking and segment removal.
AI.result: RVC alone improves classification performance; combining RVC with other augmentations yields further gains.
AI.conclusion: RVC is an effective data augmentation strategy for enhancing low-resource dialect classification models, especially when used alongside other methods.

title: Disentangling the Roles of Representation and Selection in Data Pruning
summary:
Data pruning, selecting small but impactful subsets, offers a promising way
to efficiently scale NLP model training. However, existing methods often
involve many different design choices, which have not been systematically
studied. This limits future developments. In this work, we decompose data
pruning into two key components: the data representation and the selection
algorithm, and we systematically analyze their influence on the selection of
instances. Our theoretical and empirical results highlight the crucial role of
representations: better representations, e.g., training gradients, generally
lead to a better selection of instances, regardless of the chosen selection
algorithm. Furthermore, different selection algorithms excel in different
settings, and none consistently outperforms the others. Moreover, the selection
algorithms do not always align with their intended objectives: for example,
algorithms designed for the same objective can select drastically different
instances, highlighting the need for careful evaluation.

AI.tldr: This paper systematically studies data pruning in NLP training by breaking it down into data representation and selection algorithm, showing that better representations improve instance selection and different algorithms have varied strengths without a one-size-fits-all best.
AI.motivation: Existing data pruning methods have many design choices that haven't been systematically analyzed, limiting improvements in NLP model training efficiency.
AI.method: The paper decomposes data pruning into data representation and selection algorithm components and performs theoretical and empirical analysis of their effects on instance selection quality.
AI.result: The study finds that data representations, like training gradients, critically influence selection quality; different algorithms perform variably depending on settings and do not always meet their intended objectives, sometimes selecting very different instances even with the same goal.
AI.conclusion: Effective data pruning in NLP requires careful choice of data representation and selection algorithm; no single method dominates, and evaluation must consider alignment with objectives to ensure meaningful instance selection.

title: TRACE: Training and Inference-Time Interpretability Analysis for Language Models
summary:
Understanding when and how linguistic knowledge emerges during language model
training remains a central challenge for interpretability. Most existing tools
are post hoc, rely on scalar metrics, or require nontrivial integration effort,
making comprehensive interpretability analysis difficult to deploy and
maintain. We introduce TRACE, a modular toolkit for training and inference-time
interpretability analysis of transformer models. It enables lightweight,
in-training analysis of linguistic and representational signals, including
features probing, intrinsic dimensionality, Hessian curvature, and output
diagnostics. It integrates with ABSynth, a controllable synthetic corpus
generator that provides structured annotations for precise evaluation of
linguistic feature acquisition. Experiments with autoregressive transformers
demonstrate that TRACE reveals developmental phenomena such as early syntactic
emergence, delayed semantic acquisition, and representational compression,
signals overlooked by traditional scalar metrics such as loss or accuracy. With
minimal integration effort, the tool enables layer-wise diagnostics,
convergence-based early stopping, and detection of structural errors, making
transformer analysis interpretable, actionable, and reproducible.

AI.tldr: TRACE is a modular toolkit that facilitates interpretability analysis of transformer language models during training, revealing linguistic knowledge emergence and enabling detailed diagnostics.
AI.motivation: Current interpretability tools are limited by being post hoc, reliant on scalar metrics, or requiring complex integration, hindering comprehensive analysis during training.
AI.method: TRACE provides lightweight, in-training interpretability analyses including feature probing, intrinsic dimensionality, Hessian curvature, and output diagnostics, and integrates with ABSynth for precise linguistic feature evaluation.
AI.result: Experiments show TRACE uncovers developmental linguistic phenomena such as early syntax emergence, delayed semantic acquisition, and representational compression, which traditional metrics miss.
AI.conclusion: TRACE offers interpretable, actionable, and reproducible transformer analysis with minimal integration effort, supporting layer-wise diagnostics, early stopping, and structural error detection during training.

title: Recon, Answer, Verify: Agents in Search of Truth
summary:
Automated fact checking with large language models (LLMs) offers a scalable
alternative to manual verification. Evaluating fact checking is challenging as
existing benchmark datasets often include post claim analysis and annotator
cues, which are absent in real world scenarios where claims are fact checked
immediately after being made. This limits the realism of current evaluations.
We present Politi Fact Only (PFO), a 5 class benchmark dataset of 2,982
political claims from politifact.com, where all post claim analysis and
annotator cues have been removed manually. This ensures that models are
evaluated using only the information that would have been available prior to
the claim's verification. Evaluating LLMs on PFO, we see an average performance
drop of 22% in terms of macro f1 compared to PFO's unfiltered version. Based on
the identified challenges of the existing LLM based fact checking system, we
propose RAV (Recon Answer Verify), an agentic framework with three agents:
question generator, answer generator, and label generator. Our pipeline
iteratively generates and answers sub questions to verify different aspects of
the claim before finally generating the label. RAV generalizes across domains
and label granularities, and it outperforms state of the art approaches on well
known baselines RAWFC (fact checking, 3 class) by 25.28%, and on HOVER
(encyclopedia, 2 class) by 1.54% on 2 hop, 4.94% on 3 hop, and 1.78% on 4 hop,
sub categories respectively. RAV shows the least performance drop compared to
baselines of 16.3% in macro f1 when we compare PFO with its unfiltered version.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a more realistic benchmark dataset (PFO) for fact checking political claims, revealing significant performance drops in LLMs without post claim analysis. To address these challenges, it proposes RAV, a multi-agent framework that improves fact checking accuracy across domains and maintains performance under realistic evaluation conditions.
AI.motivation: Existing fact checking benchmarks artificially include post claim analysis and annotator cues absent in real-world scenarios, limiting the realism and reliability of model evaluations.
AI.method: Creation of the PFO dataset without post claim analysis; development of RAV, a multi-agent framework with agents for question generation, answer generation, and label generation that iteratively verifies claim aspects.
AI.result: LLMs demonstrated a 22% performance drop on PFO vs. unfiltered datasets. RAV outperformed state-of-the-art methods substantially on multiple benchmarks, including RAWFC and HOVER, and showed least performance degradation on realistic datasets.
AI.conclusion: Removing unrealistic cues from datasets reveals true LLM limitations in fact checking. The proposed RAV framework effectively enhances LLM-based fact checking, generalizes across domains, and better withstands realistic evaluation conditions.

title: TACOS: Open Tagging and Comparative Scoring for Instruction Fine-Tuning Data Selection
summary:
Instruction Fine-Tuning (IFT) is crucial for aligning large language models
(LLMs) with human preferences, and selecting a small yet representative subset
from massive data significantly facilitates IFT in terms of both efficiency and
effectiveness. Nevertheless, existing approaches suffer from two limitations:
the use of simple heuristics restricts data diversity, while the singleton data
quality evaluation accounts for inconsistent criteria between independent
samples. To address the issues, we present TACOS, an innovative method that
integrates Open Tagging and Comparative Scoring for IFT data selection. To
capture data diversity, we leverage LLMs to assign open-domain tags to human
queries, followed by a normalization stage to denoise the open tags and enable
efficient clustering. Additionally, we suggest a comparative scoring method
that allows the relative quality evaluation of samples within a cluster,
avoiding inconsistent criteria seen in singleton-based evaluations. Extensive
experiments across diverse datasets and LLM architectures demonstrate that
TACOS outperforms existing approaches by a large margin. Notably, it achieves
superior instruction-following performance on MT-Bench and ranks 1st among
LLaMA2-7B-Based models on AlpacaEval 2.0, illustrating its efficacy for IFT
data selection.

AI.tldr: TACOS is a novel method for selecting high-quality, diverse data subsets for instruction fine-tuning large language models, improving efficiency and effectiveness compared to prior methods.
AI.motivation: Existing data selection methods for instruction fine-tuning rely on simple heuristics limiting diversity and evaluate sample quality independently, causing inconsistent quality assessment.
AI.method: TACOS uses large language models to assign open-domain tags to queries for clustering and applies comparative scoring within clusters to evaluate sample quality more consistently.
AI.result: Experiments across various datasets and LLMs show TACOS significantly outperforms previous approaches, achieving top instruction-following scores on MT-Bench and AlpacaEval 2.0 benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: TACOS effectively enhances instruction fine-tuning data selection by ensuring diverse and consistently evaluated samples, leading to superior model alignment with human preferences.
