models from scratch and performing large-scale evaluations, including short-
and long-context benchmarks, as well as supervised fine-tuning~(SFT). We
further propose a hybrid architecture that interleaves \rat with local
attention. By combining efficient long-range modeling with strong local
interactions, this hybrid design not only improves inference speed and reduces
cache memory usage compared to attention, but also consistently enhances
performance, for example, achieving an average 1 point gain in commonsense
reasoning tasks, up to 4 points on code tasks, and a 1 point Rouge-L increase
in a summarization SFT task. Code is available at
https://github.com/CLAIRE-Labo/RAT

AI.tldr: The paper introduces at, a novel layer combining linear recurrence within chunks and softmax attention across chunks to improve training and generation speed of transformers while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: Softmax attention in transformers creates computational bottlenecks for long-context processing. Existing approaches trade-off efficiency and accuracy inadequately.
AI.method: at partitions input into chunks, applies linear recurrence locally within each chunk, and softmax attention globally across chunks. Chunk size controls the trade-off between recurrence and attention.
AI.result: With chunk size 16, at achieves up to 7x training speed improvement and 9x generation speed-up on long sequences, maintaining or improving accuracy over standard attention. A hybrid architecture mixing at and local attention further boosts performance and efficiency on various tasks.
AI.conclusion: at offers a flexible and efficient alternative to pure attention or recurrence for transformers, demonstrating strong empirical gains in speed, memory, and performance across benchmarks and fine-tuning tasks.

title: GradOT: Training-free Gradient-preserving Offsite-tuning for Large Language Models
summary:
The rapid growth of large language models (LLMs) with traditional centralized
fine-tuning emerges as a key technique for adapting these models to
domain-specific challenges, yielding privacy risks for both model and data
owners. One promising solution, called offsite-tuning (OT), is proposed to
address these challenges, where a weaker emulator is compressed from the
original model and further fine-tuned with adapter to enhance privacy. However,
the existing OT-based methods require high computational costs and lack
theoretical analysis. This paper introduces a novel OT approach based on
gradient-preserving compression, named GradOT. By analyzing the OT problem
through the lens of optimization, we propose a method that selectively applies
compression techniques such as rank compression and channel pruning, preserving
the gradients of fine-tuned adapters while ensuring privacy. Extensive
experiments demonstrate that our approach surpasses existing OT methods, both
in terms of privacy protection and model performance. Our method provides a
theoretical foundation for OT and offers a practical, training-free solution
for offsite-tuning of large-scale LLMs.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses privacy risks in fine-tuning large language models via offsite-tuning using a new gradient-preserving compression method, GradOT, which theoretically supports and improves privacy and model performance while reducing computational costs.
AI.motivation: Existing offsite-tuning methods for adapting large language models are computationally expensive and lack theoretical underpinnings, risking privacy leakage for model and data owners.
AI.method: GradOT employs selective gradient-preserving compression techniques like rank compression and channel pruning to maintain gradient information necessary for fine-tuning while enhancing privacy and reducing computational cost.
AI.result: Experiments show GradOT outperforms existing offsite-tuning approaches by providing better privacy protection and improved model performance without additional training.
AI.conclusion: GradOT offers a theoretically grounded, efficient, and practical offsite-tuning method that improves privacy and performance in adapting large language models, addressing key limitations of prior approaches.

title: Think Twice Before You Judge: Mixture of Dual Reasoning Experts for Multimodal Sarcasm Detection
summary:
Multimodal sarcasm detection has attracted growing interest due to the rise
of multimedia posts on social media. Understanding sarcastic image-text posts
often requires external contextual knowledge, such as cultural references or
commonsense reasoning. However, existing models struggle to capture the deeper
rationale behind sarcasm, relying mainly on shallow cues like image captions or
object-attribute pairs from images. To address this, we propose \textbf{MiDRE}
(\textbf{Mi}xture of \textbf{D}ual \textbf{R}easoning \textbf{E}xperts), which
integrates an internal reasoning expert for detecting incongruities within the
image-text pair and an external reasoning expert that utilizes structured
rationales generated via Chain-of-Thought prompting to a Large Vision-Language
Model. An adaptive gating mechanism dynamically weighs the two experts,
selecting the most relevant reasoning path. Experiments on two benchmark
datasets show that MiDRE achieves superior performance over baselines. Various
qualitative analyses highlight the crucial role of external rationales,
revealing that even when they are occasionally noisy, they provide valuable
cues that guide the model toward a better understanding of sarcasm.

AI.tldr: This paper presents MiDRE, a model for multimodal sarcasm detection that combines internal and external reasoning to better understand sarcastic image-text posts, achieving superior results on benchmark datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing models for sarcasm detection mainly use shallow cues and struggle to capture the deeper rationale, especially context requiring cultural knowledge or commonsense reasoning.
AI.method: MiDRE integrates two reasoning experts: an internal expert detecting inconsistencies within the image-text pair and an external expert using structured rationales generated from a Large Vision-Language Model via Chain-of-Thought prompting, with an adaptive gating mechanism to weigh their contributions.
AI.result: MiDRE outperforms baseline models on two benchmark datasets, demonstrating the effectiveness of combining internal and external reasoning for sarcasm detection.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating external rationales, even if noisy, significantly enhances model understanding of sarcasm by providing valuable contextual cues, making MiDRE a state-of-the-art approach for multimodal sarcasm detection.

title: Dual Modality-Aware Gated Prompt Tuning for Few-Shot Multimodal Sarcasm Detection
summary:
The widespread use of multimodal content on social media has heightened the
need for effective sarcasm detection to improve opinion mining. However,
existing models rely heavily on large annotated datasets, making them less
suitable for real-world scenarios where labeled data is scarce. This motivates
the need to explore the problem in a few-shot setting. To this end, we
introduce DMDP (Deep Modality-Disentangled Prompt Tuning), a novel framework
for few-shot multimodal sarcasm detection. Unlike prior methods that use
shallow, unified prompts across modalities, DMDP employs gated,
modality-specific deep prompts for text and visual encoders. These prompts are
injected across multiple layers to enable hierarchical feature learning and
better capture diverse sarcasm types. To enhance intra-modal learning, we
incorporate a prompt-sharing mechanism across layers, allowing the model to
aggregate both low-level and high-level semantic cues. Additionally, a
cross-modal prompt alignment module enables nuanced interactions between image
and text representations, improving the model's ability to detect subtle
sarcastic intent. Experiments on two public datasets demonstrate DMDP's
superior performance in both few-shot and extremely low-resource settings.
Further cross-dataset evaluations show that DMDP generalizes well across
domains, consistently outperforming baseline methods.

AI.tldr: DMDP is a new approach for few-shot multimodal sarcasm detection using deep, modality-specific prompts for text and images, improving performance on limited data.
AI.motivation: Existing sarcasm detection models require large annotated datasets, which are scarce in real-world situations, motivating a few-shot learning approach.
AI.method: DMDP uses gated deep prompts specifically for text and visuals, injected at multiple layers for hierarchical feature learning, with prompt-sharing across layers and a cross-modal alignment module for better interaction between text and image features.
AI.result: DMDP shows superior performance on two public datasets under few-shot and low-resource settings, with good generalization in cross-dataset evaluations, outperforming existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed DMDP framework effectively detects multimodal sarcasm with limited labeled data and generalizes well, making it suitable for real-world applications with scarce annotations.

title: Unveiling the Potential of Diffusion Large Language Model in Controllable Generation
summary:
Diffusion models, originally developed for image generation, have emerged as
a promising alternative to autoregressive large language models (LLMs). We
present a theoretical analysis comparing autoregressive and masked diffusion
LLMs, revealing that the intrinsic bidirectional attention mechanism of
diffusion LLMs (dLLMs) enables superior context modeling and generation
controllability. However, existing dLLM applications face significant
challenges in controllable generation: the native multi-step denoising process
exhibits high sensitivity to sequence length, elevated hallucination rates, and
prohibitive inference costs without specialized optimizations. To address these
limitations, we propose \textbf{S}elf-adaptive \textbf{S}chema
\textbf{S}caffolding ($S^3$), a novel framework that enables dLLMs to generate
structured outputs (e.g., JSON) while maintaining semantic fidelity and
accelerating inference. Our approach injects the target schema structure into
the output context, reducing unnecessary computation while improving
controllability. Extensive experiments demonstrate that $S^3$ achieves
substantial improvements: 65\% increase in structural adherence, 48\%
enhancement in content fidelity, and 17\% reduction in hallucination rates
compared to baseline. These results establish both theoretical foundations and
practical pathways for deploying diffusion models in controllable text
generation tasks. Code and data will be publicly released.

AI.tldr: This paper explores diffusion language models (dLLMs) as an alternative to autoregressive models, identifying their advantages in context modeling and controllability but also highlighting challenges like high hallucination and inference costs. It proposes a novel Self-adaptive Schema Scaffolding (S^3) framework that incorporates schema structures to improve controllable text generation, achieving significant gains in structural adherence, content fidelity, and reduced hallucinations.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of diffusion LLMs in controllable generation, including sensitivity to sequence length, high hallucination rates, and expensive inference, and enable structured, semantically faithful, and efficient generation.
AI.method: Introducing S^3, a framework that injects target schema structures into the output context to guide diffusion LLMs in generating structured outputs, reducing unnecessary computations, enhancing controllability, and speeding up inference.
AI.result: S^3 achieved a 65% improvement in structural adherence, 48% better content fidelity, and a 17% reduction in hallucination rates compared to baseline diffusion LLMs.
AI.conclusion: The study establishes both theoretical and practical foundations for using diffusion models in controllable text generation, demonstrating that schema-guided approaches like S^3 can significantly improve performance and efficiency, offering a promising alternative to autoregressive LLMs.

title: AdS: Adapter-state Sharing Framework for Multimodal Sarcasm Detection
summary:
The growing prevalence of multimodal image-text sarcasm on social media poses
challenges for opinion mining, especially under resource constraints. Existing
approaches rely on full fine-tuning of large pre-trained models, making them
unsuitable for low-resource settings. While recent parameter-efficient
fine-tuning (PEFT) methods offer promise, their off-the-shelf use underperforms
on complex tasks like sarcasm detection. We propose AdS (Adapter-State
Sharing), a lightweight framework built on CLIP that inserts adapters only in
the upper layers and introduces a novel adapter-state sharing mechanism, where
textual adapters guide visual ones. This design promotes efficient cross-modal
learning while preserving low-level unimodal representations. Experiments on
two public benchmarks demonstrate that AdS achieves state-of-the-art results
using significantly fewer trainable parameters than existing PEFT and full
fine-tuning approaches.

AI.tldr: A novel framework AdS is proposed for detecting multimodal image-text sarcasm efficiently under low-resource constraints.
AI.motivation: The challenge is to detect sarcasm in multimodal social media content without relying on resource-heavy full fine-tuning of large models.
AI.method: AdS uses CLIP with adapters only in upper layers and a unique adapter-state sharing where textual adapters guide visual adapters, enabling efficient cross-modal learning while preserving unimodal features.
AI.result: AdS achieved state-of-the-art sarcasm detection on two benchmarks with fewer trainable parameters compared to both existing PEFT methods and full fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: AdS provides an effective and parameter-efficient solution for complex multimodal sarcasm detection tasks in low-resource scenarios.

title: DP-Fusion: Token-Level Differentially Private Inference for Large Language Models
comment:
Our code and data are publicly available here:
  https://github.com/MBZUAI-Trustworthy-ML/DP-Fusion-DPI
summary:
Large language models (LLMs) can leak sensitive information from their
context through generated outputs, either accidentally or when prompted
adversarially. Existing defenses that aim to preserve context privacy during
inference either lack formal guarantees or suffer from a poor utility/privacy
trade-off. We propose DP-Fusion, a token-level Differentially Private Inference
(DPI) mechanism that provably bounds how much an LLM's outputs reveal about
sensitive tokens in its context. We demonstrate DPI through the task of
document privatization, where the goal is to paraphrase documents so that
sensitive content (e.g., Personally Identifiable Information, PII) cannot be
reliably inferred, while still preserving the overall utility of the text. This
is controlled by a parameter $\epsilon$: $\epsilon=0$ hides PII entirely, while
higher values trade off privacy for improved paraphrase quality. DP-Fusion
works as follows: (i) partition sensitive tokens into disjoint privacy groups,
(ii) run the LLM once per group, and (iii) blend the output distributions so
that the final output remains within a fixed statistical distance of the
baseline distribution produced when no privacy group is revealed. This approach
allows fine-grained control over the privacy/utility trade-off but requires
multiple LLM forward passes.

AI.tldr: DP-Fusion is a new method using Differentially Private Inference to prevent LLMs from leaking sensitive information in their outputs, balancing privacy and text utility through parameterized control.
AI.motivation: Existing defenses against sensitive information leakage in large language model outputs either lack formal privacy guarantees or result in poor trade-offs between privacy and utility.
AI.method: DP-Fusion applies token-level Differentially Private Inference by grouping sensitive tokens, running the LLM once per group, and blending outputs to maintain output privacy within set bounds, controlled by a privacy parameter \(\epsilon\).
AI.result: DP-Fusion effectively controls the trade-off between privacy and paraphrase quality in document privatization, with \(\epsilon=0\) fully hiding sensitive data and higher values improving paraphrase utility at the cost of reduced privacy.
AI.conclusion: The proposed DPI mechanism provides provable privacy guarantees for LLM outputs while allowing adjustable privacy-utility trade-offs, though it requires multiple model forward passes.

title: Nile-Chat: Egyptian Language Models for Arabic and Latin Scripts
summary:
We introduce Nile-Chat-4B, 3x4B-A6B, and 12B, a collection of LLMs for
Egyptian dialect, uniquely designed to understand and generate texts written in
both Arabic and Latin scripts. Specifically, with Nile-Chat-3x4B-A6B, we
introduce a novel language adaptation approach by leveraging the
Branch-Train-MiX strategy to merge script-specialized experts, into a single
MoE model. Our Nile-Chat models significantly outperform leading multilingual
and Arabic LLMs, such as LLaMa, Jais, and ALLaM, on our newly introduced
Egyptian evaluation benchmarks, which span both understanding and generative
tasks. Notably, our 12B model yields a 14.4% performance gain over
Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct on Latin-script benchmarks. All our resources are publicly
available. We believe this work presents a comprehensive methodology for
adapting LLMs to dual-script languages, addressing an often overlooked aspect
in modern LLM development.

AI.tldr: Nile-Chat models are specialized LLMs for Egyptian dialects in Arabic and Latin scripts, employing a novel method to integrate script experts into one model, outperforming existing multilingual and Arabic LLMs.
AI.motivation: To create LLMs that effectively understand and generate text in dual-script Egyptian dialects, addressing the challenge of handling both Arabic and Latin scripts in language models.
AI.method: They developed Nile-Chat models, including Nile-Chat-3x4B-A6B using the Branch-Train-MiX strategy to merge script-specialized experts into a single Mixture of Experts (MoE) model.
AI.result: Nile-Chat models outperform top multilingual and Arabic LLMs on Egyptian benchmarks, with the 12B model achieving a notable 14.4% gain over Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct on Latin-script tasks.
AI.conclusion: The study offers an effective and publicly available approach for adapting large language models to languages with dual scripts, an important but underexplored area in LLM development.

title: PRIME: Large Language Model Personalization with Cognitive Memory and Thought Processes
summary:
Large language model (LLM) personalization aims to align model outputs with
individuals' unique preferences and opinions. While recent efforts have
implemented various personalization methods, a unified theoretical framework
that can systematically understand the drivers of effective personalization is
still lacking. In this work, we integrate the well-established cognitive
dual-memory model into LLM personalization, by mirroring episodic memory to
historical user engagements and semantic memory to long-term, evolving user
beliefs. Specifically, we systematically investigate memory instantiations and
introduce a unified framework, PRIME, using episodic and semantic memory
mechanisms. We further augment PRIME with a novel personalized thinking
capability inspired by the slow thinking strategy. Moreover, recognizing the
absence of suitable benchmarks, we introduce a dataset using Change My View
(CMV) from Reddit, specifically designed to evaluate long-context
personalization. Extensive experiments validate PRIME's effectiveness across
both long- and short-context scenarios. Further analysis confirms that PRIME
effectively captures dynamic personalization beyond mere popularity biases.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes PRIME, a framework integrating cognitive dual-memory models into large language model personalization to better align with individual user preferences, validated on a new dataset from Reddit.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM personalization approaches lack a unified theoretical framework to understand what drives effective personalization.
AI.method: The authors introduce PRIME, which uses episodic memory (historical user interactions) and semantic memory (long-term user beliefs) to personalize LLMs, enhanced with a personalized thinking ability inspired by slow thinking. They also create a new benchmark dataset from Reddit's Change My View forum for evaluating personalization in long contexts.
AI.result: Experiments show that PRIME performs effectively in both long- and short-context personalization and captures dynamic personalization beyond popularity biases.
AI.conclusion: PRIME successfully integrates cognitive memory theories into LLM personalization, providing a unified framework that advances effective user-aligned model outputs, supported by a new evaluation benchmark.

title: Retain or Reframe? A Computational Framework for the Analysis of Framing in News Articles and Reader Comments
summary:
When a news article describes immigration as an "economic burden" or a
"humanitarian crisis," it selectively emphasizes certain aspects of the issue.
Although \textit{framing} shapes how the public interprets such issues,
audiences do not absorb frames passively but actively reorganize the presented
information. While this relationship between source content and audience
response is well-documented in the social sciences, NLP approaches often ignore
it, detecting frames in articles and responses in isolation. We present the
first computational framework for large-scale analysis of framing across source
content (news articles) and audience responses (reader comments).
Methodologically, we refine frame labels and develop a framework that
reconstructs dominant frames in articles and comments from sentence-level
predictions, and aligns articles with topically relevant comments. Applying our
framework across eleven topics and two news outlets, we find that frame reuse
in comments correlates highly across outlets, while topic-specific patterns
vary. We release a frame classifier that performs well on both articles and
comments, a dataset of article and comment sentences manually labeled for
frames, and a large-scale dataset of articles and comments with predicted frame
labels.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a computational framework to study how news article frames about immigration align with reader comment frames, revealing correlated frame reuse across outlets with topic-specific variations.
AI.motivation: To address the gap in NLP approaches that analyze news frames and audience responses in isolation, overlooking the interactive framing dynamics between source content and audience interpretation.
AI.method: The authors refine frame labels and build a framework to reconstruct dominant frames at the sentence level in both articles and comments, aligning relevant comments with articles through topical similarity, applying this across multiple topics and outlets.
AI.result: They find high correlation of frame reuse in comments across news outlets, with variations depending on the topic. They release a well-performing frame classifier, a labeled dataset of sentences, and a large dataset with predicted frame labels for community use.
AI.conclusion: This framework advances understanding of framing interplay between news sources and audiences, providing tools and datasets that enable large-scale analysis of framing in media and public discourse.

title: Knowledge-Aware Self-Correction in Language Models via Structured Memory Graphs
summary:
Large Language Models (LLMs) are powerful yet prone to generating factual
errors, commonly referred to as hallucinations. We present a lightweight,
interpretable framework for knowledge-aware self-correction of LLM outputs
using structured memory graphs based on RDF triples. Without retraining or
fine-tuning, our method post-processes model outputs and corrects factual
inconsistencies via external semantic memory. We demonstrate the approach using
DistilGPT-2 and show promising results on simple factual prompts.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a lightweight, interpretable method to correct factual errors in LLM outputs by using structured memory graphs, applied without retraining and tested effectively on DistilGPT-2.
AI.motivation: Large Language Models often generate factual errors (hallucinations), which undermine their reliability. The motivation is to create a method to reduce these errors without costly retraining.
AI.method: They use a post-processing framework that leverages structured memory graphs composed of RDF triples to provide external semantic memory for knowledge-aware self-correction of LLM outputs.
AI.result: The method was tested on DistilGPT-2 with simple factual prompts and showed promising improvements in correcting factual inaccuracies.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides an effective and interpretable way to enhance factual accuracy of LLM outputs through external knowledge graphs, without needing to retrain the language model itself.

title: Put Teacher in Student's Shoes: Cross-Distillation for Ultra-compact Model Compression Framework
summary:
In the era of mobile computing, deploying efficient Natural Language
Processing (NLP) models in resource-restricted edge settings presents
significant challenges, particularly in environments requiring strict privacy
compliance, real-time responsiveness, and diverse multi-tasking capabilities.
These challenges create a fundamental need for ultra-compact models that
maintain strong performance across various NLP tasks while adhering to
stringent memory constraints. To this end, we introduce Edge ultra-lIte BERT
framework (EI-BERT) with a novel cross-distillation method. EI-BERT efficiently
compresses models through a comprehensive pipeline including hard token
pruning, cross-distillation and parameter quantization. Specifically, the
cross-distillation method uniquely positions the teacher model to understand
the student model's perspective, ensuring efficient knowledge transfer through
parameter integration and the mutual interplay between models. Through
extensive experiments, we achieve a remarkably compact BERT-based model of only
1.91 MB - the smallest to date for Natural Language Understanding (NLU) tasks.
This ultra-compact model has been successfully deployed across multiple
scenarios within the Alipay ecosystem, demonstrating significant improvements
in real-world applications. For example, it has been integrated into Alipay's
live Edge Recommendation system since January 2024, currently serving the app's
recommendation traffic across \textbf{8.4 million daily active devices}.

AI.tldr: The paper presents EI-BERT, an ultra-compact BERT model optimized for edge deployment with mobile-like constraints, using a novel cross-distillation technique for efficient compression.
AI.motivation: Deploying efficient NLP models on resource-limited edge devices that require privacy, real-time processing, and multitasking is challenging, necessitating ultra-compact but high-performing models.
AI.method: EI-BERT framework employs hard token pruning, cross-distillation where the teacher understands the student's perspective, and parameter quantization to compress models effectively.
AI.result: They achieved a remarkably small BERT-based model of 1.91 MB, the smallest to date for NLU tasks, and successfully deployed it in multiple real-world scenarios within the Alipay ecosystem.
AI.conclusion: EI-BERT demonstrates that ultra-compact NLP models with strong performance can be practically deployed on large-scale edge devices, significantly improving real-world applications like recommendation systems.

title: R1-RE: Cross-Domain Relationship Extraction with RLVR
summary:
Relationship extraction (RE) is a core task in natural language processing.
Traditional approaches typically frame RE as a supervised learning problem,
directly mapping context to labels-an approach that often suffers from poor
out-of-domain (OOD) generalization. Inspired by the workflow of human
annotators, we reframe RE as a reasoning task guided by annotation guidelines
and introduce R1-RE, the first reinforcement learning with verifiable reward
(RLVR) framework for RE tasks. Our method elicits the reasoning abilities of
small language models for annotation tasks, resulting in significantly improved
OOD robustness. We evaluate our approach on the public Sem-2010 dataset and a
private MDKG dataset. The R1-RE-7B model attains an average OOD accuracy of
approximately 70%, on par with leading proprietary models such as GPT-4o.
Additionally, our comprehensive analysis provides novel insights into the
training dynamics and emergent reasoning behaviors of the RLVR paradigm for RE.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes R1-RE, a reinforcement learning framework with verifiable reward for relation extraction (RE) tasks to improve out-of-domain generalization by mimicking human annotator reasoning.
AI.motivation: Traditional supervised learning approaches for RE suffer from poor out-of-domain generalization, motivating a method that better mimics human reasoning during annotation.
AI.method: The authors reframe RE as a reasoning task guided by annotation guidelines, using reinforcement learning with verifiable reward (RLVR) to train small language models to elicit reasoning abilities.
AI.result: R1-RE-7B achieves approximately 70% average OOD accuracy on two datasets, comparable to strong proprietary models like GPT-4o.
AI.conclusion: The RLVR paradigm effectively improves OOD robustness in RE and reveals important training dynamics and emergent reasoning behaviors for this task.

title: XiYan-SQL: A Novel Multi-Generator Framework For Text-to-SQL
summary:
To leverage the advantages of LLM in addressing challenges in the Text-to-SQL
task, we present XiYan-SQL, an innovative framework effectively generating and
utilizing multiple SQL candidates. It consists of three components: 1) a Schema
Filter module filtering and obtaining multiple relevant schemas; 2) a
multi-generator ensemble approach generating multiple highquality and diverse
SQL queries; 3) a selection model with a candidate reorganization strategy
implemented to obtain the optimal SQL query. Specifically, for the
multi-generator ensemble, we employ a multi-task fine-tuning strategy to
enhance the capabilities of SQL generation models for the intrinsic alignment
between SQL and text, and construct multiple generation models with distinct
generation styles by fine-tuning across different SQL formats. The experimental
results and comprehensive analysis demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness
of our framework. Overall, XiYan-SQL achieves a new SOTA performance of 75.63%
on the notable BIRD benchmark, surpassing all previous methods. It also attains
SOTA performance on the Spider test set with an accuracy of 89.65%.

AI.tldr: XiYan-SQL is a novel framework for Text-to-SQL tasks leveraging large language models by generating multiple SQL candidates through schema filtering, multi-generator ensemble, and candidate selection, achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: To exploit the advantages of large language models (LLMs) to improve SQL query generation accuracy and robustness for Text-to-SQL tasks.
AI.method: The framework includes a Schema Filter to select relevant schemas, a multi-generator ensemble using multi-task fine-tuning to create diverse high-quality SQL queries with various generation styles, and a selection model with candidate reorganization to choose the optimal query.
AI.result: XiYan-SQL outperforms previous methods, achieving 75.63% accuracy on the BIRD benchmark and 89.65% accuracy on the Spider test set, both state-of-the-art results.
AI.conclusion: The proposed XiYan-SQL framework effectively integrates schema filtering, diverse SQL generation, and candidate selection, significantly improving Text-to-SQL performance and robustness compared to existing models.

title: Why We Feel What We Feel: Joint Detection of Emotions and Their Opinion Triggers in E-commerce
comment:
23 pages, 11 figures, 7 tables. Dataset and code will be made
  publicly available
summary:
Customer reviews on e-commerce platforms capture critical affective signals
that drive purchasing decisions. However, no existing research has explored the
joint task of emotion detection and explanatory span identification in
e-commerce reviews - a crucial gap in understanding what triggers customer
emotional responses. To bridge this gap, we propose a novel joint task unifying
Emotion detection and Opinion Trigger extraction (EOT), which explicitly models
the relationship between causal text spans (opinion triggers) and affective
dimensions (emotion categories) grounded in Plutchik's theory of 8 primary
emotions. In the absence of labeled data, we introduce EOT-X, a human-annotated
collection of 2,400 reviews with fine-grained emotions and opinion triggers. We
evaluate 23 Large Language Models (LLMs) and present EOT-DETECT, a structured
prompting framework with systematic reasoning and self-reflection. Our
framework surpasses zero-shot and chain-of-thought techniques, across
e-commerce domains.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the joint task of detecting emotions and identifying opinion triggers in e-commerce reviews, creating a new dataset and a prompting framework that outperforms existing methods.
AI.motivation: Customer reviews contain vital emotional signals influencing purchasing decisions, but no prior work jointly analyzes emotion detection and the identification of opinion triggers in e-commerce contexts, limiting understanding of what causes customer emotions.
AI.method: They propose a novel joint task combining emotion detection and opinion trigger extraction based on Plutchik's 8 primary emotions, introduce a new dataset EOT-X of 2,400 annotated reviews, evaluate 23 large language models, and develop EOT-DETECT, a structured prompting framework utilizing systematic reasoning and self-reflection.
AI.result: The EOT-DETECT framework outperforms zero-shot and chain-of-thought prompting techniques on the joint task across multiple e-commerce domains, demonstrating effective modeling of the relationship between emotions and their causal opinion triggers.
AI.conclusion: By unifying emotion detection with opinion trigger extraction and providing annotated data with effective modeling strategies, the paper advances understanding of emotional drivers in e-commerce reviews, enabling better interpretation of customer feedback.

title: LOOM-Scope: a comprehensive and efficient LOng-cOntext Model evaluation framework
summary:
Long-context processing has become a fundamental capability for large
language models~(LLMs). To assess model's long-context performance, numerous
long-context evaluation benchmarks have been proposed. However, variations in
evaluation settings across these benchmarks lead to inconsistent results,
making it difficult to draw reliable comparisons. Besides, the high
computational cost of long-context evaluation poses a significant barrier for
the community to conduct comprehensive assessments of long-context models. In
this paper, we propose LOOM-Scope, a comprehensive and efficient framework for
long-context evaluation. LOOM-Scope standardizes evaluation settings across
diverse benchmarks, supports deployment of efficient long-context inference
acceleration methods, and introduces a holistic yet lightweight benchmark suite
to evaluate models comprehensively. Homepage: https://loomscope.github.io

AI.tldr: LOOM-Scope is a standardized, efficient framework for evaluating long-context capabilities of large language models, addressing inconsistencies and high computational costs in existing benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing long-context evaluation benchmarks vary in settings and require high computational resources, leading to unreliable comparisons and limited comprehensive assessments.
AI.method: LOOM-Scope standardizes evaluation settings, supports efficient inference acceleration techniques, and provides a holistic yet lightweight benchmark suite for thorough long-context evaluation.
AI.result: The framework enables consistent and efficient long-context model evaluation, reducing computational costs and facilitating more reliable comparisons across models.
AI.conclusion: LOOM-Scope offers a comprehensive, standardized, and resource-efficient solution for assessing long-context performance of large language models, advancing research in this important area.

title: "This Suits You the Best": Query Focused Comparative Explainable Summarization
summary:
Product recommendations inherently involve comparisons, yet traditional
opinion summarization often fails to provide holistic comparative insights. We
propose the novel task of generating Query-Focused Comparative Explainable
Summaries (QF-CES) using Multi-Source Opinion Summarization (M-OS). To address
the lack of query-focused recommendation datasets, we introduce MS-Q2P,
comprising 7,500 queries mapped to 22,500 recommended products with metadata.
We leverage Large Language Models (LLMs) to generate tabular comparative
summaries with query-specific explanations. Our approach is personalized,
privacy-preserving, recommendation engine-agnostic, and category-agnostic. M-OS
as an intermediate step reduces inference latency approximately by 40% compared
to the direct input approach (DIA), which processes raw data directly. We
evaluate open-source and proprietary LLMs for generating and assessing QF-CES.
Extensive evaluations using QF-CES-PROMPT across 5 dimensions (clarity,
faithfulness, informativeness, format adherence, and query relevance) showed an
average Spearman correlation of 0.74 with human judgments, indicating its
potential for QF-CES evaluation.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a novel task and dataset for query-focused comparative explainable summaries in product recommendations, leveraging large language models to generate personalized, privacy-preserving, and efficient tabular summaries.
AI.motivation: Traditional opinion summarization lacks holistic comparative insights for product recommendations and there is a scarcity of query-focused recommendation datasets.
AI.method: The paper proposes Multi-Source Opinion Summarization (M-OS) for generating Query-Focused Comparative Explainable Summaries (QF-CES), introduces the MS-Q2P dataset, and uses large language models to create tabular comparative summaries with query-specific explanations, with M-OS reducing inference latency by 40%.
AI.result: The approach is recommendation engine-agnostic, category-agnostic, personalized, and privacy-preserving, with extensive evaluations showing strong alignment (Spearman correlation 0.74) between automatic and human assessments on multiple quality dimensions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method and dataset enable effective generation and evaluation of query-focused comparative explainable summaries for product recommendations, providing meaningful insights and efficiency improvements over direct methods.

title: Word stress in self-supervised speech models: A cross-linguistic comparison
summary:
In this paper we study word stress representations learned by self-supervised
speech models (S3M), specifically the Wav2vec 2.0 model. We investigate the S3M
representations of word stress for five different languages: Three languages
with variable or lexical stress (Dutch, English and German) and two languages
with fixed or demarcative stress (Hungarian and Polish). We train diagnostic
stress classifiers on S3M embeddings and show that they can distinguish between
stressed and unstressed syllables in read-aloud short sentences with high
accuracy. We also tested language-specificity effects of S3M word stress. The
results indicate that the word stress representations are language-specific,
with a greater difference between the set of variable versus the set of fixed
stressed languages.

AI.tldr: This paper examines how self-supervised speech models, specifically Wav2vec 2.0, learn word stress in five languages, finding that stress representations are language-specific and differ notably between variable and fixed stress languages.
AI.motivation: To understand how self-supervised speech models represent word stress across different languages and to explore the language-specificity of these representations.
AI.method: The study trained diagnostic classifiers on embeddings from the Wav2vec 2.0 model to differentiate stressed vs. unstressed syllables in five languages, comparing variable stress languages (Dutch, English, German) to fixed stress languages (Hungarian, Polish).
AI.result: The classifiers accurately distinguished stressed from unstressed syllables and revealed language-specific stress representations, with larger differences between variable stress versus fixed stress languages.
AI.conclusion: Self-supervised speech model embeddings encode word stress in a language-specific manner, particularly differentiating between variable and fixed stress language groups.

title: A Tale of Two Scripts: Transliteration and Post-Correction for Judeo-Arabic
summary:
Judeo-Arabic refers to Arabic variants historically spoken by Jewish
communities across the Arab world, primarily during the Middle Ages. Unlike
standard Arabic, it is written in Hebrew script by Jewish writers and for
Jewish audiences. Transliterating Judeo-Arabic into Arabic script is
challenging due to ambiguous letter mappings, inconsistent orthographic
conventions, and frequent code-switching into Hebrew and Aramaic. In this
paper, we introduce a two-step approach to automatically transliterate
Judeo-Arabic into Arabic script: simple character-level mapping followed by
post-correction to address grammatical and orthographic errors. We also present
the first benchmark evaluation of LLMs on this task. Finally, we show that
transliteration enables Arabic NLP tools to perform morphosyntactic tagging and
machine translation, which would have not been feasible on the original texts.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a two-step method to transliterate Judeo-Arabic (written in Hebrew script) into Arabic script, leveraging character-level mapping and post-correction. It benchmarks LLMs on this task and demonstrates improved NLP applications thereafter.
AI.motivation: Judeo-Arabic texts are written in Hebrew script but contain Arabic language, making it challenging for standard Arabic NLP tools to process them. Ambiguity and code-switching complicate transliteration efforts.
AI.method: A two-step transliteration approach is proposed: first, simple character-level mapping from Hebrew to Arabic script, followed by post-correction to fix grammatical and orthographic errors. Benchmark evaluation of large language models (LLMs) on this task is also conducted.
AI.result: The approach successfully transliterates Judeo-Arabic texts, enabling the application of Arabic NLP tools like morphosyntactic tagging and machine translation, which were previously infeasible.
AI.conclusion: Transliterating Judeo-Arabic into Arabic script via the proposed method overcomes key challenges, allows the use of Arabic NLP resources, and opens new possibilities for processing these historical Jewish-Arabic texts.

title: LLMs as Architects and Critics for Multi-Source Opinion Summarization
summary:
Multi-source Opinion Summarization (M-OS) extends beyond traditional opinion
summarization by incorporating additional sources of product metadata such as
descriptions, key features, specifications, and ratings, alongside reviews.
This integration results in comprehensive summaries that capture both
subjective opinions and objective product attributes essential for informed
decision-making. While Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown significant
success in various Natural Language Processing (NLP) tasks, their potential in
M-OS remains largely unexplored. Additionally, the lack of evaluation datasets
for this task has impeded further advancements. To bridge this gap, we
introduce M-OS-EVAL, a benchmark dataset for evaluating multi-source opinion
summaries across 7 key dimensions: fluency, coherence, relevance, faithfulness,
aspect coverage, sentiment consistency, specificity. Our results demonstrate
that M-OS significantly enhances user engagement, as evidenced by a user study
in which, on average, 87% of participants preferred M-OS over opinion
summaries. Our experiments demonstrate that factually enriched summaries
enhance user engagement. Notably, M-OS-PROMPTS exhibit stronger alignment with
human judgment, achieving an average Spearman correlation of \r{ho} = 0.74,
which surpasses the performance of previous methodologies.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Multi-source Opinion Summarization (M-OS), which integrates product metadata beyond reviews to create richer summaries. It presents M-OS-EVAL, a new dataset for evaluating opinion summaries across multiple dimensions, and shows that M-OS improves user engagement and outperforms previous methods in alignment with human judgment.
AI.motivation: Traditional opinion summarization focuses mainly on reviews, missing objective product details that affect decision-making. Large Language Models' potential in this extended task is unexplored, and lack of evaluation datasets hinders progress.
AI.method: The authors created M-OS-EVAL, a benchmark dataset evaluating summaries on seven dimensions, and applied M-OS prompting methods using LLMs to generate factually enriched summaries integrating multiple product metadata sources.
AI.result: M-OS summaries led to 87% user preference in studies, indicating enhanced engagement. The M-OS prompting method achieved a Spearman correlation of 0.74 with human judgments, outperforming previous techniques.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating multiple product information sources into opinion summarization and using LLM-based prompting significantly improves summary quality and user engagement, with M-OS-EVAL providing a robust evaluation benchmark.

title: CoSteer: Collaborative Decoding-Time Personalization via Local Delta Steering
summary:
Personalized text generation has become crucial for adapting language models
to diverse and evolving users' personal context across cultural, temporal, and
contextual dimensions. While existing methods often rely on centralized
fine-tuning or static preference alignment, they struggle to achieve real-time
adaptation under resource constraints inherent to personal devices. This
limitation creates a dilemma: large cloud-based models lack access to localized
user-specific information, while small on-device models cannot match the
generation quality of their cloud counterparts. To address this dichotomy, we
present CoSteer, a novel collaborative framework that enables decoding-time
personalization through localized delta steering. Our key insight lies in
leveraging the logits difference between personal context-aware and -agnostic
outputs from local small models as steering signals for cloud-based LLMs.
Specifically, we formulate token-level optimization as an online learning
problem, where local delta vectors dynamically adjust the remote LLM's logits
within the on-device environment. This approach preserves privacy by
transmitting only the final steered tokens rather than raw data or intermediate
vectors, while maintaining cloud-based LLMs' general capabilities without
fine-tuning. Through comprehensive experiments on various personalized
generation tasks, we demonstrate that CoSteer effectively assists LLMs in
generating personalized content by leveraging locally stored user profiles and
histories, ensuring privacy preservation through on-device data processing
while maintaining acceptable computational overhead.

AI.tldr: CoSteer is a collaborative framework that enables real-time personalized text generation by steering cloud-based large language models (LLMs) using local small models on personal devices, balancing privacy, efficiency, and generation quality.
AI.motivation: Personalized text generation is crucial for adapting language models to users' diverse and evolving contexts, but existing methods struggle with real-time adaptation and privacy under resource constraints of personal devices.
AI.method: CoSteer leverages the difference in output logits from local context-aware and context-agnostic small models to steer the cloud-based LLM's token generation dynamically during decoding, formulated as an online learning problem, preserving privacy by only transmitting final steered tokens.
AI.result: Experiments show that CoSteer effectively personalizes LLM-generated content using locally stored user profiles and histories, maintaining privacy and acceptable computational costs without requiring LLM fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: CoSteer successfully resolves the trade-off between on-device personalization and cloud-based model capabilities, enabling privacy-preserving, real-time personalized text generation with efficient resource use.

title: Reason to Rote: Rethinking Memorization in Reasoning
summary:
Large language models readily memorize arbitrary training instances, such as
label noise, yet they perform strikingly well on reasoning tasks. In this work,
we investigate how language models memorize label noise, and why such
memorization in many cases does not heavily affect generalizable reasoning
capabilities. Using two controllable synthetic reasoning datasets with noisy
labels, four-digit addition (FDA) and two-hop relational reasoning (THR), we
discover a reliance of memorization on generalizable reasoning mechanisms:
models continue to compute intermediate reasoning outputs even when retrieving
memorized noisy labels, and intervening reasoning adversely affects
memorization. We further show that memorization operates through distributed
encoding, i.e., aggregating various inputs and intermediate results, rather
than building a look-up mechanism from inputs to noisy labels. Moreover, our
FDA case study reveals memorization occurs via outlier heuristics, where
existing neuron activation patterns are slightly shifted to fit noisy labels.
Together, our findings suggest that memorization of label noise in language
models builds on, rather than overrides, the underlying reasoning mechanisms,
shedding lights on the intriguing phenomenon of benign memorization.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how large language models memorize label noise without harming their reasoning abilities, finding that memorization builds on reasoning mechanisms rather than replacing them.
AI.motivation: Large language models can memorize noisy training data yet still perform well on reasoning tasks; understanding why memorization doesn't severely impact reasoning is important.
AI.method: The study uses synthetic reasoning datasets with noisy labels and analyzes language models on memorization mechanisms, including neuron activation patterns and reasoning interventions.
AI.result: Memorization relies on distributed encoding and outlier heuristics, with models still performing intermediate reasoning steps even when recalling noisy labels; disrupting reasoning hurts memorization.
AI.conclusion: Memorization of label noise leverages existing reasoning processes instead of acting as a separate look-up system, explaining why it often does not damage generalizable reasoning performance.

title: A Survey of Pun Generation: Datasets, Evaluations and Methodologies
summary:
Pun generation seeks to creatively modify linguistic elements in text to
produce humour or evoke double meanings. It also aims to preserve coherence and
contextual appropriateness, making it useful in creative writing and
entertainment across various media and contexts. Although pun generation has
received considerable attention in computational linguistics, there is
currently no dedicated survey that systematically reviews this specific area.
To bridge this gap, this paper provides a comprehensive review of pun
generation datasets and methods across different stages, including conventional
approaches, deep learning techniques, and pre-trained language models.
Additionally, we summarise both automated and human evaluation metrics used to
assess the quality of pun generation. Finally, we discuss the research
challenges and propose promising directions for future work.

AI.tldr: This paper reviews pun generation in computational linguistics, covering datasets, methods, evaluations, challenges, and future directions.
AI.motivation: To fill the gap of lacking a dedicated and systematic survey on pun generation in computational linguistics.
AI.method: A comprehensive review of datasets, conventional approaches, deep learning techniques, pre-trained language models, and evaluation metrics for pun generation.
AI.result: Summarization of existing pun generation datasets and methods, evaluation metrics, and identification of research challenges.
AI.conclusion: The paper highlights the importance of pun generation research, offering insights into current methods and suggesting future research directions to advance the field.

title: Spec-TOD: A Specialized Instruction-Tuned LLM Framework for Efficient Task-Oriented Dialogue Systems
summary:
Task-oriented dialogue (TOD) systems facilitate goal-driven interactions
between users and machines. While recent advances in deep learning have
improved the performance, TOD systems often struggle in low-resource scenarios
with limited labeled data. To address this challenge, we propose Spec-TOD, a
novel framework designed to train an end-to-end TOD system with limited data.
Spec-TOD introduces two main innovations: (i) a novel specialized end-to-end
TOD framework that incorporates explicit task instructions for
instruction-tuned large language models (LLMs), and (ii) an efficient training
strategy that leverages lightweight, specialized LLMs to achieve strong
performance with minimal supervision. Experiments on the MultiWOZ dataset, a
widely used TOD benchmark, demonstrate that Spec-TOD achieves competitive
results while significantly reducing the need for labeled data. These findings
highlight the potential of the proposed framework in advancing efficient and
effective TOD systems in low-resource settings.

AI.tldr: Spec-TOD is a new framework for training task-oriented dialogue systems effectively with limited data by using specialized instruction-tuned large language models and an efficient training strategy, showing strong results on the MultiWOZ benchmark.
AI.motivation: Task-oriented dialogue systems struggle when there is limited labeled data, which is common in low-resource scenarios.
AI.method: Spec-TOD uses explicit task instructions for instruction-tuned large language models and a lightweight, specialized training approach to optimize performance with minimal supervision.
AI.result: Experiments on the MultiWOZ dataset show Spec-TOD achieves competitive performance while significantly reducing labeled data requirements.
AI.conclusion: The Spec-TOD framework advances efficient and effective task-oriented dialogue systems in low-resource settings by reducing data needs without sacrificing performance.

title: Dialogue-Based Multi-Dimensional Relationship Extraction from Novels
comment:
The paper has been accepted by NLPCC2025. 12 pages, 5 figures, 5
  tables
summary:
Relation extraction is a crucial task in natural language processing, with
broad applications in knowledge graph construction and literary analysis.
However, the complex context and implicit expressions in novel texts pose
significant challenges for automatic character relationship extraction. This
study focuses on relation extraction in the novel domain and proposes a method
based on Large Language Models (LLMs). By incorporating relationship dimension
separation, dialogue data construction, and contextual learning strategies, the
proposed method enhances extraction performance. Leveraging dialogue structure
information, it improves the model's ability to understand implicit
relationships and demonstrates strong adaptability in complex contexts.
Additionally, we construct a high-quality Chinese novel relation extraction
dataset to address the lack of labeled resources and support future research.
Experimental results show that our method outperforms traditional baselines
across multiple evaluation metrics and successfully facilitates the automated
construction of character relationship networks in novels.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a method using Large Language Models for extracting character relationships from novels, addressing challenges in complex and implicit contexts, and introduces a new Chinese novel dataset.
AI.motivation: Automatic extraction of character relationships in novels is challenging due to complex context and implicit expressions, and there is a lack of labeled datasets for this task.
AI.method: The method employs Large Language Models enhanced by relationship dimension separation, dialogue data construction, and contextual learning strategies, leveraging dialogue structures to improve understanding of implicit relationships.
AI.result: The method outperforms traditional baselines on multiple evaluation metrics and effectively supports automated construction of character relationship networks in novels.
AI.conclusion: The proposed LLM-based approach with novel data and strategies significantly advances relation extraction in novels, demonstrating strong adaptability and improved performance in complex, implicit contexts.

title: $\textit{Grahak-Nyay:}$ Consumer Grievance Redressal through Large Language Models
summary:
Access to consumer grievance redressal in India is often hindered by
procedural complexity, legal jargon, and jurisdictional challenges. To address
this, we present $\textbf{Grahak-Nyay}$ (Justice-to-Consumers), a chatbot that
streamlines the process using open-source Large Language Models (LLMs) and
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). Grahak-Nyay simplifies legal complexities
through a concise and up-to-date knowledge base. We introduce three novel
datasets: $\textit{GeneralQA}$ (general consumer law), $\textit{SectoralQA}$
(sector-specific knowledge) and $\textit{SyntheticQA}$ (for RAG evaluation),
along with $\textit{NyayChat}$, a dataset of 300 annotated chatbot
conversations. We also introduce $\textit{Judgments}$ data sourced from Indian
Consumer Courts to aid the chatbot in decision making and to enhance user
trust. We also propose $\textbf{HAB}$ metrics ($\textbf{Helpfulness, Accuracy,
Brevity}$) to evaluate chatbot performance. Legal domain experts validated
Grahak-Nyay's effectiveness. Code and datasets will be released.

AI.tldr: Grahak-Nyay is a chatbot designed to simplify consumer grievance redressal in India using open-source LLMs and RAG, supported by novel datasets and evaluated with new metrics.
AI.motivation: Consumer grievance redressal in India is complicated by procedural and legal barriers, making it difficult for consumers to access justice.
AI.method: The authors developed Grahak-Nyay, a chatbot leveraging Large Language Models and Retrieval-Augmented Generation with a concise legal knowledge base, introduced three novel datasets and a dataset of chatbot conversations, and proposed new evaluation metrics (HAB).
AI.result: The chatbot's effectiveness was validated by legal domain experts, and it demonstrated improved accessibility and reliability in handling consumer legal queries.
AI.conclusion: Grahak-Nyay offers a practical, validated tool to streamline consumer grievance redressal in India, with resources and code to be shared for broader use.

title: Building Open-Retrieval Conversational Question Answering Systems by Generating Synthetic Data and Decontextualizing User Questions
summary:
We consider open-retrieval conversational question answering (OR-CONVQA), an
extension of question answering where system responses need to be (i) aware of
dialog history and (ii) grounded in documents (or document fragments) retrieved
per question. Domain-specific OR-CONVQA training datasets are crucial for
real-world applications, but hard to obtain. We propose a pipeline that
capitalizes on the abundance of plain text documents in organizations (e.g.,
product documentation) to automatically produce realistic OR-CONVQA dialogs
with annotations. Similarly to real-world humanannotated OR-CONVQA datasets, we
generate in-dialog question-answer pairs, self-contained (decontextualized,
e.g., no referring expressions) versions of user questions, and propositions
(sentences expressing prominent information from the documents) the system
responses are grounded in. We show how the synthetic dialogs can be used to
train efficient question rewriters that decontextualize user questions,
allowing existing dialog-unaware retrievers to be utilized. The retrieved
information and the decontextualized question are then passed on to an LLM that
generates the system's response.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a pipeline to automatically generate domain-specific training datasets for open-retrieval conversational question answering (OR-CONVQA) using organizational plain text documents, enabling training of question rewriters and improving system responses grounded in retrieved documents.
AI.motivation: Domain-specific OR-CONVQA datasets are rare but essential for real-world applications where responses must understand dialog history and be grounded in retrieved documents.
AI.method: They develop a pipeline that creates realistic synthetic OR-CONVQA dialogs by generating in-dialog Q-A pairs, decontextualized user questions, and grounding propositions from organizational documents, and use these to train question rewriters and support retrieval and response generation via large language models (LLMs).
AI.result: The synthetic data enables training of efficient question rewriters that can decontextualize questions, allowing dialog-unaware retrievers to work effectively, which combined with retrieved information and decontextualized questions, supports response generation by LLMs.
AI.conclusion: Automatically generated synthetic OR-CONVQA dialogs from organizational texts can effectively support training components for open-retrieval conversational QA systems, overcoming data scarcity and improving system grounding and response quality.

title: Emergent Semantics Beyond Token Embeddings: Transformer LMs with Frozen Visual Unicode Representations
summary:
Understanding the locus of semantic representation in large language models
(LLMs) is crucial for interpretability and architectural innovation. The
dominant paradigm posits that trainable input embeddings serve as foundational
"meaning vectors." This paper challenges that view. We construct Transformer
models where the embedding layer is entirely frozen, with vectors derived not
from data, but from the visual structure of Unicode glyphs. These non-semantic,
precomputed visual embeddings are fixed throughout training. Our method is
compatible with any tokenizer, including a novel Unicode-centric tokenizer we
introduce to ensure universal text coverage. Despite the absence of trainable,
semantically initialized embeddings, our models converge, generate coherent
text, and, critically, outperform architecturally identical models with
trainable embeddings on the MMLU reasoning benchmark. We attribute this to
"representational interference" in conventional models, where the embedding
layer is burdened with learning both structural and semantic features. Our
results indicate that high-level semantics are not inherent to input embeddings
but are an emergent property of the Transformer's compositional architecture
and data scale. This reframes the role of embeddings from meaning containers to
structural primitives. We release all code and models to foster further
research.

AI.tldr: The paper challenges the conventional view that trainable input embeddings in LLMs serve as inherent semantic meaning vectors. Instead, it shows that fixing embeddings based on Unicode glyph visuals still enables models to learn semantics through the Transformer architecture itself.
AI.motivation: To question the assumption that semantic meaning resides inherently in input embeddings and explore how semantics are represented within LLMs.
AI.method: Constructed Transformer models with frozen input embeddings derived from Unicode glyph visuals rather than trained semantic embeddings, and introduced a Unicode-centric tokenizer for universal text coverage. Compared performance against models with trainable embeddings.
AI.result: Models with fixed, visually-based embeddings still converged and generated coherent text, outperforming identical models with trainable embeddings on reasoning benchmarks (MMLU), indicating that semantics emerge from the Transformer architecture rather than embeddings.
AI.conclusion: Semantic representations in LLMs are emergent properties of the compositional Transformer architecture and large-scale data rather than inherent to input embeddings, shifting the view of embeddings as structural primitives rather than meaning containers.

title: O_FT@EvalLLM2025 : étude comparative de choix de données et de stratégies d'apprentissage pour l'adaptation de modèles de langue à un domaine
comment: 22 pages + 10 pages appendices, in French language
summary:
This paper presents the work carried out by the O_FT team, joint with Orange
and Ouest-France, on adapting language models to the defense domain as part of
the EvalLLM2025 challenge. This work focused on adapting the
\texttt{Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3} model using classical techniques of continued
pre-training and instruction-tuning. The core of our efforts is based on
collecting, generating, and selecting data for these two stages as well as for
model evaluation. Experiments show that our adapted models have better
domain-specific knowledge and improved domain-specific task processing skills,
along with comparable (or even superior) performance on general knowledge and
skills. Considering the carbon footprint of our adaptations, this work
demonstrates the feasibility of domain adaptation for relatively small models.
  --
  Ce document pr\'esente les travaux r\'ealis\'es par l'\'equipe O_FT conjointe
\`a Orange et Ouest-France sur l'adaptation de mod\`eles de langue au domaine
de la d\'efense dans le cadre du challenge EvalLLM2025. Ces travaux se sont
concentr\'es sur l'adaptation du mod\`ele \texttt{Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3}
avec des techniques classiques de poursuite du pr\'e-entra\^inement et
d'affinage sur instructions. L'essentiel de nos travaux a port\'e sur la
constitution, g\'en\'eration et s\'election de donn\'ees pour ces deux \'etapes
ainsi que pour l'\'evaluation des mod\`eles. Les exp\'eriences montrent que nos
mod\`eles adapt\'es ont de meilleures de connaissances de fond et une meilleure
capacit\'e de traitement de t\^aches sur le domaine de la d\'efense, ainsi que
des performances comparables (voire sup\'erieures) sur des connaissances ou
capacit\'es g\'en\'eralistes. Mis au regard des empreintes carbones de nos
adaptations, ces travaux d\'emontrent ainsi la viabilit\'e de l'adaptation \`a
un domaine de mod\`eles relativement petits.

AI.tldr: The paper details the adaptation of the Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3 language model to the defense domain using continued pre-training and instruction-tuning, achieving improved domain-specific performance while maintaining general capabilities, demonstrating feasible domain adaptation for small models with controlled carbon footprint.
AI.motivation: To enhance language model performance specifically for the defense domain as part of the EvalLLM2025 challenge, addressing the need for domain-specific knowledge and task processing.
AI.method: Utilized continued pre-training and instruction-tuning on the Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3 model, focusing on collecting, generating, and selecting relevant data for both adaptation stages and model evaluation.
AI.result: Adapted models showed better domain-specific knowledge and task performance in defense-related tasks, with comparable or superior results on general knowledge and skills compared to the base model.
AI.conclusion: The study proves the viability of domain adaptation for relatively small models in specialized fields like defense, balancing enhanced domain expertise with environmental considerations such as carbon footprint.

title: SIGIR 2025 -- LiveRAG Challenge Report
summary:
The LiveRAG Challenge at SIGIR 2025, held between March and May 2025,
provided a competitive platform for advancing Retrieval-Augmented Generation
(RAG) technologies. Participants from academia and industry were invited to
develop a RAG-based question-answering system using a fixed corpus
(Fineweb-10BT) and a common open-source LLM (Falcon3-10B-Instruct). The goal
was to facilitate challenging comparisons of retrieval and prompting
strategies. During the Live Challenge Day, 70 teams from 27 different countries
provided answers and supportive information to 500 unseen questions within a
strict two-hour time window. Evaluation was conducted in two stages: first an
automated LLM-as-a-judge approach was used to compute correctness and
faithfulness score, then a manual review of top ranked submissions was
conducted. The finalists were announced on June 12, 2025, with prizes awarded
during the LiveRAG Workshop at SIGIR 2025 in Padua, Italy.

AI.tldr: The LiveRAG Challenge at SIGIR 2025 featured 70 teams developing retrieval-augmented generation QA systems using a fixed corpus and LLM, evaluated by automated and manual methods on 500 unseen questions.
AI.motivation: To advance and benchmark RAG technologies by comparing different retrieval and prompting strategies under a controlled challenge setting.
AI.method: Participants built RAG-based QA systems using the fixed Fineweb-10BT corpus and Falcon3-10B-Instruct LLM, answering 500 unseen questions within two hours; evaluation combined automated LLM judging and manual review.
AI.result: 70 teams from 27 countries competed, providing diverse retrieval and prompting solutions; top submissions were identified for prizes after thorough evaluation.
AI.conclusion: The challenge successfully promoted advancements in RAG QA systems, offering a competitive platform to fairly compare methods and encourage innovation in the field.

title: Co-DETECT: Collaborative Discovery of Edge Cases in Text Classification
summary:
We introduce Co-DETECT (Collaborative Discovery of Edge cases in TExt
ClassificaTion), a novel mixed-initiative annotation framework that integrates
human expertise with automatic annotation guided by large language models
(LLMs). Co-DETECT starts with an initial, sketch-level codebook and dataset
provided by a domain expert, then leverages the LLM to annotate the data and
identify edge cases that are not well described by the initial codebook.
Specifically, Co-DETECT flags challenging examples, induces high-level,
generalizable descriptions of edge cases, and assists user in incorporating
edge case handling rules to improve the codebook. This iterative process
enables more effective handling of nuanced phenomena through compact,
generalizable annotation rules. Extensive user study, qualitative and
quantitative analyses prove the effectiveness of Co-DETECT.

AI.tldr: Co-DETECT is a mixed-initiative annotation framework combining human expertise and large language models to identify and handle edge cases in text classification, improving codebooks iteratively.
AI.motivation: To improve annotation accuracy in text classification by effectively addressing edge cases that are not well described in initial codebooks.
AI.method: Co-DETECT starts with a sketch-level codebook and dataset, uses LLMs to annotate data and flag challenging edge cases, induces generalizable edge case descriptions, and helps users update the codebook iteratively.
AI.result: User studies and analyses demonstrate Co-DETECT's effectiveness in improving annotation quality by better incorporating nuanced edge cases.
AI.conclusion: Integrating human expertise with LLM-guided annotation through Co-DETECT enables more effective and generalizable handling of edge cases in text classification annotation.

title: Verified Language Processing with Hybrid Explainability: A Technical Report
summary:
The volume and diversity of digital information have led to a growing
reliance on Machine Learning techniques, such as Natural Language Processing,
for interpreting and accessing appropriate data. While vector and graph
embeddings represent data for similarity tasks, current state-of-the-art
pipelines lack guaranteed explainability, failing to determine similarity for
given full texts accurately. These considerations can also be applied to
classifiers exploiting generative language models with logical prompts, which
fail to correctly distinguish between logical implication, indifference, and
inconsistency, despite being explicitly trained to recognise the first two
classes. We present a novel pipeline designed for hybrid explainability to
address this. Our methodology combines graphs and logic to produce First-Order
Logic representations, creating machine- and human-readable representations
through Montague Grammar. Preliminary results indicate the effectiveness of
this approach in accurately capturing full text similarity. To the best of our
knowledge, this is the first approach to differentiate between implication,
inconsistency, and indifference for text classification tasks. To address the
limitations of existing approaches, we use three self-contained datasets
annotated for the former classification task to determine the suitability of
these approaches in capturing sentence structure equivalence, logical
connectives, and spatiotemporal reasoning. We also use these data to compare
the proposed method with language models pre-trained for detecting sentence
entailment. The results show that the proposed method outperforms
state-of-the-art models, indicating that natural language understanding cannot
be easily generalised by training over extensive document corpora. This work
offers a step toward more transparent and reliable Information Retrieval from
extensive textual data.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a novel hybrid explainability pipeline combining graphs and logic to generate First-Order Logic representations for accurate full-text similarity detection. It outperforms current models in distinguishing logical relations such as implication, inconsistency, and indifference in text classification.
AI.motivation: Current machine learning and language model pipelines lack guaranteed explainability and fail to accurately distinguish between logical relationships in text, limiting reliable similarity detection and classification.
AI.method: A hybrid approach combining graph and logical methods to produce First-Order Logic representations of text, using Montague Grammar for readable outputs. The approach is evaluated on three annotated datasets and compared against pre-trained language models for entailment detection.
AI.result: Preliminary results show the proposed method effectively captures full-text similarity and logically distinguishes between implication, inconsistency, and indifference, outperforming state-of-the-art language models.
AI.conclusion: This work demonstrates that natural language understanding benefits from integrating symbolic logic with structural representations and challenges the sufficiency of large-scale training corpora alone, moving towards more transparent and reliable information retrieval.

title: An Evaluation of Large Language Models on Text Summarization Tasks Using Prompt Engineering Techniques
comment:
This manuscript is an extended version of the work accepted for
  publication in the International Journal of Advanced Computer Science and
  Applications (IJACSA), Volume 16, Issue 6, June 2025
summary:
Large Language Models (LLMs) continue to advance natural language processing
with their ability to generate human-like text across a range of tasks. Despite
the remarkable success of LLMs in Natural Language Processing (NLP), their
performance in text summarization across various domains and datasets has not
been comprehensively evaluated. At the same time, the ability to summarize text
effectively without relying on extensive training data has become a crucial
bottleneck. To address these issues, we present a systematic evaluation of six
LLMs across four datasets: CNN/Daily Mail and NewsRoom (news), SAMSum (dialog),
and ArXiv (scientific). By leveraging prompt engineering techniques including
zero-shot and in-context learning, our study evaluates the performance using
the ROUGE and BERTScore metrics. In addition, a detailed analysis of inference
times is conducted to better understand the trade-off between summarization
quality and computational efficiency. For Long documents, introduce a
sentence-based chunking strategy that enables LLMs with shorter context windows
to summarize extended inputs in multiple stages. The findings reveal that while
LLMs perform competitively on news and dialog tasks, their performance on long
scientific documents improves significantly when aided by chunking strategies.
In addition, notable performance variations were observed based on model
parameters, dataset properties, and prompt design. These results offer
actionable insights into how different LLMs behave across task types,
contributing to ongoing research in efficient, instruction-based NLP systems.

AI.tldr: This paper systematically evaluates six large language models on text summarization tasks across different domains, using prompt engineering and chunking strategies to improve performance, especially on long documents.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to comprehensively assess LLM performance in text summarization across various datasets and domains, addressing the challenge of effective summarization without large training data.
AI.method: The study evaluates six LLMs on four datasets using zero-shot and in-context prompt engineering, employs ROUGE and BERTScore for metrics, analyzes inference times, and introduces a sentence-based chunking method for long documents.
AI.result: LLMs perform competitively on news and dialog summarization; chunking significantly improves results on long scientific documents; performance varies with model size, dataset, and prompt design.
AI.conclusion: The findings provide insights into LLM summarization behavior across tasks, highlighting strategies like prompt engineering and chunking to enhance efficiency and quality in instruction-based NLP systems.

title: SMART: Simulated Students Aligned with Item Response Theory for Question Difficulty Prediction
summary:
Item (question) difficulties play a crucial role in educational assessments,
enabling accurate and efficient assessment of student abilities and
personalization to maximize learning outcomes. Traditionally, estimating item
difficulties can be costly, requiring real students to respond to items,
followed by fitting an item response theory (IRT) model to get item difficulty
estimates. This approach cannot be applied to the cold-start setting for
previously unseen items either. In this work, we present SMART (Simulated
Students Aligned with IRT), a novel method for aligning simulated students with
instructed ability, which can then be used in simulations to predict the
difficulty of open-ended items. We achieve this alignment using direct
preference optimization (DPO), where we form preference pairs based on how
likely responses are under a ground-truth IRT model. We perform a simulation by
