regardless of demographics or social status? While they offer great potential
for scalable support, LLMs may also reproduce societal biases embedded in their
training data, risking the integrity of libraries' commitment to equitable
service. To address this concern, we evaluate whether LLMs differentiate
responses across user identities by prompting six state-of-the-art LLMs to
assist patrons differing in sex, race/ethnicity, and institutional role. We
found no evidence of differentiation by race or ethnicity, and only minor
evidence of stereotypical bias against women in one model. LLMs demonstrated
nuanced accommodation of institutional roles through the use of linguistic
choices related to formality, politeness, and domain-specific vocabularies,
reflecting professional norms rather than discriminatory treatment. These
findings suggest that current LLMs show a promising degree of readiness to
support equitable and contextually appropriate communication in academic
library reference services.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates whether state-of-the-art large language models (LLMs) treat users equitably in academic library virtual reference services by testing responses across diverse user identities. It found minimal biases and appropriate role-based language use, indicating LLMs’ readiness for equitable support.
AI.motivation: To determine if LLMs can provide equitable virtual reference services without perpetuating societal biases related to demographics or social status.
AI.method: Prompted six state-of-the-art LLMs to assist library patrons varying by sex, race/ethnicity, and institutional roles, analyzed whether responses differed based on these identities, focusing on language formality and bias indicators.
AI.result: No significant differentiation by race or ethnicity was found; only minor stereotypical bias against women was detected in one model; language adapted appropriately to institutional roles reflecting professional norms rather than discrimination.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs show promising readiness to support equitable and contextually appropriate communication in academic library reference services, with minimal problematic biases detected.

title: No Language Data Left Behind: A Comparative Study of CJK Language Datasets in the Hugging Face Ecosystem
summary:
Recent advances in Natural Language Processing (NLP) have underscored the
crucial role of high-quality datasets in building large language models (LLMs).
However, while extensive resources and analyses exist for English, the
landscape for East Asian languages - particularly Chinese, Japanese, and Korean
(CJK) - remains fragmented and underexplored, despite these languages together
serving over 1.6 billion speakers. To address this gap, we investigate the
HuggingFace ecosystem from a cross-linguistic perspective, focusing on how
cultural norms, research environments, and institutional practices shape
dataset availability and quality. Drawing on more than 3,300 datasets, we
employ quantitative and qualitative methods to examine how these factors drive
distinct creation and curation patterns across Chinese, Japanese, and Korean
NLP communities. Our findings highlight the large-scale and often
institution-driven nature of Chinese datasets, grassroots community-led
development in Korean NLP, and an entertainment- and subculture-focused
emphasis on Japanese collections. By uncovering these patterns, we reveal
practical strategies for enhancing dataset documentation, licensing clarity,
and cross-lingual resource sharing - ultimately guiding more effective and
culturally attuned LLM development in East Asia. We conclude by discussing best
practices for future dataset curation and collaboration, aiming to strengthen
resource development across all three languages.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes dataset availability and quality for Chinese, Japanese, and Korean NLP in the HuggingFace ecosystem, revealing distinct cultural and institutional influences and proposing strategies for better dataset management and collaboration.
AI.motivation: To address the fragmented and underexplored state of NLP datasets for East Asian languages (CJK) despite their large speaker base and the importance of high-quality datasets for LLM development.
AI.method: Quantitative and qualitative analysis of over 3,300 NLP datasets in HuggingFace, examining cultural norms, research environments, and institutional practices shaping dataset creation and curation across Chinese, Japanese, and Korean communities.
AI.result: Identified Chinese datasets as large-scale and institution-driven, Korean datasets as grassroots community-led, and Japanese datasets focused on entertainment and subculture. Highlighted differences in dataset documentation and licensing practices.
AI.conclusion: Provides practical recommendations for improving dataset documentation, licensing clarity, and cross-lingual sharing to promote culturally attuned and effective LLM development in East Asian languages, and suggests best practices for future dataset curation and collaboration.

title: HatePRISM: Policies, Platforms, and Research Integration. Advancing NLP for Hate Speech Proactive Mitigation
comment: arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2406.19543
summary:
Despite regulations imposed by nations and social media platforms, e.g.
(Government of India, 2021; European Parliament and Council of the European
Union, 2022), inter alia, hateful content persists as a significant challenge.
Existing approaches primarily rely on reactive measures such as blocking or
suspending offensive messages, with emerging strategies focusing on proactive
measurements like detoxification and counterspeech. In our work, which we call
HatePRISM, we conduct a comprehensive examination of hate speech regulations
and strategies from three perspectives: country regulations, social platform
policies, and NLP research datasets. Our findings reveal significant
inconsistencies in hate speech definitions and moderation practices across
jurisdictions and platforms, alongside a lack of alignment with research
efforts. Based on these insights, we suggest ideas and research direction for
further exploration of a unified framework for automated hate speech moderation
incorporating diverse strategies.

AI.tldr: HatePRISM analyzes hate speech regulations and moderation strategies across countries, social media platforms, and NLP research, uncovering inconsistencies and misalignments, and proposes directions towards a unified automated hate speech moderation framework.
AI.motivation: Hateful content remains prevalent despite regulatory and platform efforts, driven by inconsistencies in definitions and moderation practices, urging a comprehensive examination and unified approach.
AI.method: Comprehensive analysis of hate speech regulations, social platform policies, and NLP datasets to identify inconsistencies and gaps in current hate speech moderation strategies.
AI.result: Found significant inconsistencies in hate speech definitions and moderation practices across jurisdictions and platforms, and a lack of alignment between research efforts and real-world policies.
AI.conclusion: Proposes further research towards a unified automated hate speech moderation framework that integrates diverse strategies for more effective management of hateful content.

title: Large Language Models' Varying Accuracy in Recognizing Risk-Promoting and Health-Supporting Sentiments in Public Health Discourse: The Cases of HPV Vaccination and Heated Tobacco Products
summary:
Machine learning methods are increasingly applied to analyze health-related
public discourse based on large-scale data, but questions remain regarding
their ability to accurately detect different types of health sentiments.
Especially, Large Language Models (LLMs) have gained attention as a powerful
technology, yet their accuracy and feasibility in capturing different opinions
and perspectives on health issues are largely unexplored. Thus, this research
examines how accurate the three prominent LLMs (GPT, Gemini, and LLAMA) are in
detecting risk-promoting versus health-supporting sentiments across two
critical public health topics: Human Papillomavirus (HPV) vaccination and
heated tobacco products (HTPs). Drawing on data from Facebook and Twitter, we
curated multiple sets of messages supporting or opposing recommended health
behaviors, supplemented with human annotations as the gold standard for
sentiment classification. The findings indicate that all three LLMs generally
demonstrate substantial accuracy in classifying risk-promoting and
health-supporting sentiments, although notable discrepancies emerge by
platform, health issue, and model type. Specifically, models often show higher
accuracy for risk-promoting sentiment on Facebook, whereas health-supporting
messages on Twitter are more accurately detected. An additional analysis also
shows the challenges LLMs face in reliably detecting neutral messages. These
results highlight the importance of carefully selecting and validating language
models for public health analyses, particularly given potential biases in
training data that may lead LLMs to overestimate or underestimate the
prevalence of certain perspectives.

AI.tldr: This study evaluates the accuracy of GPT, Gemini, and LLAMA in identifying health-related sentiments on HPV vaccination and heated tobacco products using Facebook and Twitter data, revealing notable variation by platform, health topic, and model type.
AI.motivation: To assess the feasibility and accuracy of Large Language Models in detecting nuanced health sentiments, particularly the distinction between risk-promoting and health-supporting views, which remains underexplored.
AI.method: They curated Facebook and Twitter messages on HPV vaccination and HTPs, annotated by humans as a gold standard, then tested sentiment classification accuracy of GPT, Gemini, and LLAMA across these datasets.
AI.result: All three LLMs showed substantial accuracy but differed by platform and sentiment type; models better detected risk-promoting sentiment on Facebook and health-supporting sentiment on Twitter and struggled with neutral message classification.
AI.conclusion: Selecting and validating LLMs carefully is critical for public health discourse analysis due to biases and varying performance, emphasizing cautious interpretation when using LLMs for sentiment detection in health topics.

title: Does Learning Mathematical Problem-Solving Generalize to Broader Reasoning?
summary:
There has been a growing interest in enhancing the mathematical
problem-solving (MPS) capabilities of large language models. While the majority
of research efforts concentrate on creating specialized models to solve
mathematical problems, it remains unknown how learning mathematical
problem-solving generalizes to help develop other reasoning abilities. In this
paper, we present an empirical investigation into the generalization potential
of various MPS training approaches, such as continual pretraining, instruction
tuning, and rule-based reinforcement learning across various data sources,
including both short and long chain-of-thought (CoT) samples. Evaluation on 5
mathematical and 8 general reasoning benchmarks show that continual pretraining
on math text is able to generalize to general reasoning tasks to some extent.
In constrast, instruction tuning on conventional, short MPS samples provides
limited benefits and, in many cases, even impairs generalization performance.
Notably, training with long CoT responses for MPS samples and incorporating
rule-based reinforcement learning on MPS queries exhibit distinct behavior,
significantly enhancing generalization by extending the model's reasoning
processes into other domains. These results suggest that traditional approaches
to learning MPS with short reasoning chains largely fail to achieve robust
generalization. However, the emerging paradigm of longer reasoning chains,
coupled with self-reflection, offers a promising direction for improving
generalized reasoning abilities through learning from specialized domains.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how training large language models on mathematical problem-solving transfers to broader reasoning tasks, finding that methods involving longer reasoning chains and rule-based reinforcement learning improve generalization better than short chain training.
AI.motivation: To understand whether and how learning mathematical problem-solving capabilities in large language models generalizes to improve other reasoning skills.
AI.method: Empirically evaluated different MPS training strategies—continual pretraining, instruction tuning, and rule-based reinforcement learning—using various data sources including short and long chain-of-thought samples across multiple reasoning benchmarks.
AI.result: Continual pretraining on math text offers some general reasoning improvements, instruction tuning on short MPS samples offers limited or negative effects, while training with long reasoning chains and rule-based reinforcement learning significantly enhance generalization to other reasoning tasks.
AI.conclusion: Traditional MPS training with short reasoning chains does not robustly generalize; however, using longer reasoning chains and self-reflective methods provides a promising avenue for improving general reasoning via specialized domain training.

title: SpiritRAG: A Q&A System for Religion and Spirituality in the United Nations Archive
summary:
Religion and spirituality (R/S) are complex and highly domain-dependent
concepts which have long confounded researchers and policymakers. Due to their
context-specificity, R/S are difficult to operationalize in conventional
archival search strategies, particularly when datasets are very large, poorly
accessible, and marked by information noise. As a result, considerable time
investments and specialist knowledge is often needed to extract actionable
insights related to R/S from general archival sources, increasing reliance on
published literature and manual desk reviews. To address this challenge, we
present SpiritRAG, an interactive Question Answering (Q&A) system based on
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). Built using 7,500 United Nations (UN)
resolution documents related to R/S in the domains of health and education,
SpiritRAG allows researchers and policymakers to conduct complex,
context-sensitive database searches of very large datasets using an easily
accessible, chat-based web interface. SpiritRAG is lightweight to deploy and
leverages both UN documents and user provided documents as source material. A
pilot test and evaluation with domain experts on 100 manually composed
questions demonstrates the practical value and usefulness of SpiritRAG.

AI.tldr: SpiritRAG is an interactive QA system using Retrieval-Augmented Generation designed to facilitate complex, context-sensitive searches about religion and spirituality in large, noisy datasets like UN documents, enabling easier access for researchers and policymakers.
AI.motivation: Religion and spirituality are complex, context-dependent concepts difficult to operationalize in large archival datasets, requiring extensive time and expertise to extract relevant insights, creating reliance on manual reviews and published literature.
AI.method: The authors developed SpiritRAG, a chat-based web interface system leveraging Retrieval-Augmented Generation on 7,500 UN resolution documents related to R/S in health and education, allowing integration of additional user documents for context-sensitive searches.
AI.result: Pilot testing with domain experts on 100 manually composed questions showed SpiritRAG's practical value and usefulness in facilitating complex, context-aware queries in large, unstructured datasets.
AI.conclusion: SpiritRAG effectively addresses challenges in operationalizing R/S research from large archival sources by providing an accessible, lightweight QA system that improves the efficiency and scope of contextual search for researchers and policymakers.

title: THM@SimpleText 2025 -- Task 1.1: Revisiting Text Simplification based on Complex Terms for Non-Experts
summary:
Scientific text is complex as it contains technical terms by definition.
Simplifying such text for non-domain experts enhances accessibility of
innovation and information. Politicians could be enabled to understand new
findings on topics on which they intend to pass a law, or family members of
seriously ill patients could read about clinical trials. The SimpleText CLEF
Lab focuses on exactly this problem of simplification of scientific text. Task
1.1 of the 2025 edition specifically handles the simplification of complex
sentences, so very short texts with little context. To tackle this task we
investigate the identification of complex terms in sentences which are
rephrased using small Gemini and OpenAI large language models for non-expert
readers.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses simplifying complex scientific text to aid non-experts' understanding by identifying complex terms in sentences and rephrasing them using small Gemini and OpenAI large language models.
AI.motivation: Scientific texts contain technical terms that are difficult for non-experts to understand. Simplifying such texts improves accessibility for politicians, patients' families, and other non-domain experts.
AI.method: The paper investigates identifying complex terms in sentences and then rephrasing these using small Gemini and OpenAI large language models to generate simpler versions for non-expert readers.
AI.result: The method facilitates the simplification of short, complex sentences by effectively rephrasing them with language models, making the texts more accessible to non-domain experts.
AI.conclusion: Using language models to identify and simplify complex terms in scientific texts can enhance comprehension for non-experts, supporting the aims of the SimpleText CLEF Lab task.

title: MOMENTS: A Comprehensive Multimodal Benchmark for Theory of Mind
summary:
Understanding Theory of Mind is essential for building socially intelligent
multimodal agents capable of perceiving and interpreting human behavior. We
introduce MOMENTS (Multimodal Mental States), a comprehensive benchmark
designed to assess the ToM capabilities of multimodal large language models
(LLMs) through realistic, narrative-rich scenarios presented in short films.
MOMENTS includes over 2,344 multiple-choice questions spanning seven distinct
ToM categories. The benchmark features long video context windows and realistic
social interactions that provide deeper insight into characters' mental states.
While the visual modality generally enhances model performance, current systems
still struggle to integrate it effectively, underscoring the need for further
research into AI's multimodal understanding of human behavior.

AI.tldr: MOMENTS is a benchmark to evaluate multimodal large language models on Theory of Mind using short films and multiple-choice questions.
AI.motivation: To build socially intelligent agents, understanding Theory of Mind in multimodal contexts is crucial.
AI.method: Created MOMENTS benchmark with 2,344 questions in seven Theory of Mind categories using narrative-rich short films showing social interactions.
AI.result: Visual information helps model performance, but models still struggle with effective multimodal integration.
AI.conclusion: Current multimodal LLMs need further improvement to better understand human mental states in realistic social contexts.

title: RAT: Bridging RNN Efficiency and Attention Accuracy in Language Modeling
summary:
Transformers have become the cornerstone of modern large-scale language
models; however, their dependence on softmax attention poses a major
computational bottleneck, particularly in long-context settings. In this work,
rather than following prevalent approaches such as linear attention (or SSMs)
and local attention, we introduce an intermediate design called \rat between
recurrence and attention mechanisms. It partitions the input into chunks,
applies a simple linear recurrence within each chunk to capture local
dependencies, and then performs softmax attention across chunks to model
long-range interactions. By adjusting the size of the chunk, \rat enables
flexible trade-offs, combining the strengths of RNN and attention. Empirically,
with a chunk size of 16, the \rat layer achieves a \(7\times\) improvement in
training speed with 100K token sequences and \(9\times\) in generation at 4K
sequence length, while maintaining similar or sometimes even better accuracy
compared to standard attention. We demonstrate this by training 1.3B parameter
models from scratch and performing large-scale evaluations, including short-
and long-context benchmarks, as well as supervised fine-tuning~(SFT). We
further propose a hybrid architecture that interleaves \rat with local
attention. By combining efficient long-range modeling with strong local
interactions, this hybrid design not only improves inference speed and reduces
cache memory usage compared to attention, but also consistently enhances
performance, for example, achieving an average 1 point gain in commonsense
reasoning tasks, up to 4 points on code tasks, and a 1 point Rouge-L increase
in a summarization SFT task. Code is available at
https://github.com/CLAIRE-Labo/RAT

AI.tldr: This paper introduces RAT, a novel model combining linear recurrence and softmax attention by chunking inputs for efficient long-context language model training and inference.
AI.motivation: To overcome computational bottlenecks of softmax attention in long-context settings, aiming for faster training and generation without sacrificing accuracy.
AI.method: RAT partitions inputs into chunks, applies linear recurrence within each chunk for local dependencies, and uses softmax attention across chunks for long-range interactions, adjustable by chunk size. The authors also propose a hybrid model combining RAT and local attention.
AI.result: With a chunk size of 16, RAT yields up to 7x faster training on 100K token sequences and 9x faster generation on 4K sequences, maintaining or improving accuracy. The hybrid RAT-local attention model improves inference speed, reduces cache memory, and enhances performance on commonsense reasoning, code tasks, and summarization.
AI.conclusion: RAT provides an effective middle ground between RNNs and attention mechanisms, enabling efficient and scalable long-context language modeling with strong performance gains across tasks.

title: GradOT: Training-free Gradient-preserving Offsite-tuning for Large Language Models
summary:
The rapid growth of large language models (LLMs) with traditional centralized
fine-tuning emerges as a key technique for adapting these models to
domain-specific challenges, yielding privacy risks for both model and data
owners. One promising solution, called offsite-tuning (OT), is proposed to
address these challenges, where a weaker emulator is compressed from the
original model and further fine-tuned with adapter to enhance privacy. However,
the existing OT-based methods require high computational costs and lack
theoretical analysis. This paper introduces a novel OT approach based on
gradient-preserving compression, named GradOT. By analyzing the OT problem
through the lens of optimization, we propose a method that selectively applies
compression techniques such as rank compression and channel pruning, preserving
the gradients of fine-tuned adapters while ensuring privacy. Extensive
experiments demonstrate that our approach surpasses existing OT methods, both
in terms of privacy protection and model performance. Our method provides a
theoretical foundation for OT and offers a practical, training-free solution
for offsite-tuning of large-scale LLMs.

AI.tldr: GradOT is a novel offsite-tuning method that compresses large language models by preserving gradients during adapter fine-tuning, enhancing privacy while reducing computational cost.
AI.motivation: Traditional fine-tuning of large language models poses privacy risks for model and data owners, and existing offsite-tuning methods are computationally expensive and lack theoretical grounding.
AI.method: GradOT applies gradient-preserving compression techniques like rank compression and channel pruning to a weaker emulator derived from the original model, fine-tuning adapters while maintaining privacy and gradient integrity.
AI.result: GradOT outperforms existing offsite-tuning approaches by providing better privacy protection and improved model performance with lower computational demands.
AI.conclusion: The proposed GradOT method offers a theoretically grounded, practical, and training-free solution for privacy-preserving offsite-tuning of large-scale language models.

title: Think Twice Before You Judge: Mixture of Dual Reasoning Experts for Multimodal Sarcasm Detection
summary:
Multimodal sarcasm detection has attracted growing interest due to the rise
of multimedia posts on social media. Understanding sarcastic image-text posts
often requires external contextual knowledge, such as cultural references or
commonsense reasoning. However, existing models struggle to capture the deeper
rationale behind sarcasm, relying mainly on shallow cues like image captions or
object-attribute pairs from images. To address this, we propose \textbf{MiDRE}
(\textbf{Mi}xture of \textbf{D}ual \textbf{R}easoning \textbf{E}xperts), which
integrates an internal reasoning expert for detecting incongruities within the
image-text pair and an external reasoning expert that utilizes structured
rationales generated via Chain-of-Thought prompting to a Large Vision-Language
Model. An adaptive gating mechanism dynamically weighs the two experts,
selecting the most relevant reasoning path. Experiments on two benchmark
datasets show that MiDRE achieves superior performance over baselines. Various
qualitative analyses highlight the crucial role of external rationales,
revealing that even when they are occasionally noisy, they provide valuable
cues that guide the model toward a better understanding of sarcasm.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses multimodal sarcasm detection by proposing MiDRE, a model that combines internal and external reasoning experts to better understand sarcastic image-text posts.
AI.motivation: Existing models for multimodal sarcasm detection rely on shallow cues and fail to capture deeper rationale, often needing external contextual knowledge like cultural or commonsense reasoning.
AI.method: MiDRE integrates a mixture of two reasoning experts: an internal expert for detecting incongruities in image-text pairs, and an external expert that uses structured rationales generated via Chain-of-Thought prompting of a Large Vision-Language Model, with an adaptive gating mechanism to weigh their contributions.
AI.result: MiDRE outperforms baseline models on two benchmark datasets, demonstrating the effectiveness of combining internal and external reasoning. Qualitative analyses show external rationales help better understand sarcasm despite occasional noise.
AI.conclusion: The proposed dual reasoning expert model, MiDRE, effectively improves multimodal sarcasm detection by capturing deeper rationale through external contextual knowledge, leading to superior performance and robust understanding of sarcasm.

title: Dual Modality-Aware Gated Prompt Tuning for Few-Shot Multimodal Sarcasm Detection
summary:
The widespread use of multimodal content on social media has heightened the
need for effective sarcasm detection to improve opinion mining. However,
existing models rely heavily on large annotated datasets, making them less
suitable for real-world scenarios where labeled data is scarce. This motivates
the need to explore the problem in a few-shot setting. To this end, we
introduce DMDP (Deep Modality-Disentangled Prompt Tuning), a novel framework
for few-shot multimodal sarcasm detection. Unlike prior methods that use
shallow, unified prompts across modalities, DMDP employs gated,
modality-specific deep prompts for text and visual encoders. These prompts are
injected across multiple layers to enable hierarchical feature learning and
better capture diverse sarcasm types. To enhance intra-modal learning, we
incorporate a prompt-sharing mechanism across layers, allowing the model to
aggregate both low-level and high-level semantic cues. Additionally, a
cross-modal prompt alignment module enables nuanced interactions between image
and text representations, improving the model's ability to detect subtle
sarcastic intent. Experiments on two public datasets demonstrate DMDP's
superior performance in both few-shot and extremely low-resource settings.
Further cross-dataset evaluations show that DMDP generalizes well across
domains, consistently outperforming baseline methods.

AI.tldr: DMDP is a novel framework for few-shot multimodal sarcasm detection that uses modality-specific deep prompts and cross-modal alignment to improve performance.
AI.motivation: Existing sarcasm detection models depend on large annotated datasets, which are scarce in real-world scenarios, motivating exploration in few-shot settings.
AI.method: DMDP employs gated, modality-specific deep prompts for text and visual encoders, prompt-sharing across layers for intra-modal learning, and cross-modal prompt alignment for nuanced image-text interaction.
AI.result: Experiments show DMDP outperforms baselines on public datasets in few-shot and extremely low-resource scenarios and generalizes well across different domains.
AI.conclusion: DMDP is effective for multimodal sarcasm detection in scarce data settings, leveraging deep prompts and cross-modal alignment for improved generalization and detection accuracy.

title: Unveiling the Potential of Diffusion Large Language Model in Controllable Generation
summary:
Diffusion models, originally developed for image generation, have emerged as
a promising alternative to autoregressive large language models (LLMs). We
present a theoretical analysis comparing autoregressive and masked diffusion
LLMs, revealing that the intrinsic bidirectional attention mechanism of
diffusion LLMs (dLLMs) enables superior context modeling and generation
controllability. However, existing dLLM applications face significant
challenges in controllable generation: the native multi-step denoising process
exhibits high sensitivity to sequence length, elevated hallucination rates, and
prohibitive inference costs without specialized optimizations. To address these
limitations, we propose \textbf{S}elf-adaptive \textbf{S}chema
\textbf{S}caffolding ($S^3$), a novel framework that enables dLLMs to generate
structured outputs (e.g., JSON) while maintaining semantic fidelity and
accelerating inference. Our approach injects the target schema structure into
the output context, reducing unnecessary computation while improving
controllability. Extensive experiments demonstrate that $S^3$ achieves
substantial improvements: 65\% increase in structural adherence, 48\%
enhancement in content fidelity, and 17\% reduction in hallucination rates
compared to baseline. These results establish both theoretical foundations and
practical pathways for deploying diffusion models in controllable text
generation tasks. Code and data will be publicly released.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces S³, a framework enhancing diffusion language models (dLLMs) for structured, controllable text generation, improving adherence, fidelity, and reducing hallucinations efficiently.
AI.motivation: Existing diffusion language models face challenges in controllable generation, including sensitivity to sequence length, hallucinations, and high inference costs.
AI.method: They propose Self-adaptive Schema Scaffolding (S³), injecting target schema into output context to reduce computation and improve control.
AI.result: S³ achieves a 65% increase in structural adherence, 48% better content fidelity, and 17% less hallucination versus baselines, speeding inference.
AI.conclusion: The work provides theoretical insights and practical methods for reliable, efficient, and controllable text generation using diffusion models.

title: AdS: Adapter-state Sharing Framework for Multimodal Sarcasm Detection
summary:
The growing prevalence of multimodal image-text sarcasm on social media poses
challenges for opinion mining, especially under resource constraints. Existing
approaches rely on full fine-tuning of large pre-trained models, making them
unsuitable for low-resource settings. While recent parameter-efficient
fine-tuning (PEFT) methods offer promise, their off-the-shelf use underperforms
on complex tasks like sarcasm detection. We propose AdS (Adapter-State
Sharing), a lightweight framework built on CLIP that inserts adapters only in
the upper layers and introduces a novel adapter-state sharing mechanism, where
textual adapters guide visual ones. This design promotes efficient cross-modal
learning while preserving low-level unimodal representations. Experiments on
two public benchmarks demonstrate that AdS achieves state-of-the-art results
using significantly fewer trainable parameters than existing PEFT and full
fine-tuning approaches.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses detecting multimodal image-text sarcasm on social media efficiently in resource-constrained settings by proposing AdS, a parameter-efficient fine-tuning framework that inserts adapters in upper layers and shares adapter states between text and visual modalities, achieving state-of-the-art results with fewer parameters.
AI.motivation: Multimodal sarcasm detection is challenging for opinion mining and existing full fine-tuning approaches are resource-intensive and unsuitable for low-resource environments. Parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods underperform on this complex task.
AI.method: The authors propose AdS, a lightweight framework based on CLIP that inserts adapters only in upper layers and introduces adapter-state sharing where textual adapters guide visual adapters to facilitate efficient cross-modal learning without disturbing low-level unimodal features.
AI.result: AdS achieves state-of-the-art performance on two public multimodal sarcasm benchmarks while using significantly fewer trainable parameters compared to existing PEFT and full fine-tuning methods.
AI.conclusion: AdS effectively balances performance and parameter efficiency for multimodal sarcasm detection, demonstrating that adapter-state sharing and selective adapter insertion can improve cross-modal learning under resource constraints.

title: DP-Fusion: Token-Level Differentially Private Inference for Large Language Models
comment:
Our code and data are publicly available here:
  https://github.com/MBZUAI-Trustworthy-ML/DP-Fusion-DPI
summary:
Large language models (LLMs) can leak sensitive information from their
context through generated outputs, either accidentally or when prompted
adversarially. Existing defenses that aim to preserve context privacy during
inference either lack formal guarantees or suffer from a poor utility/privacy
trade-off. We propose DP-Fusion, a token-level Differentially Private Inference
(DPI) mechanism that provably bounds how much an LLM's outputs reveal about
sensitive tokens in its context. We demonstrate DPI through the task of
document privatization, where the goal is to paraphrase documents so that
sensitive content (e.g., Personally Identifiable Information, PII) cannot be
reliably inferred, while still preserving the overall utility of the text. This
is controlled by a parameter $\epsilon$: $\epsilon=0$ hides PII entirely, while
higher values trade off privacy for improved paraphrase quality. DP-Fusion
works as follows: (i) partition sensitive tokens into disjoint privacy groups,
(ii) run the LLM once per group, and (iii) blend the output distributions so
that the final output remains within a fixed statistical distance of the
baseline distribution produced when no privacy group is revealed. This approach
allows fine-grained control over the privacy/utility trade-off but requires
multiple LLM forward passes.

AI.tldr: DP-Fusion is a token-level Differentially Private Inference mechanism that ensures large language models do not reveal sensitive information in their outputs, balancing privacy and utility during document paraphrasing.
AI.motivation: Large language models can unintentionally leak sensitive information from their input context, and existing privacy defenses either lack formal guarantees or degrade output quality significantly.
AI.method: DP-Fusion partitions sensitive tokens into disjoint privacy groups, runs the LLM separately for each group, and blends the outputs to maintain differential privacy guarantees while controlling privacy-utility trade-off through a parameter epsilon.
AI.result: DP-Fusion demonstrates controlled document privatization by paraphrasing documents to hide sensitive content such as PII, with privacy rigorously bounded and utility preserved in the text depending on epsilon values.
AI.conclusion: The proposed DP-Fusion mechanism provides a provably private inference method that carefully balances privacy and utility in LLM outputs, achieved through multiple forward passes and output blending.

title: Nile-Chat: Egyptian Language Models for Arabic and Latin Scripts
summary:
We introduce Nile-Chat-4B, 3x4B-A6B, and 12B, a collection of LLMs for
Egyptian dialect, uniquely designed to understand and generate texts written in
both Arabic and Latin scripts. Specifically, with Nile-Chat-3x4B-A6B, we
introduce a novel language adaptation approach by leveraging the
Branch-Train-MiX strategy to merge script-specialized experts, into a single
MoE model. Our Nile-Chat models significantly outperform leading multilingual
and Arabic LLMs, such as LLaMa, Jais, and ALLaM, on our newly introduced
Egyptian evaluation benchmarks, which span both understanding and generative
tasks. Notably, our 12B model yields a 14.4% performance gain over
Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct on Latin-script benchmarks. All our resources are publicly
available. We believe this work presents a comprehensive methodology for
adapting LLMs to dual-script languages, addressing an often overlooked aspect
in modern LLM development.

AI.tldr: Nile-Chat series are LLMs tailored for Egyptian Arabic dialect in both Arabic and Latin scripts, outperforming existing models on new Egyptian benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To develop LLMs that effectively understand and generate text in Egyptian dialects across Arabic and Latin scripts, an underexplored challenge in current multilingual LLMs.
AI.method: Introduced a Branch-Train-MiX strategy to merge script-specialized expert models into a single Mixture of Experts (MoE) model, especially with Nile-Chat-3x4B-A6B.
AI.result: Nile-Chat models outperform prominent multilingual and Arabic LLMs including LLaMa, Jais, ALLaM, and exceed Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct by 14.4% on Latin-script benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The work offers a robust approach to adapting LLMs for dual-script languages, significantly improving performance and providing publicly available models and benchmarks.

title: PRIME: Large Language Model Personalization with Cognitive Memory and Thought Processes
summary:
Large language model (LLM) personalization aims to align model outputs with
individuals' unique preferences and opinions. While recent efforts have
implemented various personalization methods, a unified theoretical framework
that can systematically understand the drivers of effective personalization is
still lacking. In this work, we integrate the well-established cognitive
dual-memory model into LLM personalization, by mirroring episodic memory to
historical user engagements and semantic memory to long-term, evolving user
beliefs. Specifically, we systematically investigate memory instantiations and
introduce a unified framework, PRIME, using episodic and semantic memory
mechanisms. We further augment PRIME with a novel personalized thinking
capability inspired by the slow thinking strategy. Moreover, recognizing the
absence of suitable benchmarks, we introduce a dataset using Change My View
(CMV) from Reddit, specifically designed to evaluate long-context
personalization. Extensive experiments validate PRIME's effectiveness across
both long- and short-context scenarios. Further analysis confirms that PRIME
effectively captures dynamic personalization beyond mere popularity biases.

AI.tldr: The paper presents PRIME, a unified framework for large language model personalization using episodic and semantic memory inspired by cognitive dual-memory theory, and introduces a new benchmark dataset for evaluation.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of a unified theoretical framework for effectively personalizing LLM outputs to individual user preferences and opinions.
AI.method: Integrate cognitive dual-memory model into LLM personalization by using episodic memory to reflect historical user engagements and semantic memory for long-term user beliefs; develop PRIME framework and augment it with personalized slow thinking capability; create a new dataset from Reddit's CMV for evaluation.
AI.result: PRIME demonstrates effectiveness in both long- and short-context personalization tasks, capturing dynamic personalization beyond popularity biases as validated by extensive experiments.
AI.conclusion: PRIME provides a systematic and effective approach to LLM personalization, leveraging cognitive memory models and new benchmarks to better align outputs with individual user preferences.

title: Retain or Reframe? A Computational Framework for the Analysis of Framing in News Articles and Reader Comments
summary:
When a news article describes immigration as an "economic burden" or a
"humanitarian crisis," it selectively emphasizes certain aspects of the issue.
Although \textit{framing} shapes how the public interprets such issues,
audiences do not absorb frames passively but actively reorganize the presented
information. While this relationship between source content and audience
response is well-documented in the social sciences, NLP approaches often ignore
it, detecting frames in articles and responses in isolation. We present the
first computational framework for large-scale analysis of framing across source
content (news articles) and audience responses (reader comments).
Methodologically, we refine frame labels and develop a framework that
reconstructs dominant frames in articles and comments from sentence-level
predictions, and aligns articles with topically relevant comments. Applying our
framework across eleven topics and two news outlets, we find that frame reuse
in comments correlates highly across outlets, while topic-specific patterns
vary. We release a frame classifier that performs well on both articles and
comments, a dataset of article and comment sentences manually labeled for
frames, and a large-scale dataset of articles and comments with predicted frame
labels.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a computational framework to analyze framing in news articles and reader comments together, revealing how audiences actively reorganize framing in public discourse across multiple topics and outlets.
AI.motivation: To address the gap that NLP approaches detect frames in articles and audience responses in isolation, ignoring the interactive relationship where audiences reorganize information.
AI.method: They refined frame labels, developed a framework reconstructing dominant frames from sentence-level predictions, and aligned articles with topically relevant comments across eleven topics and two news outlets.
AI.result: They found high correlation in frame reuse in comments across outlets with variation across topics; they also released a robust frame classifier, a manually labeled dataset, and a large-scale predicted frame dataset.
AI.conclusion: Their framework advances large-scale computational analysis of framing interactions between news content and audience responses, providing resources for deeper study of public opinion dynamics.

title: Knowledge-Aware Self-Correction in Language Models via Structured Memory Graphs
summary:
Large Language Models (LLMs) are powerful yet prone to generating factual
errors, commonly referred to as hallucinations. We present a lightweight,
interpretable framework for knowledge-aware self-correction of LLM outputs
using structured memory graphs based on RDF triples. Without retraining or
fine-tuning, our method post-processes model outputs and corrects factual
inconsistencies via external semantic memory. We demonstrate the approach using
DistilGPT-2 and show promising results on simple factual prompts.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a framework that uses structured memory graphs to enable large language models to self-correct factual errors without retraining.
AI.motivation: Large Language Models are powerful but often generate factual errors (hallucinations), which reduces their reliability.
AI.method: The approach involves post-processing the output of LLMs using external semantic memory represented by RDF triples to detect and correct factual inconsistencies, demonstrated on DistilGPT-2.
AI.result: The method shows promising results in correcting factual errors on simple factual prompts without needing to retrain or fine-tune the model.
AI.conclusion: Using structured memory graphs for knowledge-aware self-correction is an effective way to reduce hallucinations in LLM outputs without additional training.

title: Put Teacher in Student's Shoes: Cross-Distillation for Ultra-compact Model Compression Framework
summary:
In the era of mobile computing, deploying efficient Natural Language
Processing (NLP) models in resource-restricted edge settings presents
significant challenges, particularly in environments requiring strict privacy
compliance, real-time responsiveness, and diverse multi-tasking capabilities.
These challenges create a fundamental need for ultra-compact models that
maintain strong performance across various NLP tasks while adhering to
stringent memory constraints. To this end, we introduce Edge ultra-lIte BERT
framework (EI-BERT) with a novel cross-distillation method. EI-BERT efficiently
compresses models through a comprehensive pipeline including hard token
pruning, cross-distillation and parameter quantization. Specifically, the
cross-distillation method uniquely positions the teacher model to understand
the student model's perspective, ensuring efficient knowledge transfer through
parameter integration and the mutual interplay between models. Through
extensive experiments, we achieve a remarkably compact BERT-based model of only
1.91 MB - the smallest to date for Natural Language Understanding (NLU) tasks.
This ultra-compact model has been successfully deployed across multiple
scenarios within the Alipay ecosystem, demonstrating significant improvements
in real-world applications. For example, it has been integrated into Alipay's
live Edge Recommendation system since January 2024, currently serving the app's
recommendation traffic across \textbf{8.4 million daily active devices}.

AI.tldr: EI-BERT is an ultra-compact BERT model designed for resource-constrained edge devices, achieving strong NLP performance within a 1.91 MB model size.
AI.motivation: Deploying efficient NLP models on edge devices is challenging due to strict privacy, real-time, multitasking, and memory constraints, necessitating highly compact but effective models.
AI.method: EI-BERT uses a pipeline of hard token pruning, a novel cross-distillation technique where the teacher model considers the student's perspective for better knowledge transfer, and parameter quantization.
AI.result: The method produces a 1.91 MB BERT-based NLU model, the smallest reported, successfully deployed in Alipay's ecosystem, notably in a live Edge Recommendation system serving 8.4 million daily active users.
AI.conclusion: EI-BERT demonstrates that ultra-compact, efficient, and high-performing NLP models can be deployed effectively on edge devices, meeting real-world application needs in large-scale environments.

title: R1-RE: Cross-Domain Relationship Extraction with RLVR
summary:
Relationship extraction (RE) is a core task in natural language processing.
Traditional approaches typically frame RE as a supervised learning problem,
directly mapping context to labels-an approach that often suffers from poor
out-of-domain (OOD) generalization. Inspired by the workflow of human
annotators, we reframe RE as a reasoning task guided by annotation guidelines
and introduce R1-RE, the first reinforcement learning with verifiable reward
(RLVR) framework for RE tasks. Our method elicits the reasoning abilities of
small language models for annotation tasks, resulting in significantly improved
OOD robustness. We evaluate our approach on the public Sem-2010 dataset and a
private MDKG dataset. The R1-RE-7B model attains an average OOD accuracy of
approximately 70%, on par with leading proprietary models such as GPT-4o.
Additionally, our comprehensive analysis provides novel insights into the
training dynamics and emergent reasoning behaviors of the RLVR paradigm for RE.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces R1-RE, a reinforcement learning framework using verifiable rewards to improve out-of-domain generalization in relationship extraction tasks, achieving performance comparable to top proprietary models.
AI.motivation: Traditional supervised learning approaches for relationship extraction (RE) suffer from poor out-of-domain generalization. The authors aim to improve generalization by reframing RE as a reasoning task guided by annotation guidelines, inspired by how human annotators work.
AI.method: They propose R1-RE, the first reinforcement learning with verifiable reward (RLVR) framework for RE, leveraging small language models to elicit reasoning abilities for annotation tasks.
AI.result: The R1-RE-7B model achieves around 70% average out-of-domain accuracy on the Sem-2010 and a private MDKG dataset, comparable to leading proprietary models like GPT-4o.
AI.conclusion: RLVR with grounded rewards effectively improves the reasoning and OOD robustness of small language models in RE tasks, offering insights into their training dynamics and emergent reasoning behaviors.

title: XiYan-SQL: A Novel Multi-Generator Framework For Text-to-SQL
summary:
To leverage the advantages of LLM in addressing challenges in the Text-to-SQL
task, we present XiYan-SQL, an innovative framework effectively generating and
utilizing multiple SQL candidates. It consists of three components: 1) a Schema
Filter module filtering and obtaining multiple relevant schemas; 2) a
multi-generator ensemble approach generating multiple highquality and diverse
SQL queries; 3) a selection model with a candidate reorganization strategy
implemented to obtain the optimal SQL query. Specifically, for the
multi-generator ensemble, we employ a multi-task fine-tuning strategy to
enhance the capabilities of SQL generation models for the intrinsic alignment
between SQL and text, and construct multiple generation models with distinct
generation styles by fine-tuning across different SQL formats. The experimental
results and comprehensive analysis demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness
of our framework. Overall, XiYan-SQL achieves a new SOTA performance of 75.63%
on the notable BIRD benchmark, surpassing all previous methods. It also attains
SOTA performance on the Spider test set with an accuracy of 89.65%.

AI.tldr: XiYan-SQL is a framework that generates multiple SQL queries via multi-generator ensemble and selects the best one to improve Text-to-SQL tasks, achieving new state-of-the-art results on benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To leverage Large Language Models (LLMs) for better addressing challenges in Text-to-SQL tasks by generating diverse and high-quality SQL queries.
AI.method: The framework has three parts: a Schema Filter to select relevant schemas, a multi-generator ensemble using a multi-task fine-tuning strategy across different SQL formats to generate diverse SQL queries, and a selection model with candidate reorganization to pick the optimal SQL query.
AI.result: XiYan-SQL outperforms previous methods, achieving 75.63% accuracy on the BIRD benchmark and 89.65% on the Spider test set, setting new state-of-the-art results.
AI.conclusion: The multi-generator ensemble and candidate selection strategy effectively improve SQL generation quality and robustness for Text-to-SQL tasks.

title: Why We Feel What We Feel: Joint Detection of Emotions and Their Opinion Triggers in E-commerce
comment:
23 pages, 11 figures, 7 tables. Dataset and code will be made
  publicly available
summary:
Customer reviews on e-commerce platforms capture critical affective signals
that drive purchasing decisions. However, no existing research has explored the
joint task of emotion detection and explanatory span identification in
e-commerce reviews - a crucial gap in understanding what triggers customer
emotional responses. To bridge this gap, we propose a novel joint task unifying
Emotion detection and Opinion Trigger extraction (EOT), which explicitly models
the relationship between causal text spans (opinion triggers) and affective
dimensions (emotion categories) grounded in Plutchik's theory of 8 primary
emotions. In the absence of labeled data, we introduce EOT-X, a human-annotated
collection of 2,400 reviews with fine-grained emotions and opinion triggers. We
evaluate 23 Large Language Models (LLMs) and present EOT-DETECT, a structured
prompting framework with systematic reasoning and self-reflection. Our
framework surpasses zero-shot and chain-of-thought techniques, across
e-commerce domains.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the joint task of Emotion detection and Opinion Trigger extraction (EOT) in e-commerce reviews, proposes a dataset EOT-X, and a structured prompting framework EOT-DETECT that outperforms existing methods.
AI.motivation: To address the unexplored area of jointly detecting emotions and their causal triggers in customer reviews, which is critical for understanding customer emotional responses affecting purchasing decisions.
AI.method: The authors unify emotion detection and opinion trigger extraction based on Plutchik's 8 primary emotions, create a human-annotated dataset EOT-X of 2,400 reviews, and propose EOT-DETECT, a structured prompting framework leveraging systematic reasoning and self-reflection to evaluate 23 large language models.
AI.result: EOT-DETECT outperforms zero-shot and chain-of-thought prompting techniques across different e-commerce domains in identifying emotions and their triggers.
AI.conclusion: Their joint task and structured prompting framework effectively model the relationship between emotions and opinion triggers, improving emotion detection and explanation in e-commerce reviews.

title: LOOM-Scope: a comprehensive and efficient LOng-cOntext Model evaluation framework
summary:
Long-context processing has become a fundamental capability for large
language models~(LLMs). To assess model's long-context performance, numerous
long-context evaluation benchmarks have been proposed. However, variations in
evaluation settings across these benchmarks lead to inconsistent results,
making it difficult to draw reliable comparisons. Besides, the high
computational cost of long-context evaluation poses a significant barrier for
the community to conduct comprehensive assessments of long-context models. In
this paper, we propose LOOM-Scope, a comprehensive and efficient framework for
long-context evaluation. LOOM-Scope standardizes evaluation settings across
diverse benchmarks, supports deployment of efficient long-context inference
acceleration methods, and introduces a holistic yet lightweight benchmark suite
to evaluate models comprehensively. Homepage: https://loomscope.github.io

AI.tldr: LOOM-Scope is a new framework designed to standardize, accelerate, and comprehensively evaluate long-context capabilities of large language models.
AI.motivation: Current long-context evaluation benchmarks have inconsistent settings and high computational costs, making reliable comparison and comprehensive assessment challenging.
AI.method: LOOM-Scope standardizes evaluation protocols, integrates efficient inference acceleration methods, and provides a lightweight, holistic benchmark suite for long-context model evaluation.
AI.result: The framework harmonizes diverse benchmarks and reduces computational overhead, enabling more reliable and efficient long-context performance assessments.
AI.conclusion: LOOM-Scope facilitates consistent, efficient, and comprehensive evaluation of long-context capabilities in large language models, addressing previous limitations in the field.

title: "This Suits You the Best": Query Focused Comparative Explainable Summarization
summary:
Product recommendations inherently involve comparisons, yet traditional
opinion summarization often fails to provide holistic comparative insights. We
propose the novel task of generating Query-Focused Comparative Explainable
Summaries (QF-CES) using Multi-Source Opinion Summarization (M-OS). To address
the lack of query-focused recommendation datasets, we introduce MS-Q2P,
comprising 7,500 queries mapped to 22,500 recommended products with metadata.
We leverage Large Language Models (LLMs) to generate tabular comparative
summaries with query-specific explanations. Our approach is personalized,
privacy-preserving, recommendation engine-agnostic, and category-agnostic. M-OS
as an intermediate step reduces inference latency approximately by 40% compared
to the direct input approach (DIA), which processes raw data directly. We
evaluate open-source and proprietary LLMs for generating and assessing QF-CES.
Extensive evaluations using QF-CES-PROMPT across 5 dimensions (clarity,
faithfulness, informativeness, format adherence, and query relevance) showed an
average Spearman correlation of 0.74 with human judgments, indicating its
potential for QF-CES evaluation.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a novel task of generating Query-Focused Comparative Explainable Summaries for product recommendations using Multi-Source Opinion Summarization. It presents a new dataset MS-Q2P and demonstrates the use of Large Language Models to create personalized, privacy-preserving comparative summaries, achieving efficient inference and strong evaluation correlations with human judgments.
AI.motivation: Traditional opinion summarization lacks holistic comparative insights needed for product recommendations that inherently involve comparisons. Addressing this gap requires query-focused, explainable, and comparative summaries.
AI.method: The authors propose Multi-Source Opinion Summarization as an intermediate step to generate Query-Focused Comparative Explainable Summaries. They introduce the MS-Q2P dataset mapping queries to recommended products with metadata and employ Large Language Models to produce tabular, query-specific explanations in a personalized, privacy-preserving, and recommendation engine-agnostic manner. They also evaluate multiple LLMs and develop the QF-CES-PROMPT for assessment.
AI.result: Their method reduces inference latency by about 40% compared to directly processing raw data. Evaluations across five quality dimensions show an average Spearman correlation of 0.74 with human judgments, demonstrating the effectiveness and reliability of the generated summaries and the evaluation prompt.
AI.conclusion: The proposed M-OS approach and MS-Q2P dataset enable effective generation of query-focused, comparative, and explainable product recommendation summaries. The method is efficient, privacy-aware, and generalizable, with strong alignment to human evaluations, highlighting its potential for practical recommendation explainability.

title: Word stress in self-supervised speech models: A cross-linguistic comparison
summary:
In this paper we study word stress representations learned by self-supervised
speech models (S3M), specifically the Wav2vec 2.0 model. We investigate the S3M
representations of word stress for five different languages: Three languages
with variable or lexical stress (Dutch, English and German) and two languages
with fixed or demarcative stress (Hungarian and Polish). We train diagnostic
stress classifiers on S3M embeddings and show that they can distinguish between
stressed and unstressed syllables in read-aloud short sentences with high
accuracy. We also tested language-specificity effects of S3M word stress. The
results indicate that the word stress representations are language-specific,
with a greater difference between the set of variable versus the set of fixed
stressed languages.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how self-supervised speech models like Wav2vec 2.0 represent word stress across five languages, showing that these models accurately distinguish stressed from unstressed syllables and that their stress representations are language-specific.
AI.motivation: To understand how self-supervised speech models encode word stress across languages with different stress patterns.
AI.method: Training diagnostic classifiers on embeddings from the Wav2vec 2.0 model to classify stressed versus unstressed syllables in five languages with variable and fixed stress types.
AI.result: High classification accuracy in distinguishing stressed from unstressed syllables; stress representations differ by language type, especially between variable and fixed stress languages.
AI.conclusion: Self-supervised speech models learn language-specific word stress representations, with clear distinctions depending on stress typology.

title: A Tale of Two Scripts: Transliteration and Post-Correction for Judeo-Arabic
summary:
Judeo-Arabic refers to Arabic variants historically spoken by Jewish
communities across the Arab world, primarily during the Middle Ages. Unlike
standard Arabic, it is written in Hebrew script by Jewish writers and for
Jewish audiences. Transliterating Judeo-Arabic into Arabic script is
challenging due to ambiguous letter mappings, inconsistent orthographic
conventions, and frequent code-switching into Hebrew and Aramaic. In this
paper, we introduce a two-step approach to automatically transliterate
Judeo-Arabic into Arabic script: simple character-level mapping followed by
post-correction to address grammatical and orthographic errors. We also present
the first benchmark evaluation of LLMs on this task. Finally, we show that
transliteration enables Arabic NLP tools to perform morphosyntactic tagging and
machine translation, which would have not been feasible on the original texts.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a two-step method to transliterate Judeo-Arabic written in Hebrew script into Arabic script, enabling the use of Arabic NLP tools.
AI.motivation: Judeo-Arabic texts are written in Hebrew script but represent Arabic language variants, making it challenging to apply Arabic NLP due to script differences and linguistic complexities.
AI.method: A two-step approach involving character-level mapping from Hebrew to Arabic script followed by post-correction to fix grammatical and orthographic inaccuracies, along with benchmark evaluation of large language models (LLMs) on this task.
AI.result: The approach successfully transliterates Judeo-Arabic enabling morphosyntactic tagging and machine translation on the transliterated texts, which is not possible on the original Hebrew-script texts.
AI.conclusion: Transliterating Judeo-Arabic to Arabic script facilitates the application of Arabic NLP tools, thereby expanding computational linguistic analysis and machine translation capabilities for these historic texts.

title: LLMs as Architects and Critics for Multi-Source Opinion Summarization
summary:
Multi-source Opinion Summarization (M-OS) extends beyond traditional opinion
summarization by incorporating additional sources of product metadata such as
descriptions, key features, specifications, and ratings, alongside reviews.
This integration results in comprehensive summaries that capture both
subjective opinions and objective product attributes essential for informed
decision-making. While Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown significant
success in various Natural Language Processing (NLP) tasks, their potential in
M-OS remains largely unexplored. Additionally, the lack of evaluation datasets
for this task has impeded further advancements. To bridge this gap, we
introduce M-OS-EVAL, a benchmark dataset for evaluating multi-source opinion
summaries across 7 key dimensions: fluency, coherence, relevance, faithfulness,
aspect coverage, sentiment consistency, specificity. Our results demonstrate
that M-OS significantly enhances user engagement, as evidenced by a user study
in which, on average, 87% of participants preferred M-OS over opinion
summaries. Our experiments demonstrate that factually enriched summaries
enhance user engagement. Notably, M-OS-PROMPTS exhibit stronger alignment with
human judgment, achieving an average Spearman correlation of \r{ho} = 0.74,
which surpasses the performance of previous methodologies.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Multi-source Opinion Summarization (M-OS), which integrates multiple product data sources to create comprehensive summaries, and provides a new evaluation dataset M-OS-EVAL. It demonstrates that M-OS improves user engagement and aligns better with human judgment than prior methods.
AI.motivation: Existing opinion summarization focuses only on reviews, missing valuable product metadata which limits the completeness of summaries. Also, lack of evaluation datasets for multi-source summaries hinder research progress.
AI.method: The authors integrate reviews with product metadata (descriptions, features, specifications, ratings) to generate enriched opinion summaries. They create M-OS-EVAL, a benchmark dataset evaluating summaries on seven dimensions. Experiments include user studies and correlation with human judgments using M-OS-PROMPTS.
AI.result: M-OS summaries significantly improve user engagement, with 87% user preference over standard opinion summaries in studies. The M-OS-PROMPTS method shows a strong average Spearman correlation (0.74) with human evaluations, outperforming previous techniques.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating multiple sources of product information enables the generation of factually enriched and comprehensive opinion summaries that better align with human preferences and improve user engagement, validated through new datasets and evaluation metrics.

title: CoSteer: Collaborative Decoding-Time Personalization via Local Delta Steering
summary:
Personalized text generation has become crucial for adapting language models
to diverse and evolving users' personal context across cultural, temporal, and
contextual dimensions. While existing methods often rely on centralized
fine-tuning or static preference alignment, they struggle to achieve real-time
adaptation under resource constraints inherent to personal devices. This
limitation creates a dilemma: large cloud-based models lack access to localized
user-specific information, while small on-device models cannot match the
generation quality of their cloud counterparts. To address this dichotomy, we
present CoSteer, a novel collaborative framework that enables decoding-time
personalization through localized delta steering. Our key insight lies in
leveraging the logits difference between personal context-aware and -agnostic
outputs from local small models as steering signals for cloud-based LLMs.
Specifically, we formulate token-level optimization as an online learning
problem, where local delta vectors dynamically adjust the remote LLM's logits
within the on-device environment. This approach preserves privacy by
transmitting only the final steered tokens rather than raw data or intermediate
vectors, while maintaining cloud-based LLMs' general capabilities without
fine-tuning. Through comprehensive experiments on various personalized
generation tasks, we demonstrate that CoSteer effectively assists LLMs in
generating personalized content by leveraging locally stored user profiles and
histories, ensuring privacy preservation through on-device data processing
while maintaining acceptable computational overhead.

AI.tldr: CoSteer is a collaborative framework for personalized text generation that enables real-time adaptation by steering cloud-based large language models with signals from small local models, balancing privacy, quality, and computational constraints.
AI.motivation: Existing personalized text generation methods either require centralized fine-tuning or static user preferences, failing to adapt in real-time under resource constraints and privacy concerns of personal devices.
AI.method: CoSteer leverages the difference in logits between personalized and non-personalized outputs generated by local small models to steer cloud-based LLMs during decoding. It formulates token-level optimization as an online learning problem, adjusting the cloud model’s logits via local delta vectors without fine-tuning the cloud model.
AI.result: Experiments show CoSteer effectively improves personalized content generation using local user profiles and histories, preserves user privacy by processing data on-device, and maintains reasonable computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: CoSteer achieves a practical balance of personalization quality, privacy, and resource efficiency by enabling collaborative decoding-time personalization without cloud model fine-tuning.

title: Reason to Rote: Rethinking Memorization in Reasoning
summary:
Large language models readily memorize arbitrary training instances, such as
label noise, yet they perform strikingly well on reasoning tasks. In this work,
we investigate how language models memorize label noise, and why such
memorization in many cases does not heavily affect generalizable reasoning
capabilities. Using two controllable synthetic reasoning datasets with noisy
labels, four-digit addition (FDA) and two-hop relational reasoning (THR), we
discover a reliance of memorization on generalizable reasoning mechanisms:
models continue to compute intermediate reasoning outputs even when retrieving
memorized noisy labels, and intervening reasoning adversely affects
memorization. We further show that memorization operates through distributed
encoding, i.e., aggregating various inputs and intermediate results, rather
than building a look-up mechanism from inputs to noisy labels. Moreover, our
FDA case study reveals memorization occurs via outlier heuristics, where
existing neuron activation patterns are slightly shifted to fit noisy labels.
Together, our findings suggest that memorization of label noise in language
models builds on, rather than overrides, the underlying reasoning mechanisms,
shedding lights on the intriguing phenomenon of benign memorization.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how large language models memorize label noise without harming their reasoning abilities, finding that memorization relies on reasoning mechanisms and distributed encoding rather than simple lookup.
AI.motivation: To understand why language models memorize noisy training labels yet still maintain strong generalizable reasoning performance.
AI.method: The authors use two synthetic reasoning datasets with controlled noisy labels (four-digit addition and two-hop relational reasoning) and analyze model behavior focusing on how memorization interacts with reasoning processes and neuron activation patterns.
AI.result: Models persist in computing intermediate reasoning outputs despite memorizing noisy labels, with memorization relying on distributed encoding and slight shifts in neuron activation patterns (outlier heuristics). Intervening reasoning processes disrupt memorization.
AI.conclusion: Memorization of label noise leverages rather than impairs reasoning mechanisms in language models, explaining the phenomenon of benign memorization where noisy label memorization does not severely affect reasoning capabilities.

title: A Survey of Pun Generation: Datasets, Evaluations and Methodologies
summary:
Pun generation seeks to creatively modify linguistic elements in text to
produce humour or evoke double meanings. It also aims to preserve coherence and
contextual appropriateness, making it useful in creative writing and
entertainment across various media and contexts. Although pun generation has
received considerable attention in computational linguistics, there is
currently no dedicated survey that systematically reviews this specific area.
To bridge this gap, this paper provides a comprehensive review of pun
generation datasets and methods across different stages, including conventional
approaches, deep learning techniques, and pre-trained language models.
Additionally, we summarise both automated and human evaluation metrics used to
assess the quality of pun generation. Finally, we discuss the research
challenges and propose promising directions for future work.

AI.tldr: This paper provides the first comprehensive survey on pun generation, reviewing datasets, methods, evaluations, and challenges to guide future research.
AI.motivation: To fill the gap of lacking a systematic survey specifically dedicated to pun generation in computational linguistics.
AI.method: Review of existing pun generation datasets, conventional methods, deep learning approaches, pre-trained language models, and evaluation metrics (automated and human).
AI.result: A comprehensive summary of datasets, methods, and evaluation strategies for pun generation is provided.
AI.conclusion: The paper highlights research challenges and suggests promising avenues for future work in pun generation research.

title: Spec-TOD: A Specialized Instruction-Tuned LLM Framework for Efficient Task-Oriented Dialogue Systems
summary:
Task-oriented dialogue (TOD) systems facilitate goal-driven interactions
between users and machines. While recent advances in deep learning have
improved the performance, TOD systems often struggle in low-resource scenarios
with limited labeled data. To address this challenge, we propose Spec-TOD, a
novel framework designed to train an end-to-end TOD system with limited data.
Spec-TOD introduces two main innovations: (i) a novel specialized end-to-end
TOD framework that incorporates explicit task instructions for
instruction-tuned large language models (LLMs), and (ii) an efficient training
strategy that leverages lightweight, specialized LLMs to achieve strong
performance with minimal supervision. Experiments on the MultiWOZ dataset, a
widely used TOD benchmark, demonstrate that Spec-TOD achieves competitive
results while significantly reducing the need for labeled data. These findings
highlight the potential of the proposed framework in advancing efficient and
effective TOD systems in low-resource settings.

AI.tldr: Spec-TOD is a new framework for training task-oriented dialogue systems using limited labeled data by leveraging instruction-tuned large language models and efficient training strategies, achieving strong performance on benchmarks with minimal supervision.
AI.motivation: Task-oriented dialogue systems perform poorly in low-resource scenarios due to limited labeled data, necessitating a method to improve TOD systems under such constraints.
AI.method: Spec-TOD integrates explicit task instructions into instruction-tuned large language models and employs an efficient training strategy using lightweight, specialized LLMs to train end-to-end TOD systems with minimal data.
AI.result: On the MultiWOZ dataset, Spec-TOD achieves competitive results compared to existing methods while significantly reducing reliance on labeled data.
AI.conclusion: Spec-TOD effectively advances task-oriented dialogue systems by enabling efficient and strong performance in low-resource settings, highlighting the viability of specialized LLMs with instruction-based training.

title: Dialogue-Based Multi-Dimensional Relationship Extraction from Novels
comment:
The paper has been accepted by NLPCC2025. 12 pages, 5 figures, 5
  tables
summary:
Relation extraction is a crucial task in natural language processing, with
broad applications in knowledge graph construction and literary analysis.
However, the complex context and implicit expressions in novel texts pose
significant challenges for automatic character relationship extraction. This
study focuses on relation extraction in the novel domain and proposes a method
based on Large Language Models (LLMs). By incorporating relationship dimension
separation, dialogue data construction, and contextual learning strategies, the
proposed method enhances extraction performance. Leveraging dialogue structure
information, it improves the model's ability to understand implicit
relationships and demonstrates strong adaptability in complex contexts.
Additionally, we construct a high-quality Chinese novel relation extraction
dataset to address the lack of labeled resources and support future research.
Experimental results show that our method outperforms traditional baselines
across multiple evaluation metrics and successfully facilitates the automated
construction of character relationship networks in novels.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a method using large language models to improve relation extraction in novel texts, focusing on character relationships, and introduces a Chinese novel dataset.
AI.motivation: Extracting character relationships in novels is challenging due to complex contexts and implicit expressions, with a lack of labeled datasets hindering progress.
AI.method: The approach uses LLMs augmented with relationship dimension separation, dialogue data construction, and contextual learning strategies, leveraging dialogue structures to better understand implicit relations.
AI.result: The method surpasses traditional baselines on multiple metrics and enables automated construction of character relationship networks in novels.
AI.conclusion: The proposed LLM-based method effectively improves relation extraction in novel domains and the new dataset supports future research in this area.

title: $\textit{Grahak-Nyay:}$ Consumer Grievance Redressal through Large Language Models
summary:
Access to consumer grievance redressal in India is often hindered by
procedural complexity, legal jargon, and jurisdictional challenges. To address
this, we present $\textbf{Grahak-Nyay}$ (Justice-to-Consumers), a chatbot that
streamlines the process using open-source Large Language Models (LLMs) and
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). Grahak-Nyay simplifies legal complexities
through a concise and up-to-date knowledge base. We introduce three novel
datasets: $\textit{GeneralQA}$ (general consumer law), $\textit{SectoralQA}$
(sector-specific knowledge) and $\textit{SyntheticQA}$ (for RAG evaluation),
along with $\textit{NyayChat}$, a dataset of 300 annotated chatbot
conversations. We also introduce $\textit{Judgments}$ data sourced from Indian
Consumer Courts to aid the chatbot in decision making and to enhance user
trust. We also propose $\textbf{HAB}$ metrics ($\textbf{Helpfulness, Accuracy,
Brevity}$) to evaluate chatbot performance. Legal domain experts validated
Grahak-Nyay's effectiveness. Code and datasets will be released.

AI.tldr: Grahak-Nyay is a chatbot developed to simplify consumer grievance redressal in India using open-source LLMs and RAG techniques, supported by novel datasets and new evaluation metrics.
AI.motivation: Consumer grievance redressal in India is complicated by procedural and legal complexities, making access difficult for consumers.
